gui.tutorials.gettingStarted.addMoveBloc: Drag the move 10 steps block from the Motion category into the workspace.
gui.tutorials.gettingStarted.addSayBlock: Now drag the say hello for 2 seconds block from the Looks category and snap it below the move block.
gui.tutorials.gettingStarted.addGreenFla: Finally, drag the when green flag clicked block from Events and place it on top of your blocks. Then click the green flag to run your project!

gui.tutorials.spin.stepPointInDirection: Use the point in direction block to set which way your sprite faces before spinning.

gui.tutorials.recordASound.stepPlayYourS: Now use the play sound block to play your recorded sound in your project!

gui.tutorials.blinkLed.boardMenu: Click the Board menu in the top menu bar to see board options.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.boardManager: Click Board Manager from the dropdown. This opens the board selection dialog.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.searchUno: Type UNO in the search box to find the Arduino Uno board.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.selectUno: Click on the Arduino Uno card to select it.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.addBoard: Click the Add Board button to add the Arduino blocks to your toolbox.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.dismissConnection: A device connection dialog will appear. Close it by clicking the X button — we will use the simulator instead of a real board.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.uploadMode: Click the Upload toggle at the top to switch to Upload mode. This shows the Code and Simulator tabs on the right side.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.findSetup: Click the Arduino category in the toolbox and scroll down to the Code Generation section. You will find the setup and loop block at the bottom.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.dragSetup: Drag the setup/loop block into the workspace. This is the main block for Arduino programs. Setup runs once when the program starts, and loop runs over and over.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.addPinMode: Drag a pinMode block into the setup section. Set pin to 13 and mode to OUTPUT. This tells the Arduino that pin 13 will be used to send signals out.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.dwHigh: Drag a digitalWrite block into the loop section. Set pin to 13 and value to HIGH. This turns the LED on.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.delay1: Add a delay block after digitalWrite. Set it to 1000 milliseconds which is 1 second. The LED will stay on for this long.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.dwLow: Add another digitalWrite block. Keep pin 13 but change the value to LOW. This turns the LED off.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.delay2: Add one more delay block with 1000 milliseconds. Your blink program is now complete! The loop will repeat: LED on, wait, LED off, wait.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.simulator: Click the Simulator tab on the right side. The simulator lets you test your code without a real Arduino board.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.openPicker: Click the plus button at the bottom right to open the component picker.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.selectLed: Select LED from the component list to add it to the simulator.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.pinMapping: In the pin mapping dialog, select pin 13 from the dropdown and click Save. This connects the virtual LED to the same pin your code uses.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.ledAdded: You can now see the LED component on the simulator board, connected to pin 13.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.clickStart: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program will be compiled and sent to the virtual Arduino board.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.compiling: Wait a few seconds while the program compiles. You will see a loading indicator. Once it finishes, the simulation will start automatically.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.running: The LED is now blinking on and off every second! Congratulations, you have created your first Arduino program! Try changing the delay values to make it blink faster or slower.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.intro: Every doorbell, elevator button, and keyboard key on your laptop does one tiny thing: it senses a press and switches something else on. Today you'll teach an LED that exact trick. Press the button — the light comes on. Let go — the light goes off. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Just like Scratch has 'when green flag clicked' and 'forever', Arduino has setup and loop. Setup runs once when the program starts. Loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.pinModeLed: An Arduino has rows of labelled holes around the edge of the board — those are the pins. Each pin is a wire connection point. Some pins send voltage out (to lights, motors). Some pins listen for voltage coming in (from buttons, sensors). The Arduino needs to know which is which. Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 13 and mode to output — pin 13 will be sending voltage out to the LED.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.ifElse: We want the LED to react differently when the button is pressed vs not pressed. That's exactly what an if-else block is for — same block you've seen in Scratch. Open the Control category and drag an if-else block into the loop section. Whatever question we put in its diamond slot will run the if branch when the answer is yes, and the else branch when the answer is no.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.writeHigh: Drag a digitalWrite block into the if branch — the top half of the if-else. Set pin to 13 and value to high. When the if-question's answer is yes (button pressed), this line runs: 5 volts on pin 13, LED lights up.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.writeLow: Drag another digitalWrite into the else branch — the bottom half. Pin 13, value low. When the question's answer is no (button not pressed), this line runs instead: 0 volts on pin 13, LED dark. The blocks are done — now let's wire up the simulator.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.addLed: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LED. In the pin dialog, choose pin 13 and click Save. The virtual LED appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same pin your code is writing high and low to.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.addButton: Click the plus button again, choose Push Button, set pin to 2, click Save. The button is now wired between pin 2 and… well, that's exactly what the next step is about.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.running: Click and hold the simulated button. The LED lights up. Let go — it goes dark. You just built a real-world reactive program. The same trick is inside every doorbell, every light switch, every keyboard key, every elevator button you've ever pressed.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addExtension: Click the purple Add Extension button at the bottom left of the screen to open the Extension Library.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.progFilter: Click the Programming tab at the top to filter the extensions. You will see the HTML 5 extension.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.selectHtml5: Click the HTML 5 card to add the HTML extension to your project. This gives you all the blocks needed to build web pages.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.uploadMode: Click the Upload toggle at the top right to switch to Upload mode. This shows the HTML and View tabs on the right side.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.category: Click the HTML5 category in the blocks panel on the left. You will see all the blocks needed to build a web page.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.dragHtml: Find the html block at the top of the HTML5 flyout and drag it into the workspace. Every web page starts with this block.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addHead: Drag the head block and snap it below the html block. The head section holds information about your page like its title.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addTitle: Drag the title block inside the head block. Click the text field and type Hello Web. This text appears in the browser tab.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addBody: Drag the body block and snap it below the head block. Everything visible on your web page goes inside the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addH1: Drag the heading block inside the body block. Keep the level at 1 for a large heading. Click the text field and type Hello, Web!
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addParagraph: Drag a paragraph block and snap it below the heading inside the body. Click the text field and type This is my first web page!
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.htmlTab: Click the HTML tab on the right panel. You can see the real HTML code that your blocks have generated!
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.viewTab: Click the View tab to see your web page rendered in the browser. Congratulations — you have built your first HTML page!
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.typeSay: Click inside the Python editor and type exactly this: say("Hello World!") — with the round brackets and the quotes. The word say is a CodeSkool helper — it only works here, not in regular Python on your laptop. When you run it, it will make your penguin speak with a speech bubble on the stage. You can change the words inside the quotes to anything you like — your name, a joke, anything — just keep the word say and the brackets.
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.runSay: Now click the Run button in the toolbar below the code editor. Watch the stage on the right — a speech bubble pops up right next to the penguin with your message! That's what say does: it puts your words on the stage as a visual speech bubble, attached to the sprite. Cool, right? But say is only one way Python can show us text. There's another way — and it looks completely different.
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.typePrint: Press Enter to go to line 2, then type print("Hello World!"). Now look closely — print is different from say. print is standard Python — it's built into Python itself and works in every Python program ever written, not just CodeSkool. But print doesn't put a speech bubble on the stage. It sends your message somewhere else. Want to know where?
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.placeholder: You'll learn how to use PWM to fade an LED smoothly brighter and dimmer, like a heartbeat.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.placeholder: You'll build a working traffic light with red, yellow and green LEDs — cycling through the lights just like a real one.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.placeholder: You'll blink an LED with the SOS Morse signal — three short flashes, three long flashes, three short flashes — the universal call for help.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.placeholder: You'll build a night lamp that turns an LED on automatically when the room gets dark and off when it's bright.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.placeholder: You'll build a security alarm that sounds a buzzer when a motion sensor spots someone sneaking past.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.placeholde: You'll control the speed of a fan by turning a knob — smoothly speeding it up or slowing it down, just like a real dimmer.
gui.tutorials.automaticPlantIrrigation.p: You'll build an automatic plant waterer that reads a soil moisture sensor and switches on a pump whenever the soil gets too dry.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.placehold: You'll build a parking sensor with an ultrasonic distance module — the buzzer beeps faster as the car gets closer to the wall, just like a real car.
gui.tutorials.automaticHandSanitizer.pla: You'll build a touchless sanitizer dispenser — the ultrasonic sensor spots a hand and a servo squirts the sanitizer, all without anyone touching anything.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.placeholder: You'll build a smart door lock that opens with a secret keypad code — type the right code and a servo unlocks the door, type a wrong one and it stays shut.
gui.tutorials.smartPeopleCounter.placeho: You'll build a people counter that ticks up by one every time someone walks past an infrared sensor — just like the counters at shop doors.
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.workspace: Before we code, let's look around. The white area on the left is the Python editor — that's where you type code. The penguin on the right lives on the stage — that's where anything visual happens when you run your code. And the dark black strip at the bottom is the terminal — a place where your program can print text messages. Three places, three jobs. Let's use them!
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.terminal: See that dark black strip at the bottom of the screen? That's the terminal — some people call it the console. It's a place where Python programs write text for you to read. The stage shows visual things like sprites and speech bubbles. The terminal shows words — plain text, one line after another. Right now the terminal is empty because we haven't run the new print line yet. Let's do it!
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.runBoth: Click Run one more time. Two things happen at the same time: the penguin says Hello World! on the stage (that's the say line), AND your message appears in the terminal at the bottom (that's the print line). Same words — two totally different places. So remember: use say when you want the penguin to show a speech bubble; use print when you want to write a message in the terminal.
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.recap: Congrats — you just wrote your first Python program! Two lines of real Python. Remember the difference: say is a CodeSkool helper — it makes your sprite speak on the stage. print is standard Python — it writes text to the terminal, and it works the same way in every Python program anywhere. Try changing the words inside the quotes on either line and click Run again to see your new message.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.intro: A variable is like a labelled box. You pick a name (the label), and Python keeps a value inside. You can open the box by name whenever you want what's in it. You've already made these in Scratch — clicking "Make a Variable" and typing a name creates a new labelled box.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.typeAss:
On line 1, type:

message = "Hello Python!"

This is Scratch's orange "set my variable to Hello Python!" block, written as text. The equals sign puts the value on the right INSIDE the box labelled message. From now on, the box called message holds "Hello Python!".

Box labels can use letters, numbers, and underscores. No spaces.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.typePri:
Press Enter for line 2, then type:

print(message)

In Python, print is like Scratch's say block — but instead of showing a speech bubble on the stage, it writes the message into the terminal at the bottom. And just like dragging the orange rounded variable block into a Scratch say block, in Python you put a variable into print by typing its name. So print(message) tells Python: open the box called message, then write what's inside into the terminal.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.noQuote: Notice there are no quotes around the word message in the print line. That tiny detail changes everything. Without quotes, Python sees message as the name of a box and prints what's inside, which is Hello Python. If we put quotes around it instead, Python would ignore the box completely and just print the word message itself, letter by letter. The rule: no quotes means look in the box, quotes means it's just plain text.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.runBoth:
Click Run. The terminal shows: Hello Python!

Python ran line 1 (put "Hello Python!" in the box called message) and then line 2 (opened the box and printed what was inside).
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.tryYour: Change the text in quotes on line 1 to anything you like — your name, a joke, your favourite food. Click Run again. The print line never changes — only what's inside the box does.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.done: That's your first Python variable. You picked a name, put a value into the box with the equals sign, and opened the box with print. The same idea works for your name, a score, a list of friends — anything you want Python to remember and reuse.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.typeInput: On line 1, type: name = input("What is your name?"). The function input is standard Python — when the program runs, a small text box pops up on the stage with the question above it, and the program WAITS until the kid types an answer and presses Enter. Whatever they type is given back, and the equals sign stores it under the name name.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.typePrint: Press Enter for line 2, then type print("Hello, " + name). The plus sign here does something you haven't seen before — it GLUES two pieces of text together into one. This is called concatenation. "Hello, " is a fixed string (with a space after the comma!) and name holds whatever the kid typed. Put together they make one greeting. Notice: no quotes around name — it's a variable, like last tutorial.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.runAndAns: Click Run. The terminal prints the question and waits. Click inside the terminal, type your name, and press Enter. Python glues the two strings together and prints the greeting with your name at the end.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.recap: Change the question inside the quotes on line 1 — ask for a favourite colour, a pet's name, a superpower. Change the greeting on line 2 to match, like print("Nice choice, " + colour + "!"). Remember to store the answer in a variable with a matching name. Click Run again. Your program now knows how to talk to anyone.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.typeInput: On line 1, type: age = int(input("How old are you?")). Read the line from the inside out. The input("How old are you?") bit prints the question and waits — same as last tutorial. Then int(…) wraps around the answer. int is standard Python — it takes text and converts it into a whole number. Without int, age would hold the string "12" and we could not add to it.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.typePrint: Press Enter for line 2, then type print("You will be " + str(age + 1) + " next year"). Let's walk through what's inside the brackets. age + 1 is normal maths — if age is 12, this becomes 13. str is another standard Python helper — it turns a number back into text, because the plus sign for gluing words only works on text. We need the final sentence to be all text so print can show it as one line.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.runAndAnswer: Click Run. A text box pops up on the stage asking how old you are. Type your age as a whole number, press Enter. Python reads the text, int turns it into a number, age + 1 adds one, str turns the answer back into text, and print glues the whole sentence together and shows it in the terminal. Four little tools working as a team on one line.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.recap: Run it again with different ages. Try 9, try 99, even try 0. Each time the maths on line 2 redoes itself with the new value. What do you think happens if you type "ten" (a word instead of digits) when it asks? Try it — int can't turn that into a number, so the program will stop with a type error. That's not a mistake — it's how Python tells you it needs digits, not words.
gui.tutorials.pythonTypeConversionFixThe:
Type these two lines:

a = input("Enter number: ")
b = input("Enter number: ")

Notice there is NO int around input this time. So a and b will hold strings like "12" and "34" even if the kid types digits. We're doing this on purpose — to see what goes wrong when text pretends to be a number.

Leave a blank line, then type:

print(a + b)            # wrong

The # starts a comment — Python ignores anything after # on a line, but we humans can read it. Think for a second: a is "12" and b is "34". What will "12" + "34" do?

Now add the correct line underneath:

print(int(a) + int(b))  # correct

int is standard Python — it converts a string of digits into a real number. int(a) is the number 12, int(b) is the number 34, and 12 + 34 is real addition. Same plus sign, totally different behaviour because now its sides are numbers.

Click Run. Type 12 for the first number, type 34 for the second. The terminal prints 1234 from the wrong line and 46 from the right line. Same inputs, same plus — but the wrapping with int changed everything. That's what "type conversion" means: turning a value into the kind of thing you actually need.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.typeInpu: Type line 1: age = int(input("How old are you?")). You've done this pattern before — ask a question, wrap the answer in int because we'll compare the number. The kid's age will flow into the decision you're about to build.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.typeIf:
Leave a blank line, then type:

if age >= 10:
    print("You can join!")

if is a standard Python keyword. The condition age >= 10 means "age is 10 or more". The colon at the end of the line is the "then" part — what follows must be INDENTED (four spaces at the start of the next line). Only the indented lines run when the condition is true.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.typeElse:
Type these two lines next:

else:
    print("You are too young")

else is Python's way of saying "otherwise". Its indented line only runs when the if's condition was false. Together, if and else cover every possibility — the kid is old enough, OR they are not. There is no in-between here.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.run: Click Run. Type an age of 12 — Python prints "You can join!" because 12 is greater than or equal to 10. Run it again and type 5 — Python prints "You are too young" because 5 is less than 10. One program, two different paths, depending on the answer.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.typeVar:
Type these three lines:

age = 15
has_pass = True
vip = False

age holds a number like before. has_pass and vip hold True and False — Python's two special yes/no values. They're called booleans after the mathematician George Boole, who first wrote down the rules of true/false logic. You'll use these three variables in every if below.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.typeAnd:
Leave a blank line, then type:

if age > 10 and has_pass:
    print("Allowed (AND)")

Read it in English: "if age is more than 10 AND has_pass is True". Both halves must be true for the whole thing to be true. 15 > 10 is true, and has_pass is True — so Python prints the line. If has_pass were False, Python would skip even though the age check passed.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.typeOr:
Type next:

if age > 18 or has_pass:
    print("Allowed (OR)")

In English: "if the kid is older than 18 OR has a pass". With or, only ONE side needs to be true. age > 18 is false (15 is not over 18), but has_pass is True — so the whole thing is true and the line prints. or is lenient; and is strict.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.typeNot:
Type next:

if not has_pass:
    print("No pass")

not flips the value. If has_pass is True, not has_pass is False. If has_pass is False, not has_pass is True. Right now has_pass is True, so not has_pass is False and nothing prints. Change has_pass to False on line 2 and run again — the line will print.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.setup:
Type these three setup lines:

print("🎯 Guess the number!")

number = 5

guess = int(input("Enter your guess: "))

number is the secret answer — hardcoded to 5 for now. guess holds whatever the kid types, wrapped in int so it's a real number we can compare.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.run: Click Run. Type 3 — Python says "📉 Too low". Run again, type 9 — "📈 Too high". Run once more, type 5 — "🎉 Correct!". Same program, three different endings — each one picked by elif.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.pinModeBtnInput: Drag a second pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 2 and change mode to input. input is the opposite of output — instead of sending voltage out, pin 2 will listen for voltage coming in. That's how the Arduino will know when you've pressed the button.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.pinModePullup: There's a problem with plain input: when a pin is connected to nothing, the reading flips between high and low randomly. The Arduino can't tell what's really happening on that pin. Click the mode dropdown and change it to input pullup. This tells the Arduino to quietly hold pin 2 at high (5 volts) all on its own, until something specific changes that. Now the readings are stable.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.dropEquals: Open the Operators category. Drag the equals block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if-else — that's where the yes-or-no question goes. The equals block compares two things; we'll fill in what to compare in the next two steps.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.dropRead: Back to Arduino. Drag a digitalRead block. Drop it into the left slot of the equals block. Set its pin to 2. digitalRead is how a program senses the world — it checks what voltage is sitting on a pin right now and reports back high (5 volts) or low (0 volts). It doesn't change anything; it just looks.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.dropLow: Still in the Arduino category, drag a low block. Drop it into the right slot of the equals block. Your question now reads: 'is pin 2 low right now?' Or in plain words: 'is pin 2 at 0 volts right now?'
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.whyLow: Strange, right? Pressing should mean on, not off. Here's why. input pullup holds pin 2 at high (5 volts) all the time. The button is wired so pressing connects pin 2 to a special wire that's at 0 volts. That pulls pin 2's reading down from 5 volts to 0 volts — which is low. So pressed = low. We'll meet that 0-volt wire when we wire up the button.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.meetWrite: We've used digitalRead to listen. Now meet its twin: digitalWrite — the block that lets your program control things. digitalWrite takes two inputs: a pin number and a value (high or low). When you set a pin high, the Arduino puts 5 volts on it. When you set it low, 0 volts. That's it. Connect an LED, a buzzer, a motor driver, or even another Arduino — digitalWrite is how your program turns them on and off. We'll drop two of these next: one for when the button is pressed, one for when it isn't.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.meetGround: A battery has two terminals — positive (+) and negative (−). On an Arduino, the negative terminal is called ground, often labelled GND on the board. Ground is just a name for 0 volts. Now we can tell the full story of our button. When the button is not pressed, input pullup is gently holding pin 2 at 5 volts — so digitalRead reports high. When you press the button, it connects pin 2 directly to ground. That direct connection beats the gentle pullup, so pin 2 drops to 0 volts — and digitalRead reports low. that'S why pressed = low and not-pressed = high. The mystery from the question we built earlier is solved.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.experiments: Try this: swap high and low in the two digitalWrite blocks. Now pressing turns the LED off, and letting go turns it on — a backwards lamp. Or add a second LED on pin 12, give it its own pinMode and digitalWrites, and make it light up only when the button is released. Two LEDs, opposite reactions, same button.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.recap: Five minutes ago you didn't know any of this. Now you do: pinMode tells the Arduino which pins send voltage and which listen for it. digitalWrite tells a pin to be high (5 volts) or low (0 volts) — that's how programs control the world. digitalRead asks a pin what voltage is on it right now — that's how programs sense the world. if-else lets your program decide between two paths — same as Scratch. ground, often labelled GND, is the name for the 0-volt wire — the reference point for everything else. Programs that read the world, decide, and react are everywhere — alarms, traffic lights, smart locks, your phone's buttons. You can write them now.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.intro: Last tutorial you stored a value yourself: message = "Hello Python!". But the kid running your program can't change it without editing the code. Today we'll let the program ask a question and use whatever the kid types. In Scratch this is the "ask and wait" block. In Python it's one short line — but it unlocks every game, quiz, and chat program you'll ever write.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.textFact: One important thing to remember about input: it always gives back TEXT. Even if the kid types 12, Python sees the string "12", not the number 12. We're not doing maths today so this doesn't matter — but keep it in mind. When you ask for an age or a price, you'll need to convert that string into a number first.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.run: Click Run. A small text box pops up on the stage with the question — that's input waiting. Type your name in the box and press Enter (or click the tick button next to the box). The moment you submit, the wait ends and Python runs the next line.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.greeting: Now the terminal shows Hello, followed by whatever you typed. Python glued "Hello, " and your name together with the plus sign and print put the whole thing in the terminal. Same program, different answer each time you run it — that's the magic of input.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.intr: Today's project: a game where you play Rock Paper Scissors against the computer. Three new ideas in one tutorial — random.choice for picking from a list, multiple elifs to cover the rules, and the and operator (you met it in Boolean Logic) for asking two questions at once. You already know everything else.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.impo:
Type the very first line:

import random

random is a standard Python module that adds dice-roll-like helpers. import means "load this set of tools so I can use them". After this line, anything inside random becomes available — the most useful one for games is random.choice.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.titl:
Type:

print("Rock Paper Scissors")

player = input("Enter rock, paper, or scissors: ")

The player variable holds whatever the kid types — text. We won't wrap with int because we're comparing words, not numbers.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.comp:
Type:

choices = ["rock", "paper", "scissors"]
computer = random.choice(choices)

print("Computer chose:", computer)

choices is a list of the three options. random.choice(choices) picks ONE at random — like rolling a 3-sided die labelled rock/paper/scissors. The print line shows the kid what the computer picked, so they can see whether they won.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.tie:
Type:

if player == computer:
    print("It's a tie")

If both picked the same thing, it's a tie — easiest case to check first. Putting the simplest case first keeps the elif chain that follows clear.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.lose:
Type:

else:
    print("You lose")

If none of the if/elif branches matched, it's a loss. else is the catch-all. With nine possible (player, computer) combinations and 4 branches above (1 tie + 3 wins), this else covers exactly the 5 losing cases — neat.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.run: Click Run. Type rock. The terminal prints what the computer chose and one of: It's a tie, You win, or You lose. Run it five or six times — random.choice will pick a different option most times, so each run feels new.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.reca: Three new things this tutorial: import random, random.choice, and an elif chain with and. With these you can build dice games, magic 8-balls, random Mad Libs, or a coin flipper. Try it: wrap the whole game in a while loop and let the kid play round after round without re-running — you already know while from earlier.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.intro: Last tutorial, your if checked ONE thing — age >= 10. But real decisions often need more. A theme-park ride might need "tall enough AND has a ticket". A free coupon might accept "adult OR has a voucher". Today you'll learn the three small words Python uses to combine conditions: and, or, not. Each one is a standard Python keyword.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.truthyF: Python has True and False — but inside an if, it's actually more flexible than that. A few values count as 'falsy' — Python treats them as False even though they aren't the word False. Those are: 0, the empty text "", an empty list [], an empty dict {}, and None. Everything else — any non-zero number, any non-empty text, any non-empty list — is 'truthy' and counts as True. So if my_list: works as 'is the list not empty?'. Useful to remember as you write more if-checks.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.run: Click Run. The terminal prints Allowed (AND) and Allowed (OR) — and Access denied at the bottom. The not line stayed silent because has_pass is True. Try changing values on lines 1-3 and running again: make vip = True and Access will flip to allowed; make has_pass = False and the AND check will stop passing.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.recap: You now have and, or, not. With these three words you can express almost any rule a game or app would need. A Rock Paper Scissors judge uses and chains to spot who wins. A login screen uses and to require both username and password. A discount rule uses or to accept either a coupon or a member card. Keep them in your head: and is strict, or is lenient, not flips, parentheses group.
gui.tutorials.pythonBreak.intro: The loops you've built so far end on a fixed condition — guess the right number, count to n, walk every item. Real programs often need a different kind of stop: keep going until something specific happens, then jump out. Today's keyword is break — Python's instant exit from any loop.
gui.tutorials.pythonBreak.setup:
Type the title and an empty list:

print("Type a word to add. Type stop to finish.")

words = []

The square brackets create an empty list — no items yet. The kid will type words one at a time and the list will grow.
gui.tutorials.pythonBreak.whileTrue:
Type:

while True:
    word = input("Word: ")

while True is Python's way of saying "loop forever" — True never becomes False, so the loop has no stopping condition. The only way out will be break. Inside the loop, ask the kid for a word.
gui.tutorials.pythonBreak.break:
Still inside the loop, type:

    if word == "stop":
        break

break is standard Python. The moment Python runs break, it pops out of the nearest loop instantly — even if the loop's condition is still True. Without break, while True would run forever and the kid could never finish.
gui.tutorials.pythonBreak.append:
Still inside the while, but OUTSIDE the if (same indent as the if), type:

    words.append(word)

When the word isn't stop, this line runs and the word joins the list. When the word is stop, break has already fired and Python never reaches this line.
gui.tutorials.pythonBreak.print:
Back to NO indent — outside the while — type:

print("Your words:", words)

This line only runs after break has fired. The indentation tells the whole story: indented lines are inside the loop, the un-indented line below is what happens after.
gui.tutorials.pythonBreak.run: Click Run. Type apple, then banana, then mango, then stop. The terminal shows your three words gathered in a list. Run again with anything you like — the kid decides when the loop ends.
gui.tutorials.pythonBreak.recap: while True with break is one of Python's most useful loop patterns. Chat bots that listen until quit. Games that loop until game-over. Calculators that compute until the kid says done. Any time the program should keep going until SOMETHING happens — break is the way out.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuildAListAsYouGo.in: The shopping bills you've built so far throw away each item right after adding it to the total — once the loop is over, only the final number is left. Today you'll keep a record of every item the kid added, by growing a list as the loop runs. By the end, your program will print the total AND show every name in the basket.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuildAListAsYouGo.se:
Type the opening:

print("Store Your Items")

n = int(input("How many items? "))

Familiar territory — title and count. The new variable comes next.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuildAListAsYouGo.em:
Type:

items = []
total = 0

The square brackets [] make an empty list — like a row of empty boxes, ready to be filled. items is a list, total is a single number. Both start empty/zero. As the loop runs, items will grow and total will climb.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuildAListAsYouGo.lo:
Type:

for i in range(n):
    name = input("Enter item name: ")
    price = int(input("Enter price: "))
    qty = int(input("Enter quantity: "))

    items.append(name)

    total = total + (price * qty)

The loop body asks three questions: name (text), price (number), quantity (number). items.append(name) adds the name to the END of the list — append is a list method, standard Python. The list grows by one each loop.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuildAListAsYouGo.ap: Lists are different from variables. A variable holds ONE value at a time — assigning a new value forgets the old. A list holds MANY values in order, and append adds a new one without forgetting the rest. After three appends with apple, banana, mango, items will look like ['apple', 'banana', 'mango'].
gui.tutorials.pythonBuildAListAsYouGo.su:
After the loop ends — back to no indent — type:

print("Items bought:", items)
print("Total Bill:", total)

The first line prints the whole list at once — Python knows how to display a list neatly with the names in square brackets. The second line prints the total. Two snapshots of what just happened: the contents and the cost.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuildAListAsYouGo.ru: Click Run. Type 3 items. Add apple at 10 each, qty 2. Add bread at 30, qty 1. Add milk at 50, qty 1. The terminal prints Items bought: ['apple', 'bread', 'milk'] and Total Bill: 100. The list remembered every name in the order you typed them.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuildAListAsYouGo.re: Lists are one of Python's most useful tools. You can store quiz questions, costume names for a sprite, levels in a game, students in a class, even other lists. Python has more list tricks built in — counting how many things are in a list, finding the biggest, sorting them — each in one short word.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInAdvanced.intr: You've seen sum, max, min, len, sorted — those work on data alone. Today's tools work WITH a function you wrote yourself: map applies your function to every item in a list, filter keeps only the items your function approves of. Plus zip and enumerate, which pair lists up neatly. Together they often replace a whole for loop with one short line.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInAdvanced.abs:
Type:

num = -5
print("Absolute value:", abs(num))

abs is standard Python — it removes the sign. abs(-5) gives 5; abs(5) gives 5; abs(0) gives 0. Tiny tool, useful when you don't care about direction, only distance.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInAdvanced.map:
Type:

numbers = [1, 2, 3]

def double(x):
    return x * 2

print("Doubled:", list(map(double, numbers)))

map takes two arguments — a FUNCTION and a LIST. It runs the function on every item and gives back a new sequence of results. We wrap it in list(...) to force the results out into a real list. Result: [2, 4, 6] — every number doubled, no for loop in sight.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInAdvanced.filt:
Type:

def greater_than_one(x):
    return x > 1

print("Filtered:", list(filter(greater_than_one, numbers)))

filter also takes a function and a list, but the function must return True or False. Only items where the function returns True survive. greater_than_one is True for 2 and 3, False for 1 — so the result is [2, 3].
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInAdvanced.zip:
Type:

names = ["Aman", "Riya", "Kabir"]
scores = [90, 85, 88]

print("Paired:", list(zip(names, scores)))

zip pairs items from two lists by position — first with first, second with second. Result: [('Aman', 90), ('Riya', 85), ('Kabir', 88)] — a list of tuples. Useful for printing a table, or for matching things up after sorting them separately.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInAdvanced.enum:
Type:

print("With index:", list(enumerate(names)))

enumerate pairs every item with its position number — starting at 0. Result: [(0, 'Aman'), (1, 'Riya'), (2, 'Kabir')]. Useful when you want to loop over a list and ALSO know how far along you are: "3rd in line, here is your name".
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInAdvanced.run: Click Run. The terminal shows the absolute value, the doubled list, the filtered list, the paired tuples, and the indexed pairs. Each result that would have taken 3-5 lines of for loop fits in one line. That's why these tools are favourites of experienced Python programmers.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInAdvanced.reca: Quick guide. map: transform every item the same way. filter: drop items that fail a test. zip: pair lists up by position. enumerate: tag each item with its index. abs: drop the sign. As your programs grow these will save you typing — and once you spot the pattern, your code reads more like a sentence than a recipe.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.fun: We've been using the word FUNCTION for a while — print, int, input, len. Let's nail down what one actually is. A function is like a tiny vending machine: you give it something (the input), it does its job inside, and sometimes it gives you something back (the output). print takes a value and shows it on the terminal. int takes text and gives back a number. len takes a list and gives back its size. Same shape — input, job, output — but each does a different job. Today we'll meet a whole shelf of these built-in machines.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.int: Python comes with dozens of small functions ready to use — no import needed. Today we'll meet the most useful ones for working with lists: sum, max, min, len, sorted, type. They each do one specific job, and together they're enough to write a real little stats program in five lines.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.lis:
Type:

numbers = [5, 8, 2, 10]

print("Numbers:", numbers)

The square brackets here create a LIST — a single name that holds many values in order. numbers holds five-eight-two-ten as one bundle. Lists are everywhere in Python: shopping items, quiz questions, scores, friend names. The first print shows the whole list.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.sum:
Type three more lines:

print("Total:", sum(numbers))
print("Highest:", max(numbers))
print("Lowest:", min(numbers))

sum adds every number in the list. max returns the biggest, min the smallest. All three are standard Python — they work on any list of numbers, no matter how long. Imagine writing a for loop yourself for each — these save you the work.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.len:
Type:

print("Count:", len(numbers))

len tells you how many things are in a list. Here it'll print 4, because the list has four numbers. len also works on strings — len("Python") is 6 — and on most other Python collections.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.sor:
Type:

print("Sorted:", sorted(numbers))

sorted gives you back a NEW list, smallest to largest, without changing the original. After this line numbers is still [5, 8, 2, 10] — the print just shows [2, 5, 8, 10]. Important: sorted does not modify; it returns a new sorted copy.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.typ:
Type the last line:

print("Type:", type(numbers))

type shows what KIND of thing a value is — Python prints something like <class 'list'>. Useful when debugging — if a print shows a string when you expected a number, type tells you so. type works on any value: type(5) is int, type("hi") is str, type(True) is bool. One more thing — Python is what's called DYNAMICALLY TYPED, which is a fancy way of saying: a variable doesn't lock into one type. The same name `x` can hold a number now and text later. Python tracks the TYPE on the value, not on the box.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.run: Click Run. The terminal shows six lines: the original list, the total 25, the highest 10, the lowest 2, the count 4, the sorted version, and the type. Six built-in tools, one short program.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.rec: Change numbers to your test scores, your daily steps, the number of likes on your last 5 posts. The same six tools work on any list. Pair them with strings, files, scores, votes — anywhere you have a bundle of values, these built-ins save you a loop.
Last tutorial your functions PRINTED their results, but they couldn't pass values to other functions. Today you'll meet return — the way a function hands a result back to whoever called it. With return, the answer from one function can flow straight into the next. That's how you connect tiny commands into bigger programs.

Type:

def add(a, b):
    return a + b

Notice — no print this time. The keyword return is standard Python. It says "give this value back to the caller and stop". The caller can then store the value, print it, or pass it into another call. One small but important note: if a function has no return at all (or just an empty return), Python quietly hands back a special value called None — meaning 'nothing here'. You'll meet None more later; for now just remember: every function hands SOMETHING back, even if it's None.

Type:

def multiply(a, b):
    return a * b

Same shape as add, just multiplication instead of addition. Two tiny functions, each doing one job and handing the answer back. Now we can connect them.

Below, type:

result = multiply(add(2, 3), 4)

Read it from the inside out. add(2, 3) runs first and returns 5. That 5 becomes the FIRST argument to multiply. multiply(5, 4) runs and returns 20. The whole expression gives 20, which gets stored in result. Two functions chained — the answer from one piped straight into the other.

Type:

print(result)

You could also have written print(multiply(add(2, 3), 4)) directly — but storing the answer in a named variable makes long programs easier to read. Either way works.

Click Run. The terminal prints 20. (2 + 3) is 5, then 5 × 4 is 20. The two functions cooperated without you writing any extra code — return made it possible. Imagine a third function divide(a, b) — you could chain that on too: divide(multiply(add(2, 3), 4), 2).

Big programs are made of small functions that hand results to each other. With return, every function becomes a reusable building block. The same chained-function pattern shows up everywhere — converting units, parsing dates, pricing items with tax, scoring quiz answers. Anywhere you'd want to feed one calculation into another, return is the bridge.
gui.tutorials.pythonCustomCommands.intro: So far you've used Python's built-in commands — print, input, len. Today you'll teach Python a NEW command that does whatever you want. We call this making a function. In Scratch this is the "My Blocks" feature — you make a custom block that does a chunk of work, then drop it anywhere. In Python, the keyword is def.
gui.tutorials.pythonCustomCommands.def:
Type:

def greet():
    print("Hello!")

def is standard Python — short for "define". The line def greet(): is saying "make a new command called greet that takes no extra info". The colon at the end and the indented line below tell Python what greet should DO when called. Notice nothing has happened yet — defining a function just teaches Python the recipe.
gui.tutorials.pythonCustomCommands.defVs: Defining a function is like writing the recipe down on a card. Nothing gets cooked. To actually run the steps inside, you have to CALL the function — type its name with parentheses on a line, like greet(). The parentheses are how Python knows you want to USE the function, not just point at it.
gui.tutorials.pythonCustomCommands.callT:
Below the def block, with NO indent, type:

greet()
greet()
greet()

Three calls — Python will run the function's body three separate times. This is the magic: write the print line ONCE, reuse it as many times as you like. If you ever wanted to change "Hello!" to something else, you only edit ONE line and every call updates.
gui.tutorials.pythonCustomCommands.run: Click Run. The terminal prints Hello! three times — once for each call. Same recipe, executed three times. Try changing the print inside greet to something else and run again — all three calls produce the new message. One change, three updates.
gui.tutorials.pythonCustomCommands.recap: Functions let you NAME a chunk of work. Instead of repeating the same lines, you write them once and reuse the name. Bigger programs are mostly functions calling other functions. Anywhere you find yourself writing the same lines twice, that's a hint to wrap them in a def and reuse.
gui.tutorials.pythonDateAndTime.intro: Times and dates show up in every app — message timestamps, photo dates, game high-score boards, birthday reminders, expiry warnings. Python has a built-in module called datetime that hands you the current moment and lets you pull out the year, the month, the day, the hour. Same import idea you used for random and math, plus one new shape of import.
gui.tutorials.pythonDateAndTime.fromImpo:
Type the very first line:

from datetime import datetime

Until now you typed import X. The from version pulls one specific tool out of a module. The first datetime here is the module name; the second is the class inside it. After this line, datetime (the class) is yours to use directly — no need to write datetime.datetime every time.
gui.tutorials.pythonDateAndTime.getNow:
Leave a blank line, then type:

now = datetime.now()

print("Right now:", now)

datetime.now() asks Python: what is the current date and time, exactly? The answer comes back as a single datetime value and gets stored in now. Printing it shows the date, the time, and a tiny bit of extra precision — all in one go.
gui.tutorials.pythonDateAndTime.parts:
Type four more lines to grab individual pieces:

print("Year:", now.year)
print("Month:", now.month)
print("Day:", now.day)
print("Hour:", now.hour)

The dot tells Python: take this datetime and grab one of its built-in pieces. now.year, now.month, now.day, now.hour each give back a single number. There's also now.minute, now.second, and now.weekday — try those after you've run this.
gui.tutorials.pythonDateAndTime.strftime:
Type the pretty-format lines:

pretty = now.strftime("%A, %d %B %Y")
print(pretty)

strftime is how a datetime turns itself into nicely-formatted text. The string with percent codes is the recipe — %A is the full weekday name, %d the day number, %B the full month name, %Y the four-digit year. Whatever you put between the codes — commas, spaces, dashes — gets kept in the output. So today might come out as Saturday, 26 April 2026. Different recipe, different layout.
gui.tutorials.pythonDateAndTime.run: Click Run. The terminal prints the full timestamp, then the year, month, day, hour each on their own line, then the pretty formatted version at the bottom. Run again in a minute — the hour and minute will have moved forward, the date will look the same. Run again next week — the day will have changed.
gui.tutorials.pythonDateAndTime.recap: datetime is one of the most useful modules in Python. Anywhere your program needs to know WHEN something happened, or stamp a piece of data with the moment — message timestamps, log entries, save-game dates, photo metadata, expiry checks, age-from-birthday calculations — datetime has the tool. The same dot-grab pattern you used for tuple parts works for time pieces too.
gui.tutorials.pythonFstrings.intro: When you mixed text and numbers earlier you used the plus sign to glue them together — and wrapped numbers in str so the plus would behave. It works but gets long fast. Today's tutorial: a cleaner way that's standard Python — the f-string. Real programmers reach for it almost every day.
gui.tutorials.pythonFstrings.setup:
Type three variables:

name = "Aman"
score = 85
total = 100

A name as text, a score and a total as numbers. The greeting we print will use all three.
gui.tutorials.pythonFstrings.plus:
Type the long way first, the way you've done before:

print("Hi, " + name + "! You scored " + str(score) + " out of " + str(total))

Notice the str around every number — without it, plus would refuse to mix text with numbers. Lots of plus signs, lots of quotes, easy to miss one. It works — it just isn't pretty.
gui.tutorials.pythonFstrings.fstring:
Type the same greeting the new way:

print(f"Hi, {name}! You scored {score} out of {total}")

The little f right before the opening quote is the magic. Inside the quotes, anywhere you want a variable's value, write its name in curly braces. Python fills in each one for you. Numbers don't need str — Python sorts that out automatically. One sentence, no plus signs, no str.
gui.tutorials.pythonFstrings.run: Click Run. The terminal shows two identical greetings — same words, same numbers — produced two completely different ways. Same output, very different code.
gui.tutorials.pythonFstrings.recap: Real Python programmers use f-strings everywhere — error messages, filenames, log lines, terminal output, web pages. The plus-glue still works, but f-strings read like English: a sentence with the variables sitting in their slots. From here on, prefer f-strings whenever you mix text and values.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.intro: You've got enough pieces now to build a real game. The computer will think of a secret number and give the kid a hint — too high, too low, or correct. You already know if and else. Today you'll learn elif — the word that lets you chain THREE possible answers together in one decision.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.title:
Type the first line:

print("Guess the number!")

This just prints a title in the terminal when the game starts. Anything in quotes is just text to Python — your title can be whatever you like.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.secre:
Leave a blank line, then type:

number = 5

This is your "secret" answer. It's not very secret right now — the kid can read the code. Real games hide the answer by having the computer pick a random number, but for now 5 is fine — it keeps our attention on the decision logic.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.guess:
Leave a blank line and type:

guess = int(input("Enter your guess: "))

Same pattern as the Numbers tutorial — ask a question and wrap with int, because we're going to COMPARE it to number. Comparing works on real numbers, not text.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.equal: Notice the double equals sign in if guess == number. That's not a typo. One equals sign (=) means STORE — like age = 10 stores 10 under age. Two equals signs (==) means CHECK — "are these the same?". Mixing them up is the most common bug in early Python code. Read your if conditions carefully.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.tooLo: Click Run. Type 3. The terminal says Too low. Walk through what happened: the first if asked is 3 the same as 5 — false. The elif asked is 3 greater than 5 — false. The else caught everything else. Python ran exactly ONE branch.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.tooHi: Click Run again. Type 9. Now the terminal says Too high. This time the first if was still false, but the elif was true — 9 is greater than 5 — so Python ran that one and skipped the else. First-true-wins.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.corre: Click Run once more. Type 5. The terminal says Correct! The very first if was true, so Python ran it and didn't even LOOK at the elif or else. Three runs, three different messages, one program.
gui.tutorials.pythonHandleAnyNumberOfInp: All your functions so far had a fixed number of parameters — exactly two for add(a, b), one for greet(name). But what if you don't know in advance how many things will be passed? Today you'll meet *args — a Python feature that lets ONE function accept any number of arguments, from one to a hundred.

Type:

def total(*numbers):
    result = 0
    for n in numbers:
        result = result + n
    return result

The star * is what's new. *numbers tells Python: "collect every argument that gets passed and put them all into a list called numbers". Inside the function, you treat numbers like any other list — for n in numbers loops over them, and we add each one onto result.

Without the star, you'd have to define total(a, b), total(a, b, c), total(a, b, c, d) — separate functions for each count. The star says "however many they pass, gather them up". The name args is just convention — you could call it numbers, items, parts. The * is what does the magic.

Below, type:

print(total(5, 3))
print(total(1, 2, 3, 4))
print(total(10, 20, 30, 40, 50))

Three calls with different numbers of arguments — 2, 4, and 5. Python collects each call's arguments into the numbers list and total adds them all up. No need to redefine the function for different argument counts.

Click Run. The terminal prints 8, 10, 150 — sums of the three different sets of numbers. Same one-line definition handled all three. Try total() with no arguments — it'll return 0, because the loop runs zero times.

Many built-in Python functions use *args under the hood. print can take any number of things to print — print("a", "b", "c") works because of *args. max(1, 2, 3) and min(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) too. Now that you've written one yourself, you understand how those tools work. Anywhere you need a function that adapts to one input or fifty — totalling quiz scores, joining player names, summing daily steps in a fitness app — *args is the trick.
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.meetPython: Python is a programming language — a way to tell a computer what to do, written in words and symbols people can read. Guido van Rossum created it back in 1991 and named it after Monty Python's Flying Circus, a comedy show he liked. Today people use Python everywhere — to build websites, games, robots, and even the AI behind your favourite apps. It's friendly to read, easy to learn, and fits inside lots of jobs. Let's start by saying hello.
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.interpreterRea: When you click Run, Python opens your file and reads it line by line — like reading a recipe top to bottom. Line 1 first, then line 2. Each line gets done before the next one starts. That's why the order of lines matters: Python doesn't peek ahead, it just walks down the page. Watch the terminal fill in line by line as Python reaches each print.
gui.tutorials.pythonIndexingAndSlicing.i: Lists hold many values in order, and so do strings — every character of a string sits at a position too. Today you'll learn the exact ways to reach in and grab one item, the last item, a chunk in the middle, every other one, or the whole thing reversed. Two new ideas: indexing means picking ONE position; slicing means picking a RANGE.
gui.tutorials.pythonIndexingAndSlicing.l:
Type:

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "mango", "kiwi", "grape"]

Five items, each at its own position. Python counts from ZERO — apple sits at 0, banana at 1, mango at 2, kiwi at 3, grape at 4. The first item is always at position 0, never at 1.
gui.tutorials.pythonIndexingAndSlicing.i:
Now the indexing block:

# Indexing — pick one item by position
print(fruits[0])
print(fruits[2])
print(fruits[-1])

The line starting with the hash sign is a comment — a note for humans, which Python ignores. The square brackets after the list reach IN to one position. fruits[0] is apple. fruits[2] is mango. And fruits[-1] is grape — the LAST item. A negative number counts from the end. fruits[-2] would be kiwi, fruits[-3] mango, all the way back.
gui.tutorials.pythonIndexingAndSlicing.s:
Type the slicing block:

# Slicing — pick a chunk
print(fruits[1:4])
print(fruits[:3])
print(fruits[2:])

A slice gives you a chunk. The colon between two numbers separates the START position from the STOP position. fruits[1:4] gives positions 1, 2, and 3 — banana, mango, kiwi. The stop number is where Python stops, NOT the last item to include. Leave the start blank and Python begins at zero. Leave the stop blank and Python goes to the end. So fruits[:3] is the first three; fruits[2:] is everything from position 2 onward.

Type:

# Step — every other item
print(fruits[::2])

A second colon adds a STEP — how many positions to jump each time. The number two means take one, skip one, take one, skip one. So fruits[::2] gives apple, mango, grape — every other fruit. The two empty slots before and after the colons mean start at the beginning and go all the way to the end. With a step of three you'd jump in threes; with a step of one you'd take every item.
gui.tutorials.pythonIndexingAndSlicing.r:
Type:

# Reverse — read backwards
print(fruits[::-1])

A negative step flips the direction. Step minus one means take every item, but going backwards. So fruits[::-1] gives grape, kiwi, mango, banana, apple. This is the shortest way to reverse a list in Python — no extra function needed. The same trick works on any string too, which you'll see in the next step.
gui.tutorials.pythonIndexingAndSlicing.s:
Type the last block:

# Same tricks work on strings
word = "Python"
print(word[0])
print(word[1:4])
print(word[::-1])

A string is a sequence of characters at positions, just like a list of single letters. word[0] is P. word[1:4] is yth — three characters from positions 1, 2, and 3. word[::-1] is nohtyP — the whole word reversed. Every trick you learned on the fruits list works on text in exactly the same way.
gui.tutorials.pythonIndexingAndSlicing.r: Click Run. The terminal prints each line in order — the three indexing answers, then the three slicing chunks, then every-other-fruit, then the reversed list, then the same tricks on the word. Eleven different ways to look INTO the same data.

Use a single index when you want ONE item — the third question in a quiz, the last enemy in a level. Use a slice when you want a CHUNK — the top three scores, the first half of a story, the suffix of a filename. A negative index is perfect for last and second-to-last. A second colon adds a step — every second item, every third, or with a minus sign, the whole thing backwards. The same square-bracket syntax works on a list of fruits, a list of names, a list of scores, or any string. Square brackets are how Python lets you reach into ordered things.
gui.tutorials.pythonFiles.intro: Last tutorial you used open, write, and close to save and load a score — three lines per file operation. Today you'll meet a cleaner Python pattern called with that bundles the open and close into one block, so you can never forget the close. Same idea, fewer lines, safer code.
gui.tutorials.pythonFiles.mountFS: Before we write any file, tell Python where to put it. Look at the top of the terminal toolbar — there's a button labelled File System. Click it. A folder picker opens — pick any folder on your computer, or make a new one called shopping. Click Allow when your browser asks. From this moment on, every file your code saves lands in that real folder on your computer. You'll be able to open it later and see your data sitting there as a real file.
gui.tutorials.pythonFiles.list:
Type:

items = ["apple", "banana", "mango"]

Three fruits in a list — the data you want to remember. In a real shopping app this list might come from the kid typing items in. Today we'll save these three to a file, then read them back.
gui.tutorials.pythonFiles.write:
Type:

with open("shopping.txt", "w") as f:
    for item in items:
        f.write(item + "\n")

open is standard Python. The first part is the filename, the second part is the mode — w for write, which means create a new file or replace the old one. The as f stores the open file as a name we can use inside the indented block. Loop through items and write each one, with a backslash-n at the end to put each on its own line.
gui.tutorials.pythonFiles.withExplain: The with keyword is a Python feature for things that need to be closed when you're done with them — files, network connections, and more. The moment the indented block under with ends, Python automatically closes the file. No closing line to write yourself, no risk of forgetting. Always use with when you open a file.
gui.tutorials.pythonFiles.read:
Type:

with open("shopping.txt", "r") as f:
    saved = f.read()

Same open and with — but this time the mode is r for read. f.read pulls every character in the file into one string, which we store as saved. The with closes the file when the indented block ends, just like before.
gui.tutorials.pythonFiles.print:
Type:

print(saved)

The whole file's contents — three fruits, each on its own line — comes back as one string and prints to the terminal. Whatever you wrote, you just read back. The file held your data while no Python code was running.
gui.tutorials.pythonFiles.run: Click Run. Python writes the three fruits into shopping.txt, then opens the same file and reads them back into saved, then prints saved. The terminal shows your three fruits, each on its own line. Now open the folder you picked at the start of this tutorial — shopping.txt is sitting there as a real file on your computer's disk.
gui.tutorials.pythonFiles.recap: Files are how programs REMEMBER. Save your game's high score, your todo list, the kid's name and avatar — anything you want to keep between runs. The pattern is the same every time: click File System once to pick a folder, then open with a mode and do the read or write inside a with block. Python closes the file for you and writes it straight to your computer's disk. Open the folder a month from now — your shopping.txt is still there with the same three fruits, just like a real Python program on a regular computer.
gui.tutorials.pythonKeepTrying.intro: Last tutorial you nested an if inside an else to give a second chance. But what if you want to keep asking forever, until the kid guesses right? Copying the same code over and over wouldn't scale. Today you'll meet the while loop — Python's way of saying "keep doing this as long as a condition is true". In Scratch this is the "repeat until" block. In Python it's one keyword: while.
gui.tutorials.pythonKeepTrying.setup:
Type the opening:

print("Guess the number!")

number = 5

guess = int(input("Enter your guess: "))

Same three lines you've used twice already — title, secret, first guess. The interesting bit comes next.
gui.tutorials.pythonKeepTrying.regrab:
Still indented inside the while, type:

    guess = int(input("Try again: "))

This is critical — you MUST change guess somewhere inside the loop. Otherwise guess stays the same number it was before the loop started, guess != number stays true forever, and the loop never ends. That's called an infinite loop — a real bug you'll hit more than once.
gui.tutorials.pythonKeepTrying.celebrate:
Go back to the very left edge (no indent) and type:

print("Correct!")

This line is OUTSIDE the while — Python only runs it after the loop has finished. And the loop only finishes when guess == number. So by the time this line runs, the kid has guessed right. Notice how the indentation tells the whole story of what runs when.
gui.tutorials.pythonKeepTrying.run: Click Run. Start far away — type 1. Terminal: Too low. Try 9 — Too high. Try 3 — Too low. Try 6 — Too high. Try 5 — Correct! The loop ran four times and each time re-asked for a guess. The moment your guess matched, the loop stopped and the celebration printed.
gui.tutorials.pythonKeepTrying.recap: Two dangers with while. One: forgetting to update the variable inside the loop, so the condition never changes — that's called an infinite loop. Two: what if the kid just wants to QUIT without guessing? Right now the only way out is to guess correctly. Python has a keyword called break that pops you out of a loop early — useful when the condition is still true but you've decided to stop. Think chat bots that quit on the word bye, shopping baskets you stop adding items to, or a quiz that ends the moment you score full marks.
gui.tutorials.pythonLists.intro: A list is ONE name that holds MANY values in order. Imagine a row of labelled boxes lined up — that's a list. In Scratch you've made these by clicking "Make a List" and adding items one at a time. In Python it's a single line: name equals square brackets, values inside, commas between.
gui.tutorials.pythonLists.make:
Type:

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "mango"]

Three items, each at its own position. Python counts from ZERO — apple sits at 0, banana at 1, mango at 2. The first item is always at position 0, never at 1. Items can be strings, numbers, even other lists.
gui.tutorials.pythonLists.inspect:
Type two lines:

print(fruits)
print(len(fruits))

The first prints the whole list — Python shows it back with the brackets and commas, exactly how you wrote it. len is standard Python — it counts how many items are in the list. With three fruits, len(fruits) gives 3. len works on strings and most other collections too.
gui.tutorials.pythonLists.append:
Type:

fruits.append("kiwi")
print(fruits)

The dot tells Python: take this list and run a built-in command on it. append adds a new item to the END of the list. The list grew by one — you didn't make a new list, the same fruits now has four items. This is called a method — built-in commands that belong to a type.
gui.tutorials.pythonLists.loop:
Type:

for f in fruits:
    print("I like", f)

You've used for with range before — that gave you numbers 0, 1, 2 and so on. This time, for f in fruits walks every item of the list directly. Each turn through the loop, f IS the next item — first apple, then banana, then mango, then kiwi. No range, no index, no counting. Just every item, one by one. This is one of the most common patterns in real Python — for X in <list> shows up in almost every program that handles many things.
gui.tutorials.pythonLists.run: Click Run. The terminal shows the original three fruits, the count 3, the grown list of four (with kiwi added), and four lines beginning with I like. One short program, four different ways of looking at the same list.
gui.tutorials.pythonLists.recap: A list is one of Python's most useful types. Anywhere you'd say "lots of things, all the same kind" — game scores, friend names, todo items, photos, levels, costumes, classmates — a list fits. From here on, lists show up in almost every program you write.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.intro: Every program you've written so far runs the same way from top to bottom, one line after another. Today your program will start to DECIDE — it will do one thing when the answer is big enough, and something different when it isn't. In Scratch this is the "if-else" block shaped like a C. In Python it's two keywords: if and else. Let's build one.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.indentEx: Look at the four spaces before print. Python uses those spaces — called indentation — to know which lines belong to the if. Most editors, including this one, add them automatically when you press Enter after a colon. If you forget to indent, Python will complain. If you add extra spaces, Python will complain. Same amount of indent, every time. Python's creator chose indentation on purpose — there's a famous Python motto, 'readability counts'. The shape of your code IS part of what it means.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.runPass: Click Run. Type 12 when it asks. The terminal prints You can join! — because 12 is 10 or more, so the if was true and Python ran the first indented line. It SKIPPED the else branch entirely.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.runFail: Click Run again. This time type 5. Now the terminal prints You are too young — because 5 is not 10 or more, the if was false, and Python ran the else branch instead. One program, two different paths, chosen live depending on what the kid answers.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.recap: You used greater-than-or-equal — that's the angle bracket pointing right next to an equals sign. Python has more comparisons too. Greater than. Less than. Less than or equal. Two equals signs side by side mean is exactly the same. An exclamation mark and an equals sign mean is not the same. Try changing the condition to is exactly 10, or is less than 18. Each one gives the program a different rule for who gets in.
gui.tutorials.pythonMath.intro: Python's basic maths covers plus, minus, times, divide. But what about square roots, powers, the value of pi, rounding decimals? Python has a math module — a bundle of built-in maths tools you unlock with one line: import math. Same kind of import you used for random.
gui.tutorials.pythonMath.import:
Type the very first line:

import math

import is standard Python — it loads a bundle of tools so the rest of your code can use them. After this line, anything inside math is available — written as math.toolname.
gui.tutorials.pythonMath.piSqrt:
Type two lines:

print("Pi:", math.pi)
print("Square root of 16:", math.sqrt(16))

math.pi is the famous circle constant, about 3.14159. math.sqrt finds the square root — for 16 it gives 4.0 (the dot-zero means it's a decimal, not a whole number). Both standard Python.
gui.tutorials.pythonMath.powCeilFloor:
Type three more lines:

print("2 to the power 10:", math.pow(2, 10))
print("Round up 3.2:", math.ceil(3.2))
print("Round down 3.8:", math.floor(3.8))

math.pow takes a base and an exponent — two to the power ten gives 1024. ceil rounds UP to the next whole number, so 3.2 becomes 4. floor rounds DOWN, so 3.8 becomes 3. Useful when you need a guaranteed whole answer.
gui.tutorials.pythonMath.run: Click Run. The terminal shows the value of pi, the square root of sixteen, two to the power ten, three-point-two rounded up, and three-point-eight rounded down. Five lines of maths, none of which Python's plus-minus-times-divide could do on their own.
gui.tutorials.pythonMath.recap: math is one of dozens of modules that come built into every Python install — random was another. Anywhere your code needs more than basic maths, math has the tool. Want the area of a circle? math.pi times radius times radius. The diagonal of a screen? math.sqrt of width-squared plus height-squared. Round a price up to the next rupee? math.ceil. Try math.factorial of 5, or math.log of 100 — each one is a single line, and the heavy maths is done for you.
Last tutorial each item had one price. But real shoppers buy 3 apples, 2 chocolates, 5 pencils. Today's program asks for BOTH the price AND the quantity for each item, and multiplies them. Same loop shape — one extra input, one tiny piece of maths.

Type the opening:

print("Shopping Bill with Quantity")

n = int(input("How many items? "))

total = 0

Same title-count-zero pattern as last time. The for loop comes next, with one new line inside.

Type:

for i in range(n):
    price = int(input("Enter price: "))
    qty = int(input("Enter quantity: "))

    total = total + (price * qty)

The loop body now asks TWO questions per iteration — price and quantity. The line total = total + (price * qty) does maths first (price times quantity = subtotal for that item), then adds the subtotal into the running total. Parentheses make the order obvious.

Python actually does multiplication before addition without the parentheses — but writing (price * qty) makes the line easy to read. Future-you, looking at this code months from now, won't have to remember the order-of-operations rules. Parentheses are free; clarity is priceless.

Python doesn't run a line left to right — it follows a precedence ladder. Parentheses are at the top: Python does whatever is inside them first. Next come power and multiply/divide. Then plus and minus. After that come comparisons like > and ==, and at the bottom sit the logical words and / or / not. So in 2 + 3 * 4, Python multiplies first (3 * 4 = 12), then adds (2 + 12 = 14). When in doubt, add parentheses — they jump anything to the top of the ladder.

Same finishing block as last tutorial — back to no indent:

print("Total Bill:", total)

if total > 100:
    print("Discount applied!")
    total = total - 10

print("Final Amount:", total)

Notice how reusing the same shape across tutorials helps you read code faster — your eyes know where to look.

Click Run. Type 2 items. Enter price 20 quantity 3 — that's 60. Enter price 30 quantity 2 — that's 60. Total: 120 — over 100, so the discount kicks in, Final Amount: 110. Two items but six things in the basket.

Things to play with: lower the discount threshold to 50, change the discount amount to a percentage like total = total * 0.9. Or print the line subtotal each loop so the kid sees the running tally. Two new tools so far — for + range, and a running total — already enough to build a quiz score counter, a fitness step tracker, a piggy bank app, or a game level summer.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.intro: Remember from the last tutorial — input always gives back text, even when the kid types digits. Today we'll see why that matters. Your program will ask for an age, add one to it, and predict the kid's next birthday. Adding only works on real numbers — so we need a way to turn "12" (text) into 12 (a number) before Python can add anything.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.strWhy: Here's the rule: the plus sign adds numbers OR glues text, never a mix. If you write "You will be " + 13 + " next year", Python stops with an error. Text + text works. Number + number works. Text + number is a mistake Python refuses to guess about. That's why we use str to turn 13 into "13" first — now everything is text and plus glues them all.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.realError: Try one more thing — when it asks your age, type a word like "hello" instead of a number. Watch the terminal turn red. That's an ERROR. Python is just saying 'I don't understand — hello isn't a number.' Errors are normal — every coder hits them every day. They aren't scary, and your code didn't break the computer. Read the LAST line of the red text — it tells you exactly what Python got confused about. We'll learn how to handle errors properly later, but for now, just know: red text = Python is asking for help.
gui.tutorials.pythonOneCommandMultipleAn: Last tutorial your function did the SAME thing every time you called it — print Hello! Today you'll write functions that take INFORMATION when called, and behave differently depending on what they get. Same command name, many different answers. The information is called a parameter.

Type:

def multiply(a, b):
    print(a * b)

Three functions now, all defined but none called yet. Each one takes two numbers, does some maths with them, and prints the answer. Notice how similar the three definitions look — same shape, just a different operator. That's a hint we'll improve later.

Below the defs (no indent), type:

add(5, 3)
subtract(10, 4)
multiply(2, 6)

When you call add(5, 3), the parameter a gets 5 and b gets 3 — like Python's filling the empty boxes. The function runs, prints 8, and finishes. Each call below uses different numbers — the same function gives a different answer each time.

Click Run. The terminal prints 8, 6, 12 — the answers from each call. Same trio of functions could now be reused with any numbers. Try add(100, 200) or multiply(7, 7) — the function definitions never change, only what you pass in.

Two words you'll hear: PARAMETER is the empty box in the function definition (a and b). ARGUMENT is the actual value you pass in when you call (5 and 3). Same idea, just the names differ depending on which side you're on. Don't worry about getting the names right — most programmers use them interchangeably.

Right now your functions PRINT but don't give back a value you can use. So you can't write x = add(5, 3) and then USE x. To make that work, the function needs to hand the value back instead of just printing it. Python has a keyword for exactly that — return. Once a function returns its answer, the caller can store it, print it, or feed it into another function — calculate a price with tax, look up a player's score from their name, build a chain of small calculators each handing the answer to the next.
gui.tutorials.pythonOneCommandOneJob.int: Sometimes one calculation has several answers — a sum AND a product, a name AND an age, x AND y coordinates.
