Until now, our functions could only return ONE thing. Today you'll learn to return TWO at the same time, then unpack them on the receiving side. Tiny addition to what you already know about return.
gui.tutorials.pythonOneCommandOneJob.def:
Type:

def calculate(a, b):
    return a + b, a * b

Look at the return — there are TWO values separated by a comma. Python wraps them up together as a pair. The function still hands ONE thing back; that one thing just happens to contain both values. We'll see how to take them apart on the next line.
gui.tutorials.pythonOneCommandOneJob.unp:
Below, type:

sum_result, product_result = calculate(5, 3)

The two names on the left, separated by a comma, are unpacking. Python takes the pair returned by calculate and puts the first value into sum_result, the second into product_result. This is called tuple unpacking — and it works any time a function returns multiple values.
gui.tutorials.pythonOneCommandOneJob.pri:
Type:

print("Sum:", sum_result)
print("Product:", product_result)

Now each value is a normal variable you can use independently. The function did one job — both calculations on the same two numbers — and handed back both results in one trip.
gui.tutorials.pythonOneCommandOneJob.run: Click Run. The terminal prints Sum: 8 and Product: 15. One call, two answers, both useful. Try changing the numbers — calculate(7, 7) gives 14 and 49.
gui.tutorials.pythonOneCommandOneJob.rec: Real uses: a function that splits a full name into first and last; a function that returns the min AND max of a list; a function that returns the x AND y of a sprite's position. Returning multiple values keeps related results together. The pair Python builds on the right of return is a tuple — the same fixed-together pack you met in the Tuples tutorial.
gui.tutorials.pythonPickWhatMatters.intr: You've passed numbers, strings, even multiple values into functions. Today you'll pass an ENTIRE LIST as a single argument — and the function will loop through it inside. The function picks out only the items that match a rule, and returns how many it found. Combining functions with lists is where Python starts to feel powerful.
gui.tutorials.pythonPickWhatMatters.patt: This shape — start at zero, loop through, bump up when a condition is true, return the total — is one of the most common patterns in programming. Same shape works for counting words, ticking off completed tasks, scoring a quiz. Once you spot the pattern in your own problem, the code almost writes itself.
gui.tutorials.pythonPickWhatMatters.call:
Below the def block, type:

nums = [5, 12, 7, 20, 3]

print(count_big(nums))

The whole list nums is passed in as a single argument. count_big loops over it inside and gives back how many items were greater than 10. Spoiler: 12 and 20 — that's 2.
gui.tutorials.pythonPickWhatMatters.run: Click Run. The terminal prints 2 — the count of items greater than 10. Try with a different list — change nums to [50, 60, 70] and you'll get 3. Or to [1, 2, 3] and you'll get 0. Same function, any list, instant answer.
gui.tutorials.pythonPickWhatMatters.reca: Stretch challenge: change count_big to count_above and add a second parameter — count_above(numbers, threshold) — so you can ask "how many bigger than 100?" or "how many bigger than 0?" Same pattern, just one extra empty box at the top of the function. You're now writing functions other people would actually use — count quiz answers above a passing score, count students taller than 1 metre, count messages longer than 50 letters.
gui.tutorials.pythonSaveYourData.intro: Real apps remember things between runs. Today you'll wrap save and load into reusable functions, then build a tiny menu that lets the kid save a score, load it back, or exit. One short program brings together almost everything you've learned: def, files, while True with break, input, and if-elif chains. This is what real Python feels like — small pieces working as a team.
gui.tutorials.pythonSaveYourData.mountFS: Before we write any file, tell Python where to put it. Look at the top of the terminal toolbar — there's a button labelled File System. Click it. A folder picker opens — pick any folder on your computer, or make a new one called scores. Click Allow when your browser asks. From this moment on, every file your code saves lands in that real folder on your computer. Open it later and you'll see score.txt sitting there as a real file.
gui.tutorials.pythonSaveYourData.saveFun:
Type the first function:

def save_score(score):
    file = open("score.txt", "w")
    file.write(str(score))
    file.close()

open is standard Python — it asks Python to open a file. The first piece is the filename, the second is the mode. The mode "w" means WRITE — make a fresh file (or replace the old one). file.write puts the score into the file as text — note str(score), because files only hold text. file.close finishes the work and lets Python release the file. Three lines: open, write, close.
gui.tutorials.pythonSaveYourData.menuLoo:
Below the functions (no indent), type:

while True:
    print("1. Save")
    print("2. Load")
    print("3. Exit")

    choice = input("Choose: ")

while True is the loop-forever pattern you met with break. Each turn, print a three-option menu, ask the kid to choose. The if/elif/else below will route them to the right action.
gui.tutorials.pythonSaveYourData.handleS:
Still inside the while, type:

    if choice == "1":
        s = input("Enter score: ")
        save_score(s)

When the kid picks 1, ask for a score and call save_score with it. save_score handles all the file work — this code doesn't need to think about open or close at all. That's the win of wrapping work in a function.
gui.tutorials.pythonSaveYourData.handleL:
Type the next branch:

    elif choice == "2":
        print("Saved:", load_score())

elif keeps the chain going. When the kid picks 2, call load_score and print whatever it returns. The function does the open-read-close work; this line just shows the result.
gui.tutorials.pythonSaveYourData.handleE:
Type the last branch:

    elif choice == "3":
        break

break pops out of the while, just like the chat-bot tutorial. Without it the kid would be stuck in the menu forever. With it, picking 3 ends the program cleanly.
gui.tutorials.pythonSaveYourData.run: Click Run. The menu shows up. Pick 1, type 100 as the score. The menu shows again. Pick 2 — the terminal prints Saved: 100, the value you just stored. Pick 3 to exit. Now open the folder you picked at the start of this tutorial — score.txt is sitting there as a real file with 100 in it. Run again — pick 2 straight away without saving — the file still has 100 from last time.
gui.tutorials.pythonSaveYourData.recap: Look at how much you used in one short program: File System mount to pick a real folder, def to make commands, open + write + close to touch a file, while True with break to keep a menu running, input to ask, if-elif to route, return to hand back the saved value. Seven big ideas working together. The same skeleton — menu loop, save and load functions, a file holding state on disk — is how high-score boards, todo apps, chat histories, and game saves all work behind the scenes.
gui.tutorials.pythonSecondChance.intro: Last tutorial's game ended on one guess — right or wrong, the game was over. Real games don't work that way. Today you'll build a version that gives the kid a second try if they miss the first time. We'll do it by NESTING one if inside another else — a little decision tree inside the bigger decision.
gui.tutorials.pythonSecondChance.setup:
Type these lines — same opening as last time:

print("Guess the number (2 chances)!")

number = 5

guess = int(input("Enter your guess: "))

The title mentions the 2 chances up front so the kid knows what to expect. number is still 5. The first guess is taken exactly like before.
gui.tutorials.pythonSecondChance.outerIf:
Type:

if guess == number:
    print("Correct!")

This is the "got it first try" path. If the kid guessed 5, Python prints the celebration and skips everything else. The interesting work happens in the else branch — when the first guess was wrong.
gui.tutorials.pythonSecondChance.elseSta:
Type:

else:
    print("Try again")

    guess = int(input("Second chance: "))

Note the BLANK line between the two indented lines — that's for readability, Python doesn't mind. The else block first tells the kid they missed, then asks for another guess. The variable guess gets reassigned — the new answer overwrites the old one in the same box.
gui.tutorials.pythonSecondChance.firstWi: Click Run. Type 5 straight away. Terminal prints Correct! and stops. Python never entered the else — the second-chance code sat there untouched. That's how branches work: only the path you take runs.
gui.tutorials.pythonSecondChance.missWin: Run again. Type 3. Terminal says Try again and asks Second chance. Type 5 this time. Terminal prints Correct! The outer if was false, so the else ran — it prompted, got a new guess, and the INNER if caught it. Two tries, one win.
gui.tutorials.pythonSecondChance.missMis: Run once more. Type 3, then 7. Terminal: Try again, then Game over. The outer if missed, the else ran, the inner if missed too, the inner else was the final catch. Two chances, both wrong — game over. The shape of your code controls exactly how the game feels.
gui.tutorials.pythonSecondChance.recap: Copy-pasting the same code for two tries works — but imagine writing it for 10 tries, or "keep going until they win". You'd run out of room fast. Python has a way to repeat the same block of code until a condition stops being true — a loop. Your game can accept unlimited tries in just a few lines once you know the loop pattern.
gui.tutorials.pythonSets.intro: Lists keep every value, even if it's repeated. Tuples too. Today you'll meet a set — a collection that automatically throws out any duplicates, so you only ever keep one of each. Sets are the perfect tool for questions like "what unique words appeared?" or "which players are on BOTH teams?"
gui.tutorials.pythonSets.lists:
Type:

team_a = ["Aman", "Riya", "Kabir", "Aman"]
team_b = ["Riya", "Zoya", "Kabir"]

Notice team_a has "Aman" twice — maybe by mistake, maybe because Aman scored twice. Lists keep both copies. We're going to convert these into sets and watch the duplicates disappear.
gui.tutorials.pythonSets.convert:
Type:

a = set(team_a)
b = set(team_b)

set is standard Python — it takes a list (or any collection) and gives you back a set with only the unique values. After this line a holds {"Aman", "Riya", "Kabir"} — the duplicate "Aman" was dropped automatically.
gui.tutorials.pythonSets.unique:
Type:

print("Unique Team A:", a)

The terminal will show three names — "Aman" only once. Sets use curly braces {} when shown, just like dictionaries — but no key:value pairs, just bare values.
gui.tutorials.pythonSets.intersect:
Type:

print("Common players:", a & b)

The & sign between two sets gives you the INTERSECTION — the values that appear in BOTH. "Riya" and "Kabir" are on both teams, so the result is the set {"Riya", "Kabir"}. One symbol, no loop, instant answer.
gui.tutorials.pythonSets.run: Click Run. The terminal shows the unique Team A (three names, no duplicates) and the common players (two names). All this without writing a single loop or if. Sets are tiny but mighty.
gui.tutorials.pythonSets.recap: Two more useful operators: a | b is the UNION (everyone on either team). a - b is the DIFFERENCE (everyone on Team A who is not on Team B). All standard Python. Use a set anywhere duplicates would be wrong: unique words in a story, unique IPs visiting a website, students who finished a quiz.
gui.tutorials.pythonSmartShoppingBill.in: Today you'll write a tiny shopping bill. The kid says how many items they bought, then types the price of each one — and Python adds them up. You'll meet two new tools at once: the for loop with range, and a running total. By the end you'll have a working cashier program.
gui.tutorials.pythonSmartShoppingBill.co:
Type:

print("Smart Shopping Bill")

n = int(input("How many items? "))

The variable n holds the number of items. We wrap input in int because we'll loop that many times — and you can't loop a string. Notice we picked a short name, n — that's a common Python convention for "count" variables.
gui.tutorials.pythonSmartShoppingBill.to:
Type:

total = 0

This is the running total — empty at the start. Every loop iteration will add the new price into total. The pattern "start at zero, add as you go" shows up everywhere — counting, scoring, summing — so it's worth noticing.
gui.tutorials.pythonSmartShoppingBill.fo:
Type:

for i in range(n):
    price = int(input("Enter price: "))
    total = total + price

for and range are both standard Python. range(n) makes a sequence of numbers 0, 1, 2 … up to n-1 — exactly n numbers. for i in range(n) means "do the indented block n times". Each loop the kid types a price, and total = total + price adds it on top of whatever total already had.
gui.tutorials.pythonSmartShoppingBill.di:
Back to no indent — the discount logic runs ONCE, after the loop has added every price:

print("Total Bill:", total)

if total > 100:
    print("Discount applied!")
    total = total - 10

print("Final Amount:", total)

Notice print can take more than one thing — it'll glue them with a space. The if checks the running total and knocks 10 off if it's big enough.
gui.tutorials.pythonSmartShoppingBill.sm: Click Run. Type 3 items. Enter prices 20, 30, 40. The terminal prints Total Bill: 90 — under 100, no discount, Final Amount: 90. Your for loop ran exactly three times because range(3) makes 0, 1, 2 — three values.
gui.tutorials.pythonSmartShoppingBill.bi: Run again. This time type 4 items. Enter 50, 50, 50, 50. Total: 200. Now the if catches it — Discount applied!, and Final Amount: 190. Same code, two different stories, depending on the prices.
gui.tutorials.pythonSmartShoppingBill.re: Three new ideas in one tutorial: for loops, range, and running totals. With these you can score a quiz, count up dance moves, build a virtual piggy bank, or sum up steps in a fitness app. Anywhere you have a count and want a sum, this for loop with a running total is the pattern.
gui.tutorials.pythonStoreDataWithMeaning: Lists and tuples store values by POSITION — color[0] is red because red was first. That works for short, ordered things, but for a student record you really want to look things up by name: name, score, level. Today you'll meet the dictionary — a structure that pairs a key (the label) with a value, like name tags on every piece of data.

Type:

student = {
    "name": "Aman",
    "score": 85,
    "level": 3
}

The curly braces {} make a dictionary. Inside, every entry is key: value. The keys are the labels ("name", "score", "level") and they're strings here. The values can be ANY kind of thing — a string, a number, a list, even another dictionary. Commas separate entries.

Type:

print(student["name"])
print(student["score"])

The square brackets after the dictionary's name reach IN, just like with a list — but instead of a number index, you use the KEY. student["name"] gives back "Aman". student["score"] gives back 85. Look up by meaning instead of by position — much easier to read.
Click Run. The terminal prints Aman and 85 — the two values you asked for by name. The third entry (level: 3) was left untouched, because we didn't ask for it.

Imagine the same student as a list: ["Aman", 85, 3]. Position 0 is the name, 1 is the score, 2 is the level. Six months later, you'd have to remember what each position MEANT. With a dictionary, the meaning is in the key — student["score"] tells you exactly what 85 represents. Code that reads itself.

After your two prints, try:

student["score"] = 90
student["grade"] = "A"
print(student)

The first line changes the existing score to 90. The second adds a brand new entry called grade. Then print(student) shows the whole dictionary. Dictionaries can grow and change — much like lists, but indexed by name.

Use a dictionary anywhere you'd write "X has a Y" in plain English: a sprite has a costume, a score, a position. A user has a name, an age, an email. A game level has a title, enemies, a goal. Most game saves and chat messages get sent around the internet as dictionaries (in a format called JSON) — what you've learned today translates directly.
gui.tutorials.pythonStrings.intro: Whenever a kid types something into your program, the answer comes back as text — sometimes with extra spaces, sometimes in the wrong case, sometimes with words you want to swap out. Python has built-in string methods to clean and reshape text without you re-typing anything. Today: strip, upper, lower, replace, and the in operator.
gui.tutorials.pythonStrings.text:
Type:

text = "  python code  "

Notice the spaces at the BOTH ends inside the quotes — they count as part of the value. Try clicking right after the second quote and you'll see the cursor jump past two spaces. Real input from kids often has stray spaces too — so cleaning is a real-life skill.
gui.tutorials.pythonStrings.strip:
Type:

print(text.strip())

Notice the dot — text.strip() means "call the strip method ON this text". Methods are functions that belong to a value. strip is standard Python — it returns a NEW string with leading and trailing whitespace removed, but text itself doesn't change.
gui.tutorials.pythonStrings.case:
Type:

print(text.upper())
print(text.lower())

upper returns a copy with every letter in CAPITALS. lower returns one with every letter in small case. Both standard Python. Useful when comparing answers — "Yes", "yes", "YES" should all count, so lowercase the kid's answer before checking.
gui.tutorials.pythonStrings.replace:
Type:

print(text.replace("python", "java"))

replace finds every copy of the first piece and swaps in the second. The original text is unchanged — replace returns a new string, just like the others. Could be useful for things like censoring rude words, building a Mad Libs game, or fixing typos.
gui.tutorials.pythonStrings.run: Click Run. The terminal shows the cleaned string, the uppercase version, the lowercase version, the replaced version, and Found python — because text contains python (with the spaces stripped, even). Five tiny tools, each doing one job perfectly.
gui.tutorials.pythonStrings.recap: Combine these with input and you can build a chat bot that recognises greetings even with weird spacing, a quiz that accepts answers in any case, or a story generator that swaps in the kid's name. Strings are 80% of what real programs do — knowing these methods well makes everything easier.
gui.tutorials.pythonTryExcept.intro: Real users type unexpected things. Real maths sometimes divides by zero. Until now, any error has crashed your program with a red wall of text. Today you'll learn try and except — Python's way of catching errors so the program can recover and keep running. You'll also meet finally, a block that ALWAYS runs, error or not.
gui.tutorials.pythonTryExcept.try:
Type:

try:
    a = int(input("Enter number: "))
    b = int(input("Enter another number: "))
    
    print(a / b)

try is standard Python — it tells Python "the indented lines below MIGHT fail; if they do, don't crash, jump to the except block I'll write next". The risky bits here are int(input(...)) (kid types non-digits?) and a / b (kid types 0 for b?).
gui.tutorials.pythonTryExcept.except:
Type:

except:
    print("Error occurred")

except is also standard Python — it catches the error from the try block above. When anything inside try fails, Python jumps straight here, runs your except code, and the program keeps going instead of crashing.
gui.tutorials.pythonTryExcept.finally:
Type:

finally:
    print("Done")

finally is also standard Python — it runs at the end whether or not there was an error. Useful for cleanup steps: closing files, hanging up network connections, saving progress. Even if the try crashes AND the except runs, finally still runs after.
gui.tutorials.pythonTryExcept.runOk: Click Run. Type 10 for the first number, 2 for the second. The terminal prints 5.0 (Python's division gives a decimal) and Done. The try block ran cleanly, the except was skipped, and finally still ran at the end.
gui.tutorials.pythonTryExcept.runZero: Run again. Type 10, then 0. The terminal prints Error occurred (the except caught the divide-by-zero) and Done (finally still ran). Without try/except, Python would have crashed loudly. With it, the program handles the problem gracefully and keeps moving.
gui.tutorials.pythonTryExcept.runText: Run once more. Type hello when it asks for a number. int can't convert that — error. Same except catches it, same Error occurred, same Done. One try/except handles many different kinds of failure, all in one neat block.
gui.tutorials.pythonTryExcept.recap: Use try/except whenever the line could fail because of OUTSIDE input — kid's text, files that might not exist, network calls that might time out. Don't use it to hide bugs in YOUR own code — those should be fixed, not silenced. Wrap the int(input(...)) line in any earlier game with try/except and the program stops crashing when the kid types nonsense — that one change makes any program much friendlier.
gui.tutorials.pythonTuples.intro: Sometimes a few values belong as a unit and shouldn't change once set — an x-y position on a stage, the red-green-blue parts of a colour, the day-month-year of a date. Python has a special type for these: a tuple. It looks like a list, but with round brackets instead of square — and once made, you can't add, remove, or change items. Lists grow and shrink; tuples stay fixed.
gui.tutorials.pythonTuples.color:
Type:

color = (255, 100, 50)

The round brackets and commas make a tuple. Three values inside — red, green, blue — packed together as one. This is exactly what graphics libraries use to describe a colour. Notice the brackets are () round, NOT [] square. Square brackets make a list; round brackets make a tuple.
gui.tutorials.pythonTuples.index:
Type:

r = color[0]
g = color[1]
b = color[2]

The square brackets after a tuple's name reach IN — color[0] grabs the first value, color[1] the second, color[2] the third. Important: Python counts from ZERO, not one. The first thing is at position 0. This is the same indexing that works for lists and strings.
gui.tutorials.pythonTuples.print:
Type:

print("Red:", r)
print("Green:", g)
print("Blue:", b)

Three prints — each labelled. Now you've taken a packed-together tuple, opened it up, and shown each piece on its own.
gui.tutorials.pythonTuples.run: Click Run. The terminal prints Red: 255, Green: 100, Blue: 50 — the three pieces of the original colour, neatly labelled. The tuple stayed put as a whole; we just looked into it.
gui.tutorials.pythonTuples.vsList: Lists [a, b, c] can grow with append, shrink, and reorder. Tuples (a, b, c) cannot — once made, they're fixed. Use a tuple when the values belong together and shouldn't change as a group. Use a list when you'll be adding or removing items as the program runs. Same indexing, different rules about modification.
gui.tutorials.pythonTuples.recap: Use them for coordinates: position = (x, y). RGB colours: (red, green, blue). Dates: (year, month, day). Anything where the count is fixed and changing one piece without the others would be wrong. Tuples also let a function hand back more than one value at a time — return (name, score) packs both into one tuple the caller can unpack.
gui.tutorials.pythonTypeConversionFixThe: You've been using int to turn text into numbers when you need to add or compare. Today you'll see exactly why — side by side. We'll take two numbers from the kid, once without int (wrong) and once with int (right), and watch what Python actually does. Spoiler: the same plus sign behaves completely differently depending on what it's adding.

When the plus sign is between two strings, Python glues them — no maths happens. "12" + "34" becomes "1234", the digits stuck together, NOT 46. This is exactly the concatenation you used to make greetings two tutorials ago. Python is doing the same thing here — it just isn't what we wanted this time.

From now on, every time you take a number from input and want to do maths with it, wrap it in int (for whole numbers) or float (for decimals like 3.5). There is also str going the other way — number back into text. int, float, str are all standard Python. Keep them handy — they show up in every program that mixes input and maths.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.namingR: Before we put something in a box, let's pick a good label. Python has rules for names. A name must start with a letter or an underscore — never a digit. After that you can use letters, digits, and underscores, but no spaces and no minus or @ signs. Names are case-sensitive — score and Score are TWO different boxes. And a few words are reserved for Python itself, like for, if, def, return, class, import — those can't be names. The convention people follow is snake_case — small letters with underscores between words, like player_name or high_score.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.tagAnatomy: Every HTML tag has three parts — the OPEN tag, the CLOSE tag, and the STUFF you put between. The blocks you will use shape themselves around the stuff. Watch — every C-shape block IS one tag with a slot for what is inside. Open at the top, close at the bottom, the slot in the middle.
gui.tutorials.htmlTextFormatting.slotIns: Every C-shape block has a SLOT in the middle — that is the content slot. Whatever you put in the slot is the STUFF between the open tag and the close tag. Today we will see what happens when blocks go INSIDE other blocks slots. That is called nesting.
gui.tutorials.htmlImages.attributesConce: Some tags carry extra information on the OPEN tag itself — like an tag carrying the picture's web address and a description. Those extras are called ATTRIBUTES — a name and a value, joined by an equals sign. The block has a slot for each one.
gui.tutorials.htmlSections.labelledRooms: Until now we used <div> — a generic box you label yourself. Now you will meet semantic tags — <header>, <nav>, <section>, <footer>. They do the same job as div, but their NAMES tell helpers (search engines, screen readers) what each part of your page IS. Like rooms in a house — a div is just "a room", but <kitchen> tells everyone what it is for.
gui.tutorials.htmlForms.whereDataGoes: When the reader fills in your form and clicks Send, the browser tries to send the data somewhere. WHERE? That depends on the form's action — the address you give it. We have not given this form an action yet, so clicking Send will not go anywhere — it just reloads. For now, you have built the LOOK of a form. Sending it for real is a bigger topic for another tutorial.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssIntro.structureVsSt: Until now you have learned HTML — the STRUCTURE of a page (head, body, headings, paragraphs, lists). Today you start learning CSS — the STYLE (colours, sizes, spacing, fonts). HTML decides what is there. CSS decides how it looks. Two jobs. Same page.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.everyElementIsB: Every element on your page — every heading, every paragraph, every div — is a BOX. And every box has four layers: the CONTENT in the middle, the PADDING around the content, the BORDER at the edge, and the MARGIN keeping its neighbours away on the outside. That is called the BOX MODEL — it is the most important diagram in CSS.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.stackedVsRow: By default, divs and other block elements STACK on top of each other — one per line, top to bottom. That is what you have seen so far. Today you will meet display: flex — a CSS power that arranges them SIDE BY SIDE in a row instead. Same boxes, different layout.
gui.tutorials.htmlTextFormatting.combine: Drag a paragraph below the underlined text. Inside the paragraph, drag a b block. Inside the b, drag an i block. Inside the i, drag a text block and type "This is bold AND italic." Tags inside tags COMBINE — the page shows both effects on the same words.
gui.tutorials.htmlTextFormatting.tripleS: Now nest a u inside the i. Same words inside become bold, italic, AND underlined. The deeper you nest, the more effects pile on.
gui.tutorials.htmlUploadImage.twoAddress: You have now seen TWO kinds of address. The image tutorial used a long web address starting with https:// — that works from anywhere on the internet. This time you typed just my-pet.jpg — that is a SHORT address that only works because the file lives in the SAME folder as your page. Long addresses for far-away pictures, short addresses for files you uploaded right here.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.clientServer: When you type youtube.com into your browser, the browser sends a quick message to a faraway computer that's always switched on — that computer is called a SERVER. The server has all of YouTube's HTML files saved on it. It sends one back to your browser. Your browser reads that HTML and draws the page on your screen. Every site works this way — Google, your school's website, your favourite game site. CodeSkool is special — your HTML lives RIGHT HERE in this editor while you build it. Once your page is done you can save it and put it on a real server too — and then anyone in the world can visit it.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssIntro.markupVsProgr: Quick comparison. HTML is what we call a MARKUP language — it DESCRIBES things on the page (this is a heading, this is a paragraph, this is a list). It can't ask the user a question. It can't add 2 plus 2. It just lays out what's there. CSS is similar — it describes how things LOOK. Python is different — Python is a PROGRAMMING language. Python can DO things — ask the user, decide, repeat, calculate. Three different jobs working together: HTML structures the page, CSS styles it, and Python or JavaScript can make it move and respond.
gui.tutorials.htmlDivs.pageAsTree: Look at what you've built. The body holds a div. The div holds a heading and a paragraph. Picture this as a TREE — the body is the trunk, the div is a branch, the heading and paragraph are leaves on that branch. Every HTML page is a tree like this — tags hold other tags, which hold more tags. This shape has a name: the DOM (short for Document Object Model). When you read about HTML online, you'll see that word a lot. Just remember — the DOM is your page drawn as a tree, with the outer tags closer to the trunk and the inner tags out at the leaves. Selectors, CSS rules, and animations all work by walking this tree.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.cascade: What if TWO CSS rules tell the same element different things? Imagine one rule says div { color: blue; } and another says .card { color: red; } — which colour does your card actually get? CSS has a tie-breaker: the more SPECIFIC selector wins. .card is more specific than just div, so the card will be red. Selectors with an id (like #main) are even more specific. If you ever have a CSS rule that doesn't seem to do anything, check if a more specific rule somewhere else is overriding it. The browser is just following the specificity ladder — once you know the order, the rules become predictable.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.units: When you read CSS online you'll meet several size units. Here's a quick guide. px (pixels) — one tiny dot on the screen, the simplest unit. em — relative to the current font size. rem — relative to the page's root font size. % (percent) — a percentage of the parent's size. vh and vw — percentage of the viewport (the visible browser window). For now, px is the easiest and works everywhere. As you build bigger sites you'll want em and rem so sizes scale together when zooming, and vh/vw for full-screen layouts. You don't need them all yet. Just recognise them when you see them.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.inheritance: Here's a useful CSS trick. If you set a colour or font on the body, every heading, paragraph, and word INSIDE the body inherits it automatically — you don't have to repeat it on each element. We say the property CASCADES down the page tree, parent to child to grandchild. So one rule like body { font-family: Comic Sans MS; } makes every paragraph, list item, and heading use Comic Sans — unless you override it on a specific element later. This is why CSS feels efficient once you get used to it: write the style once at the top, let it flow down to everything inside.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.urlAnatomy: Look at the URL you typed. A web address has parts — and once you know them, web addresses stop feeling random. https:// is the PROTOCOL — the language the browser uses to talk to the server. The next part is the DOMAIN — the name of the site (like google.com or youtube.com). After the slash comes the PATH — which page on that site you want. Sometimes there's a question mark and extra info called QUERY parameters. Every link you'll write breaks down this way. Knowing the parts helps you spot bad links — wrong protocol, wrong domain, missing path — and write working ones.
gui.tutorials.htmlImages.selfClosing: When you grab the img block in a moment, notice something different — it's a STACK shape, not a C-shape. Most blocks (like p, h1, div) have a SLOT in the middle for content to nest inside. img doesn't. That's because the img tag is SELF-CLOSING — it stands alone, holding all its information in attributes (src and alt). Other self-closing tags work the same way: input (the form box) and br (a line break). They don't WRAP content — they ARE the content. When you see a stack-shape block in the toolbox, that's your hint that the tag is self-closing.
gui.tutorials.htmlTextFormatting.inlineV: Here's something you might have noticed. Your bold text and italic text sat on the SAME LINE as the other words. But your paragraphs and headings each started on a NEW line. Why the difference? HTML elements come in two flavours. INLINE elements (like b, i, u, a) sit in the flow of words like extra colour or emphasis — they don't break the line. BLOCK elements (like p, h1, div) each take a whole line by themselves — the next thing has to start below them. Most of the time you don't have to think about it; the browser does the right thing automatically. Just know the difference exists — it explains why some things wrap into the line and others jump down.
gui.tutorials.cMath.intro: In Scratch you used hexagon-shaped math blocks like (x) + (y) and (x) * (y) — they slotted into other blocks where a number was needed. C does the same math but written FLAT, the way you'd write it on paper: x + y, x * y. The five C operators you'll use 99% of the time are + (plus), - (minus), * (times — note: NOT the letter x), / (divide), and % (modulo, the leftover after division). Today you'll see all five in action with two whole numbers, and we'll meet a surprising twist with division that trips up every beginner.
gui.tutorials.cMath.setup: Lines 4 and 5 set up int a = 10; and int b = 3; — two whole-number variables we'll use as inputs to the math. The lines below do the math with them and print each result. Using variables (instead of writing 10 + 3 directly) makes it easy to change the inputs and re-run. We could have called them x and y, or num1 and num2 — a and b are just short and clear for a math demo.
gui.tutorials.cMath.basicOps: Lines 6, 7, and 8 print a+b, a-b, and a*b. Notice the math expression sits INSIDE the printf, after the comma — that means the math happens FIRST, then printf prints whatever the result was. Same as Scratch — the inner block runs before the outer block. So a+b becomes 13, a-b becomes 7, a*b becomes 30. The * (asterisk) is multiplication — never use the letter x for times in C, the compiler would think x is a variable name. Plus and minus are the same as on a calculator.
gui.tutorials.cMath.division: Line 9 is a / b — that's 10 divided by 3. On a calculator you'd expect 3.3333... But because BOTH a and b are int (whole numbers), C does INTEGER DIVISION — it throws away the decimal part and gives you 3. Just 3, no remainder shown. This is one of the biggest gotchas in C — every beginner gets caught by it once. The rule: if both sides of / are int, the answer is int (rounded down). To get the decimal answer (3.33), at least ONE of a or b would need to be a float. We'll see that in a later chapter. For now, just remember: int / int = int.
gui.tutorials.cMath.modulo: Line 10 uses the % symbol — that is MODULO, not percent. Modulo gives you the LEFTOVER (the remainder) after division. 10 % 3: how many times does 3 fit into 10? Three times (3+3+3 = 9), with 1 left over. So 10 % 3 = 1. Try in your head: 17 % 5? 5 fits into 17 three times (15), with 2 left over. So 17 % 5 = 2. Modulo is super useful for one specific question: "is this number even or odd?" — n % 2 is 0 for even numbers and 1 for odd numbers, ALWAYS. You'll see this trick in many later chapters. (Side note: in printf strings, %% prints a literal % sign, because a single % means "format code starting".)
gui.tutorials.cMath.run: Predict before clicking Run: what will the division line show? Did you guess 3 or 3.33? Click Run. The Console shows: 13, 7, 30, 3 (yes, 3, not 3.33), 1. Now change a to 17 and b to 5 and Run again. Predict the new outputs first, then check. Same operations, different numbers. Math in C is mostly the same as math on a calculator — except for that integer-division surprise.
gui.tutorials.cArrLoop.intro: Arrays plus for loops are the most common combination in all of C. So common that the pattern has a name: "iterating an array". The trick is to use the for loop counter (usually i) as the array INDEX. The loop counts 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — exactly the valid indexes for a 5-slot array. Inside the body you can use nums[i] to grab whichever slot the counter is currently pointing at. Today you'll print every slot AND compute the sum and average — three useful things in one short loop.
gui.tutorials.cArrLoop.print: Lines 6 to 8: for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { print nums[i]; }. The loop counter i goes 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — exactly the valid indexes. Inside, nums[i] picks the right slot each pass. So pass 1 prints nums[0] (10), pass 2 prints nums[1] (20), and so on. This is the canonical "print every item" pattern. Notice the condition is i < 5 (less than) not i <= 5 — because the last valid index is 4, not 5. Common bug: writing i <= 5 walks one slot off the end.
gui.tutorials.cArrLoop.sum: Line 5: int sum = 0; — this BEFORE the loop. Inside the loop body: sum += nums[i]; — adds the current slot to the running total. This is the ACCUMULATOR pattern: start a variable at zero (or sometimes one), then add (or multiply, or count) each item as you walk through the array. Used everywhere — totalling a bill, counting matching items, finding the maximum, computing averages. Once you see this pattern in two or three programs, you'll spot it everywhere.
gui.tutorials.cArrLoop.avg: Line 11: sum / 5. Both sum and 5 are int, so this is INTEGER division — drops any decimal. For our numbers (sum = 150 ÷ 5 = 30) it's exact, no decimal lost. But for, say, sum = 23 ÷ 5 = 4 (would-be 4.6 lost the .6). To get the decimal, you'd compute as (float)sum / 5 — that "casts" sum to a float so the division is float-division. We'll keep it int for now; just know the trick exists.
gui.tutorials.cArrLoop.max: Challenge: find the LARGEST number in the array. The pattern is similar to accumulator. Add a line BEFORE the loop: int max = nums[0]; — start max at the first element. INSIDE the loop, add: if (nums[i] > max) max = nums[i]; — if the current item beats max, update max. After the loop, print max. Trace: max=10, see 10 (no), 20 (yes, max=20), 30 (yes, max=30), 40 (yes, max=40), 50 (yes, max=50). Final max=50. Same shape as the sum pattern — start somewhere, update as you go.
gui.tutorials.cArrLoop.run: Click Run. Console: nums[0] = 10, nums[1] = 20, ..., nums[4] = 50, Sum = 150, Average = 30. The for loop did 5 prints AND computed the sum AND the loop ran in microseconds. Real productivity. Try changing the array to {7, 14, 21, 28, 35} and Run again — predictions: each number prints, sum = 105, average = 21.
gui.tutorials.cArrLoop.recap: for (int i = 0; i < SIZE; i++) — burn this single line into your muscle memory. It's how you walk every array, every string, every list of anything in C. Hundreds of millions of lines of C code use this exact line shape. Combined with the accumulator pattern (start at 0, add as you go), you have the toolkit for processing any collection of data. Sum, average, count matches, find max, find min — all variations on this same combo.
gui.tutorials.cArr2d.intro: A 1D array is a single ROW of slots — last chapter's scores[5] was 1D. A 2D array is a GRID — rows AND columns. Think of a tic-tac-toe board (3 rows × 3 columns), a chessboard (8 × 8), a Sudoku puzzle (9 × 9), pixels in a small picture (whatever × whatever), a school timetable (5 days × 8 hours). Anywhere you have a regular grid of values, a 2D array is the natural fit. Today: a simple 3×3 grid filled with numbers 1 to 9, walked through with two nested for loops.
gui.tutorials.cArr2d.declare: Lines 4 to 8: int grid[3][3] = { {1,2,3}, {4,5,6}, {7,8,9} };. The shape: int NAME[ROWS][COLS] = { {row0}, {row1}, {row2} };. The TWO sets of brackets — [3][3] — say "3 rows, 3 columns". The init values look like a list of lists, each inner { } being one row. Reading line by line: row 0 is {1,2,3}, row 1 is {4,5,6}, row 2 is {7,8,9}. So the grid has 9 cells total (3 × 3 = 9).
gui.tutorials.cArr2d.setCells: To access a cell: grid[row][col]. grid[0][0] is top-left (= 1). grid[2][2] is bottom-right (= 9). grid[1][1] is the center (= 5). grid[1][2] is middle row, third column (= 6). The convention is ALWAYS [row][col] — outer index first (which row), inner index second (which column within that row). Don't mix them up — grid[2][1] (third row, second column = 8) is different from grid[1][2] (second row, third column = 6).
gui.tutorials.cArr2d.init: Lines 9 to 14: outer for runs rows 0 to 2, inner for runs cols 0 to 2 within each row. Inside the inner body: printf("%d ", grid[row][col]); — print the current cell with a space after it. After the inner loop finishes one row, line 13 prints \n to drop to the next line. Same nested-loops pattern as the chapter on nested loops, now with the grid index using both counters.
gui.tutorials.cArr2d.read: You don't always need a loop. Try adding (after the existing nested loops) a single line: printf("Center: %d\n", grid[1][1]); — that just prints 5 (the center cell). You can use grid[row][col] anywhere a normal int would go: in math (grid[0][0] + grid[2][2]), in conditions (if (grid[1][1] == 5)), in function arguments. The bracket syntax is your tool for digging into ANY specific cell whenever you need it.
gui.tutorials.cArr2d.nested:
Click Run. Console:
1 2 3
4 5 6
7 8 9
Nine numbers in a tidy square. The nested loop printed each cell, and the printf("\n") after each row dropped to the next line. The output is a real 2D layout. Without the newline, all 9 cells would land on one long line.
gui.tutorials.cArr2d.recap: Tic-tac-toe (3×3 of chars: 'X', 'O', or ' '), Sudoku (9×9 of ints 0-9), a small picture (rows × cols of pixel colors), a school timetable (days × periods), a dungeon map (rows × cols of room types). Anywhere you have a grid of regular cells, 2D arrays are the natural fit. The nested for-loop pattern walks every cell. You now have the foundation for any grid-based program.
gui.tutorials.cArr.intro: Scratch had Lists — at the top of the Variables panel, you could click "Make a List" and create a list that holds many values. You could add items to it, look up item N, replace items, etc. C calls this same idea an ARRAY. Same fundamental concept: ONE name, many slots, each slot at a numbered position. Today: a list of five test scores in a single int array. By the end you'll know how to make an array, fill it up, read any slot, and change a slot's value.
gui.tutorials.cArr.declare: Line 4: int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88};. Three pieces. (1) int scores — an array of ints called scores. (2) [5] — the SIZE: this array has 5 slots. The size is fixed at creation; you can't grow or shrink it later. (3) = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88} — the initial values, separated by commas, surrounded by curly braces. Slot 0 gets 90, slot 1 gets 85, slot 2 gets 78, slot 3 gets 92, slot 4 gets 88. Like Scratch's "add 90 to [scores]" five times, but in one neat line.
gui.tutorials.cArr.indexFirst: Line 5: scores[0] — this picks the FIRST slot of the array. C counts from ZERO, not from 1. This is one of the biggest gotchas for kids coming from Scratch (where Lists are 1-indexed: item 1 is the first). In C, the first slot is index 0, the second is index 1, the third is index 2, and so on. So our 5-slot array has indexes 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — there is no index 5. Burn this into memory: arrays are zero-indexed in C.
gui.tutorials.cArr.indexLast: Line 6: scores[4] — the LAST slot of a 5-slot array. Count: slot 0, slot 1, slot 2, slot 3, slot 4. That's 5 slots, last index = size - 1 = 4. If you tried scores[5] (one past the end), C would NOT stop you at compile time — it would try to read whatever bits are in memory at that location, giving you garbage or crashing the program. This is one of the most dangerous bugs in C, called a "buffer overrun". Always: last index = size - 1.
gui.tutorials.cArr.update: Line 7: scores[0] = 100;. Same syntax as a normal variable assignment, just with the [index]. After this line, slot 0 holds 100 instead of the original 90. The other four slots are unchanged. Like Scratch's "replace item 1 of [scores] with [100]" — overwrite ONE slot, leave the rest alone. You can read OR write any slot using the [index] syntax — read by using it in an expression, write by putting it on the LEFT of an = sign.
gui.tutorials.cArr.run: Click Run. Console: First score: 90, Last score: 88, Updated first: 100. Five values stored in ONE variable, accessed by index. Next chapter we'll learn the most important array trick: walking through ALL the slots with a for loop. That combination — array plus for loop — is the foundation for almost everything you do with collections of data.
gui.tutorials.cBreakCont.intro: Sometimes inside a loop you want to STOP the whole loop EARLY (before the condition naturally becomes false), or SKIP the rest of the current pass and jump to the next one. C gives you two special keywords for these: break (exit the loop completely) and continue (skip to the next pass). Scratch had "stop this script" inside a forever block — that's like break. Today you'll see both used together: break out at i=5, and skip even numbers with continue.
gui.tutorials.cBreakCont.break: Lines 5 to 7: if (i == 5) { break; }. The moment i is 5, break fires and the WHOLE loop exits — the rest of the loop body is skipped, and so are all future iterations. Useful for "I found what I was looking for, no need to keep searching" — like checking 100 names for a match and stopping the moment you find one. Without break, you'd either keep looking after success or have to add complicated condition tracking.
gui.tutorials.cBreakCont.continue: Lines 8 to 10: if (i % 2 == 0) { continue; }. Remember % from the arithmetic chapter? i % 2 is 0 for even numbers and 1 for odd. So this if reads "if i is even". When continue fires, C SKIPS the rest of the loop body for THIS pass and jumps straight to the next iteration — the printf below NEVER runs for even values. Use continue when you want to SKIP certain inputs but keep going.
gui.tutorials.cBreakCont.run: Trace pass by pass. i=1: not 5, odd → print 1. i=2: not 5, even → continue (skip print). i=3: not 5, odd → print 3. i=4: not 5, even → continue. i=5: equals 5 → BREAK → loop ends. Click Run. The Console shows just two numbers: 1 and 3. break and continue together let you express "skip these, stop at that" in one short loop — a powerful pattern.
gui.tutorials.cConst.why: Some values in your program never change while it runs. Pi is always 3.14159..., the maximum number of lives in your game might always be 3, the speed limit on a road is always 60. These are CONSTANTS — values that should be set once and never modified. C lets you mark them as constant so that anyone (including future-you) can't accidentally change them. There are two ways to make constants in C: #define at the top of the file (a kind of search-and-replace) and const inside your function (a real variable that's locked). Today you'll see both. Using constants makes your code clearer (the reader sees PI not 3.14 — they immediately know what it means) and safer (if it can't change, it can't accidentally be wrong).
gui.tutorials.cConst.define: Lines 3 and 4 use #define — pronounced "hash define". This is a special instruction (note the # at the start, like #include). #define does a SEARCH-AND-REPLACE on your code BEFORE the compiler even reads it. Anywhere PI appears below, the compiler swaps in 3.14 first. Same for MAX_LIVES — anywhere it appears, the compiler swaps in 3. The convention in C is to use ALL CAPITAL LETTERS for #define names (like PI, MAX_LIVES, BUFFER_SIZE) so that anyone reading your code instantly recognizes them as constants and not regular variables. #define is great for constants you might use across multiple files (like a PI you share between math files). It's also great for "magic numbers" — instead of writing 3 in 20 places in your code, write MAX_LIVES once and use the name everywhere.
gui.tutorials.cConst.const: Line 7 uses const int — a different way to make a constant. Read it as: "constant integer named speed, value 60". It looks just like a regular variable declaration but with const stuck on the front. Unlike #define, const is a REAL variable — it has a type (int), takes memory, and you can pass it around. The "const" part just locks it: any attempt to change it later (like writing speed = 80; on the next line) would get refused by the compiler. Try it — add the line speed = 80; below line 7 and watch the compiler complain. const is preferred over #define when the constant only matters inside one function (like a local speed limit). Both ways work; pick whichever fits the situation.
gui.tutorials.cConst.run: Click Run. The Console shows three lines: PI = 3.14, Lives = 3, Speed = 60. Each printf used its constant the same way it would use a normal variable. The kid (or you in 6 months) reading this code can see PI, MAX_LIVES, and speed at a glance — much clearer than seeing 3.14, 3, and 60 scattered through the code with no explanation. The general rule: if a value is fixed and meaningful, give it a NAME. Constants make code self-documenting. Use #define for shared values across many files, use const for one-function locks.
gui.tutorials.cDoWhile.why: Both while and for check the condition BEFORE running the body for the first time. If the condition is already false at the start, the body NEVER RUNS. Sometimes you need the opposite: run the body at least ONCE, THEN check whether to do it again. That's where do-while comes in. The body runs first, the condition is checked AFTER. This shape fits situations like menus ("show the menu, read input, ask again if invalid") where the first action — showing the menu — must happen no matter what.
gui.tutorials.cDoWhile.loop: Lines 5 to 8 are the do-while loop. The shape: do { body; } while (condition); — note the SEMICOLON after the closing parenthesis on the while line. (Regular while loops don't have a semicolon there — only do-while does.) The body runs first, no matter what. Then the condition is checked. If true, jump back and do it again. If false, exit. Inside the body, n-- is critical — without it the loop would run forever, just like with regular while.
gui.tutorials.cDoWhile.run: Trace it: n=10, run body (print "n is 10", n becomes 9), check 9>5 true → loop again. Run body (print "n is 9", n becomes 8), check 8>5 true → again. ... Run body (print "n is 6", n becomes 5), check 5>5 FALSE → exit. Click Run. Console shows 10, 9, 8, 7, 6 — the body ran five times. Notice the body printed n BEFORE n was decremented — that's why we see 10 first, not 9.
gui.tutorials.cDoWhile.useCase: A real-world example: a menu. "Pick a number 1 to 9: " — show the menu, read input, check if it's valid; if not, ask again. The "show the menu" step has to happen at LEAST once before you can ask anything. With a regular while loop, you'd have to print the menu once before the loop AND inside the loop — repeating yourself. With do-while, the print is just inside the body and runs naturally on the first pass. Use do-while whenever the body MUST execute at least once. For everything else, regular while or for is more common.
gui.tutorials.cElseIf.why: if-else gave you a way to pick between TWO paths. But what if you need to pick between FOUR — Grade A, B, C, or F? Or seven — days of the week? Or ten — different shop items? You CHAIN if-elses together using the special form "else if". C tries each condition in order from top to bottom, and the FIRST one that's true wins — the rest are skipped completely. The final else (no condition) catches everything that didn't match any of the previous conditions. It's like a flowchart with multiple decision diamonds in a row.
gui.tutorials.cElseIf.first: Lines 5 to 7 check the first condition: score >= 90 → print "Grade: A". If score is 90 or higher, this prints A and the entire chain STOPS — none of the else if blocks are even checked. This is important to understand: ORDER MATTERS in an if-else if chain. C goes top-down and picks the first match. If you put score >= 60 first, then EVERY score 60+ would match (because 95 is also 60+) and you'd never reach the more-specific A or B checks. So always go from MOST SPECIFIC to LEAST SPECIFIC, top to bottom.
gui.tutorials.cElseIf.chain: Lines 7 to 11 chain three more checks: 75+ is B, 60+ is C, anything below those is F (the final else). Notice the structure: } else if (...) { ... } — the else and the if are joined together. You can chain as many else if blocks as you want — five, ten, twenty. After all the else ifs, the final else (no condition) is the catch-all that runs only when NONE of the previous conditions matched. Exactly ONE branch always runs — same guarantee as plain if-else, just with more options.
gui.tutorials.cElseIf.run: Score is 75. Trace it: 75 >= 90? NO. 75 >= 75? YES. So the second branch wins → "Grade: B" prints, and C skips the rest. Click Run to confirm. Now experiment: change score to 95 (should be A), then 65 (should be C), then 40 (should be F). Each different score triggers a different branch. This pattern — chaining checks from highest to lowest — is one of the most common shapes in real-world programs (grading systems, age groups, traffic light colors, anything with categories).
gui.tutorials.cElseIf.order: Imagine you accidentally put score >= 60 FIRST instead of score >= 90. What would happen? Every score 60 or higher would match the first condition and print "Grade: C" — even a 95 would print C, because C never gets to check the higher-grade conditions after the first match wins. So a top scorer would get a C. Bug! The fix is always: put your STRICTEST condition first (>= 90), then loosen as you go down. This is the most common bug in else-if chains. Read your conditions from top to bottom and ask: "is each one stricter than the next?" If not, reorder.
gui.tutorials.cFor.intro: Last chapter you used while to count 1 to 5. It worked, but it took 4 separate lines: declare counter, while-condition, body, count++. The for loop bundles all that bookkeeping into ONE line. Scratch's "repeat (10)" block is the closest match — it counts a fixed number of times and runs its body each time. while is great for "repeat UNTIL something happens" (unknown count), for is great for "repeat exactly N times" (known count). For most simple counting jobs, for is the right choice.
gui.tutorials.cFor.threePieces: Look at line 4: for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++). Inside the parentheses are THREE pieces separated by SEMICOLONS. (1) START: int i = 1; — declare and initialize the counter, runs ONCE before the loop begins. (2) KEEP-GOING-WHILE: i <= 5; — checked BEFORE each pass; if true, body runs; if false, loop exits. (3) AFTER-EACH-PASS: i++ — runs at the END of each pass, before checking the condition again. All three pieces of loop bookkeeping in one line. The semicolons between the pieces are required (the third piece has no semicolon after it because it's the last).
gui.tutorials.cFor.body: Line 5 is the body of the for loop — printf("Step %d\n", i);. The body runs once PER PASS, with i changing each time. Same idea as the SUBSTACK slot inside Scratch's "repeat" block. The braces { } around the body work the same way as in if-else and while — they group "the stuff to do each pass". You can put as many lines inside the braces as you want.
gui.tutorials.cFor.loop: Trace the whole loop step by step. Pass 1: i=1, 1<=5 true, print "Step 1", i becomes 2. Pass 2: i=2, 2<=5 true, print "Step 2", i becomes 3. Pass 3: i=3, print "Step 3", i becomes 4. Pass 4: i=4, print "Step 4", i becomes 5. Pass 5: i=5, 5<=5 true, print "Step 5", i becomes 6. Check 6<=5 → false → exit loop, jump to Done!. Five prints total, then the final Done! line. The for loop did the same job as last chapter's while in fewer lines.
gui.tutorials.cFor.run: Click Run. Console: Step 1, Step 2, Step 3, Step 4, Step 5, Done!. Try changing 5 to 10 and Run — 10 steps. Try i = 0; i < 5; — that counts 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 (still 5 passes, but starting at 0 — the most common style in real C code, especially with arrays which are also 0-indexed). Notice the < instead of <= because you want it to stop AT 5, not include 5. Whether you start at 0 or 1 is a STYLE choice — both work — but the C tradition is to start at 0.
gui.tutorials.cFor.play: Change the for to: for (int i = 5; i >= 1; i--). Three changes: start at 5 (not 1), keep going while i is at LEAST 1 (not at MOST 5), and SUBTRACT 1 each pass (not add). Run. Console: Step 5, Step 4, Step 3, Step 2, Step 1, Done!. Same loop structure, opposite direction. for loops can count UP, DOWN, by 2s (i += 2), by 10s, in any pattern — you control the three pieces.
gui.tutorials.cFuncP.why: Last chapter's greet() always said the same thing — "Hello, friend!". What if you wanted to greet Alice, then Bob, then Charlie — each by name? You could define three separate functions, but that's repetitive. The right way is to make ONE function that takes the name as an INPUT — then call it with different names. In Scratch, "Make a Block" let you add input slots to your custom blocks (number, string, boolean) — same idea here. C functions take inputs by listing them in the parentheses; those inputs are called PARAMETERS.
gui.tutorials.cFuncP.param: Lines 3 to 5: void greet(char name[]) { printf("Hello, %s!", name); }. The new piece is char name[] inside the parentheses — that's a PARAMETER. char name[] means "a string variable named name". The square brackets [] are part of how C declares string parameters — for now just remember the shape. Inside the function, name acts like a local variable that holds whatever the caller passed in. The %s in the printf is the format code for STRINGS (like %d for int and %c for char). It plugs the name into the message.
gui.tutorials.cFuncP.callTwice: Lines 8 and 9: greet("Alice"); and greet("Bob");. Same function, different inputs. The string in the parentheses is the ARGUMENT — the actual value being passed to the function. Inside greet, the parameter name takes on that value (first "Alice", then "Bob"). Notice the strings are in DOUBLE quotes — that's the C convention for strings (single quotes for char). So one function, two different outputs because the caller controls the input.
gui.tutorials.cFuncP.run: Click Run. Console: "Hello, Alice!" then "Hello, Bob!". The function is a TEMPLATE; the call fills in the blank. Add a third call: greet("Charlie"); below the first two. Run. Three lines now. You can have hundreds of callers, each with a different argument. This is why functions are so powerful — they make ONE chunk of code reusable in MANY situations.
gui.tutorials.cFuncR.why: So far our functions just DID things — printed messages — but didn't hand back any answer. Sometimes you want a function to compute something and HAND BACK the result so the caller can use it. Think of Scratch's "reporter blocks" — the round green blocks that GIVE BACK a value, like ((x) + (y)) or (sqrt of (9)). You can't just stand them on the script area; you have to slot them INTO another block. Same idea in C: a function that returns a value can be used wherever a value would normally fit — in math, in printf, anywhere.
gui.tutorials.cFuncR.return: Lines 3 to 5: int square(int n) { return n * n; }. Two big changes from last chapter's function. (1) The first int means "this function RETURNS an int" — it gives back a whole number when it finishes. Last chapter we used void (returns nothing); now we use int (returns an int). (2) Inside the body, return n * n; — return is the magic keyword. It calculates n times n and HANDS THAT VALUE back to whoever called square. The function exits the moment return runs.
gui.tutorials.cFuncR.call: Line 8: int result = square(5); — this is where the return value gets CAUGHT. C reads it as: "call square with 5, get back the answer, store it in result". square(5) computes 5 * 5 = 25, returns 25, the = stores 25 into result. Now the function call square(5) is acting like an EXPRESSION — like the number 25 itself. You can use a returning-function-call anywhere you'd use a number: in math, in conditions, in printf, in other function calls.
gui.tutorials.cFuncR.run: Click Run. Console: "5 squared is 25". Now try CHAINING: replace the printf with printf("Sum of squares: %d\n", square(7) + square(3));. square(7) returns 49, square(3) returns 9, the sum is 58. Reporter-block-style chaining works in C the same way it did in Scratch — function calls plug into bigger expressions.
gui.tutorials.cFunc.why: Until now everything you wrote lived inside main. That's fine for tiny programs but real programs are hundreds or thousands of lines — main would be unreadable. The fix is to FACTOR chunks of code out into named units called FUNCTIONS. Once a function is defined, you call its name whenever you need that chunk to run. Scratch had this exact feature: "Make a Block" in the My Blocks tab. You defined a custom block (define [my-block]), then dragged that block into your scripts whenever you wanted that code to run. C functions are the same idea, just typed.
gui.tutorials.cFunc.define: Lines 3 to 5 define a function called greet: void greet() { printf("Hello, friend!"); }. Three pieces. (1) void means "this function returns NOTHING" — it just does stuff, doesn't hand back an answer (more on returning values in two chapters). (2) greet() is the NAME with empty parentheses — empty because greet takes no inputs. (3) The body is between curly braces, and contains the code that runs when greet is called. The function is defined ABOVE main so that main can SEE it (C reads top to bottom — you must define before you use).
gui.tutorials.cFunc.call: Line 8: greet(); — that's a function CALL. Just the name and parentheses, with a semicolon. When C reaches this line, it JUMPS up to greet's body, runs everything inside, then comes back down here and continues. Like dragging a custom block onto your script in Scratch. The empty parentheses are required even for no-input calls — it's how C knows you mean "call greet" instead of just "the value of greet".
gui.tutorials.cFunc.run: Click Run. Console: "Hello, friend!". The flow: program starts, hits main, hits greet(), jumps up to greet's body, runs the printf, returns to main, hits return 0, ends. Same output as if you'd just put the printf directly in main. So WHY use a function for one printf? Because right now you can't see the win. The win comes when you call the function MORE THAN ONCE — coming up next.
gui.tutorials.cFunc.callMany: Add two more greet(); lines below the first — three total calls. Run. Console shows "Hello, friend!" three times. Now the win is obvious: you wrote the printf ONCE inside greet, but it printed THREE times because you called greet three times. Imagine if greet had ten lines of complicated code — without the function you'd have to copy all ten lines three times. With the function, you change greet ONCE and all three callers update automatically. This is the superpower of functions — write code in one place, use it many places.
gui.tutorials.cGuess.plan: A classic game: the computer picks a SECRET number between 1 and 100. You guess a number; the computer says "Higher!" or "Lower!" depending on whether your guess is too low or too high. You keep guessing until you get it right. The game uses lots of what you've learned: a while loop (until correct), if-else (give the right hint), scanf (read your guess), and a NEW thing — random numbers. By the end you'll have a real game that's actually fun to play.
gui.tutorials.cGuess.includes: Lines 1 to 3: three different toolboxes. stdio.h for printf and scanf (familiar). stdlib.h for the rand() function (random number generator). time.h for the time() function (current time, used as a seed). Why two extra includes? Because computers don't actually have "true" randomness — they use a formula that LOOKS random. If we don't SEED the formula with a different starting value each run, every "random" pick would be the same number every time. time(NULL) gives the current time as a seed, so each run picks differently.
gui.tutorials.cGuess.getSecret: Lines 6 and 7: srand(time(NULL)); seeds the random generator (do this ONCE near the start of the program). int secret = rand() % 100 + 1; picks a random number. rand() returns a big random int (0 to about 2 billion). rand() % 100 gives the remainder when divided by 100, so the result is 0 to 99. Adding 1 shifts the range to 1 to 100 (the inclusive range we want). The kid never sees this number until they guess it.
gui.tutorials.cGuess.readGuess: Lines 9 to 13: while (guess != secret). The != means "not equal". So this reads as "while the guess is NOT yet the secret, keep going". Inside the loop: prompt for a guess, read it with scanf, then give a hint with if-else. The loop runs until guess matches secret — could be 3 tries or 30, the loop adapts. (This is a great example of when to use while instead of for: you don't know the count upfront.)
gui.tutorials.cGuess.giveHint: Lines 12 and 13: if guess < secret → tell kid "Higher!" (your guess was too low, aim higher). else if guess > secret → tell kid "Lower!" (your guess was too high, aim lower). Notice there's NO else for the equal case — because if guess equals secret, the while condition becomes false and the loop just ends naturally. Less code that way.
gui.tutorials.cGuess.setup: Line 8: int guess = 0; — set guess to 0 BEFORE the while loop. Why 0? Because the while needs something to check on the very first pass. Setting guess to 0 ensures the loop runs at least once (since secret is 1-100, never 0, so 0 != secret is always true). Any starting value other than a possible secret would work — we picked 0 because it's outside the secret range and easy to see at a glance.
gui.tutorials.cGuess.while: A short philosophy break. for is great when you know the count up front — "loop exactly 5 times". while wins when you DON'T — "loop until something happens, no idea how long it'll take". This game might end in 1 try (lucky guess) or 50 (very unlucky). while fits this kind of unknown-length situation perfectly. Pick the loop type that matches the problem: for for fixed counts, while for unknown counts.
gui.tutorials.cGuess.body: Say secret is 42 and you guess 50. The loop checks 50 != 42 → true → enter the body. printf shows the prompt. scanf reads 50 into guess (well, was already 50). 50 < 42? No. 50 > 42? Yes → print "Lower!". Loop body ends, jump back to the while check. 50 != 42 still true → loop again. You type 30. 30 < 42? Yes → "Higher!". Loop again. You type 42. 42 != 42 → FALSE → exit the loop.
gui.tutorials.cGuess.won: Line 15: printf("You got it!\n"); — placed AFTER the while loop's closing brace. This line runs ONCE, after the loop ends. Since the loop only ends when guess == secret, this is always the right moment to celebrate. Code OUTSIDE the loop runs once; code INSIDE runs every pass. Knowing the difference is a key skill for designing good loops.
gui.tutorials.cGuess.play: Click Run. Console: "Guess (1-100): ". Click in the Console, type a number (50 is a smart first guess — splits the range in half), and press Enter. The hint tells you to go higher or lower. Use the hint to BISECT: if "Higher", try 75 (halfway between 50 and 100). If "Lower", try 25. Keep bisecting and you'll always win in 7 tries or fewer (this is called binary search — a very famous algorithm).
gui.tutorials.cHello.openEditor: Click the C++ Editor card. As soon as you do, the editor opens with a starter program already typed for you — six short lines. We are going to read EACH line together before clicking Run, so you understand what every piece does. This first program is called "Hello, World" and it's the very first program every programmer writes in every language. The job is super simple: print the words Hello, world! to the screen. By the end of this chapter you will know what those six lines mean, you will have run them, and you will have changed the message to your own.
gui.tutorials.cHello.workspace: Take a moment to look around. The big white panel in the MIDDLE is the editor — that is where C code lives. It's like the script area in Scratch, except instead of dropping blocks you type letters. On the RIGHT side you can see two tabs called Log and Console. The Log tab is where the COMPILER talks to you — it shows messages like "compiling..." or "build successful". The Console tab is where YOUR PROGRAM talks — anything your program prints appears there. Think of the Console as the C version of Scratch's Stage. The Run button is at the top right with a triangle icon — that's what you click to make your program go.
gui.tutorials.cHello.include: Line 1 says #include <stdio.h>. Let's break it down. The word "include" means "bring in this toolbox". The thing in the brackets — stdio.h — is the name of the toolbox. stdio is short for STandard Input Output, and the .h means "header file". So the whole line is saying: "I am going to use the standard tools for printing and reading, please bring them in for me." In Scratch, all the blocks were already there in the panel on the left — you didn't have to ask. C is fussier: you have to ASK for the tools you want to use. Without this line, the printf below wouldn't exist and the compiler would shout at you. The hash sign # at the start tells the compiler "do this BEFORE you compile the rest" — it's a special instruction. You'll see #include at the top of nearly every C program you ever read.
gui.tutorials.cHello.main: Lines 3 to 6 contain the most important block of any C program: int main() { ... }. Let's decode each piece. The word int means "this section will give back a whole number when it finishes" — we'll see why in a moment. The word main is the name of this section, and it is special: every C program has a main, and that's where the program starts running. The empty parentheses ( ) are where INPUTS would go — main usually takes none, so they are empty. The curly braces { and } mark the START and END of the section's body. Everything between them runs in order, top to bottom, the moment the program starts. If you remember Scratch's "when green flag clicked" hat block, main is C's version of that hat block — the entry point. The two lines inside (lines 4 and 5) are what main does: print a message, then return 0.
gui.tutorials.cHello.printf:
Line 4 is the line that does the actual work: printf("Hello, world!
"); — say that out loud as "print f, open quote, Hello comma world exclamation, backslash n, close quote, semicolon". printf is the C function for printing text. The f at the end means "formatted" — we'll explore the formatting features in later chapters. Whatever you put inside the QUOTES is what gets printed to the Console. The strange-looking 
 at the end is a special code that means NEW LINE — like pressing the Enter key on a keyboard. Without 
, the next thing printed would land right after the exclamation mark on the same line. The semicolon ; at the very end is super important — every statement in C ends with one. If you forget the semicolon, the compiler will refuse to build your program and complain. In Scratch you used a "say [Hello!]" block to show a message — printf is C's version of say.
gui.tutorials.cHello.return: Line 5 is return 0; — this tells the operating system "I am finished, and everything went well." The number 0 means OK. Any other number signals that something went wrong (1 might mean "file not found", 2 might mean "out of memory", etc. — different programs use different numbers). Most of the time, your main will end with return 0; — that is the standard "all good" signal. Remember from line 3 we said int main() means main GIVES BACK a whole number? This line is what gives it back. Without this line, the compiler would warn you. Always end your main with return 0; until you have a reason to do otherwise.
gui.tutorials.cHello.runFirstTime: Time to make it actually run. Click the Run button — it's the play-style triangle icon at the top right. The very first time you click Run in a session, it takes about 45 seconds because CodeSkool is downloading the C compiler in the background. While you wait, watch the Log tab on the right — you will see a sequence of messages: first "downloading...", then "compiling...", then "running" or "ready". Every Run after this first one is instant — the compiler stays loaded for the rest of the session. So if this first run feels slow, that's totally normal — wait it out. If you ever see RED text in the Log tab, that means a compile error (usually a missing semicolon or a typo). Don't panic — read the error, fix the line it points to, and run again.
gui.tutorials.cHello.console: Once Run finishes, click the Console tab on the right. There it is — Hello, world! printed by your very own C program. That's exactly the same thing you would see if you ran a C program in a black command-line window on a real computer. You just wrote, compiled, and ran your first C program! Now try this: change the text inside the quotes on line 4 — make it say something else, like "Hello, [your name]!" — and click Run again. The Console will show your new message. This is the same loop you used in Scratch (edit the script, click the flag, watch what happens) — just typed instead of dragged. Get comfortable with that loop because every chapter from here on uses it.
gui.tutorials.cIfElse.intro: In Scratch, the "if-then-else" block was a C-shaped block with TWO slots inside — one for "then do this" and one for "else do this". A hexagon-shaped condition slotted into the top. C has the exact same idea, just typed differently: if (...) { ... } else { ... }. The hexagon condition becomes round PARENTHESES. The two SUBSTACK slots become two pairs of CURLY BRACES. The logic is identical — based on a true/false test, run one of two paths. Today you'll write your first if-else in C, then test it with different values to see both branches fire.
gui.tutorials.cIfElse.age: Line 4 is just int age = 12; — a regular int variable holding 12. The next lines will check this variable against the threshold for being a teenager (13). The whole point of if-else is to make different things happen based on the VALUE of a variable — without if-else your program would do exactly the same thing every time, no matter what.
gui.tutorials.cIfElse.if: Lines 5 to 7 are the if block: if (age >= 13) { printf("You are a teenager."); }. Three pieces. (1) The keyword if. (2) The CONDITION inside parentheses: age >= 13 means "age greater than or equal to 13". (3) The BODY inside curly braces: the code that runs when the condition is true. The braces { } work the same way as Scratch's SUBSTACK slot — they hold "the stuff to do when the condition matches". You can put as many statements inside the braces as you want.
gui.tutorials.cIfElse.else: Lines 7 to 9 are the else block: } else { printf("Not a teenager yet."); }. The else block runs when the if condition is FALSE. EXACTLY ONE of the two branches will run — never both, never neither. This is the key superpower of if-else: it picks one path. Without an else, you only have an "if" that may or may not run; with else, you guarantee that one branch always runs. Use else when you want a fallback for "what to do if the condition wasn't met".
gui.tutorials.cIfElse.run: Before clicking Run, predict in your head which branch fires. age is 12. The condition is age >= 13. Is 12 greater than or equal to 13? NO. So the if condition is FALSE, and the else branch runs. Click Run. The Console shows "Not a teenager yet." Now change age to 15 and Run again. This time 15 >= 13 is TRUE, so the if branch wins and the Console shows "You are a teenager." Try age = 13 too (right at the boundary — the >= includes 13).
gui.tutorials.cIfElse.compare: Here are all the C comparison operators you'll use in conditions: < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less than or equal), >= (greater than or equal), == (EQUAL — note TWO equals signs), != (NOT equal). The double-equals == catches everyone at least once. SINGLE equals = means assignment ("set this variable to that value"); DOUBLE equals == means comparison ("are these two values the same?"). Mixing them up is the most common bug in all of C. If you write if (age = 13), C will SET age to 13 and the if always runs (because 13 is treated as "true"). You almost certainly meant if (age == 13). Triple-check this every single time.
gui.tutorials.cInput.intro: Until now your programs only TALKED — printf shouted things at the Console and that was it. Real programs also LISTEN — they take input from the kid using them. In Scratch, the "ask [What's your name?] and wait" block did exactly this: the program would pause, the kid would type an answer, and the answer block would hold what they typed. C's version of "ask" is called scanf (pronounced "scan-eff"). It's a bit fussier than Scratch — you must say WHAT TYPE you're asking for (a number? a letter? a whole sentence?) — but the idea is the same: pause the program, let the kid type, save what they typed into a variable. Today you'll write your first interactive program.
gui.tutorials.cInput.declare: Look at line 4: int age; — note that there is NO equals sign and no value. We're creating the variable but NOT putting anything in it yet. In the previous chapters you always wrote int age = 12; with a starting value. This time we're leaving it empty because the kid is going to fill it in when they type. The compiler still needs to know it exists and what type it is, but the actual number gets filled in by scanf later. (Note: until scanf runs, the variable holds GARBAGE — whatever random bits were in that memory. So don't print it before reading.)
gui.tutorials.cInput.prompt:
Line 5 is your normal printf: printf("How old are you? "); — but notice there is NO 
 at the end. That is on purpose. By skipping the 
, the cursor stays on the SAME LINE as the question — so when the kid types their answer, it appears right after the question mark, like a real form. It would look wrong if the question was on one line and their typed answer was on the next. Without the 
: "How old are you? 12". With the 
: "How old are you?" then a new line and "12" awkwardly below. This little detail makes interactive programs feel polished.
