gui.tutorials.cInput.scanf: Line 6 is the new magic: scanf("%d", &age); — three things to learn here. (1) "%d" tells scanf "expect a whole number" — same %d we use in printf, but here it means "read this type". (2) &age (with the AMPERSAND in front of age) tells scanf "put the number into the box at the address of age". The & means "address of" — it gives scanf the LOCATION of the variable, not its current value. (3) Forgetting the & is the most common scanf bug ever. Without &, your program will crash when it runs. Always: variable in printf, &variable in scanf. It feels weird at first but you'll get used to it.
gui.tutorials.cInput.run: Click Run. The Console shows the question "How old are you? " and then sits there waiting. Click inside the Console (so it has focus), type a number (like 12 or 100), and press Enter. The program wakes up — scanf reads what you typed, puts it into age, and the next printf shows it back: "You are 12 years old." This is your first INTERACTIVE C program. Try running it again with different numbers — the printf reflects whatever you type. If you type something that's NOT a number (like the word "twelve"), scanf gets confused and the printf shows garbage. We'll cover handling bad input later; for now just type a number.
gui.tutorials.cLogic.intro: So far your conditions have been simple — one comparison at a time. But real-world rules are often "BOTH this AND that" or "EITHER this OR that". Scratch had three green hexagon blocks for combining conditions: "and", "or", and "not". C uses SYMBOLS instead: && (and), || (or), ! (not). Same job, shorter to type. Today you'll see all three on a movie-theater example: must be 13+ AND have a ticket; under 5 OR over 65 = free; if NOT have a ticket, buy one first.
gui.tutorials.cLogic.and: Lines 6 to 8 use && which means AND: if (age >= 13 && hasTicket). For the IF to fire, BOTH halves must be true. age is 15, so age >= 13 is true. hasTicket is 1, which counts as true (in C, 0 means false; anything else means true). Both true → the print runs and "You can enter." appears. If EITHER half were false, the print would be skipped. Use && when you need EVERY rule to be satisfied at once. Read && out loud as "and" — it makes the code easier to follow.
gui.tutorials.cLogic.or: Lines 9 to 11 use || which means OR: if (age < 5 || age > 65). For the IF to fire, AT LEAST ONE half must be true. age is 15. age < 5? No. age > 65? No. Both false → the print is SKIPPED, "Free entry." does not appear. Try changing age to 3 — then age < 5 is true, so the OR is true, and the print fires. The two pipes || are typed by holding Shift and pressing the \ key (above Enter on most keyboards). Read || out loud as "or".
gui.tutorials.cLogic.not: Lines 12 to 14 use ! which means NOT — it FLIPS true to false and false to true: if (!hasTicket). hasTicket is 1 (true), so !hasTicket becomes false → the print is skipped. If you change hasTicket to 0 (no ticket), then !hasTicket becomes true → the print fires. Use ! when you want to act on the OPPOSITE of a condition. A common pattern: if (!isLoggedIn) { ask them to log in; }. Read ! as "not" — !x is "not x".
gui.tutorials.cLogic.run: Trace each line in your head before clicking. age=15, hasTicket=1. (1) && line: 15 >= 13 true AND 1 is true → print runs ("You can enter."). (2) || line: 15 < 5 false OR 15 > 65 false → both false → silent. (3) ! line: !1 is false → silent. So only ONE line should print. Click Run to confirm. Now change hasTicket to 0 and Run again — different lines fire. Try age = 3 with hasTicket = 1, then age = 70 with hasTicket = 1. Each combination triggers a different mix of branches.
gui.tutorials.cMathLib.intro: Scratch had a green Operators block with a dropdown — you could pick sqrt, sin, cos, log, abs, and more. C has the same set of math functions, but they're in a separate TOOLBOX (LIBRARY) called math.h. Just like #include <stdio.h> brings in printf and scanf, #include <math.h> brings in sqrt, pow, sin, cos, and friends. Today you'll add the math include, then use the three most common math functions: sqrt (square root), pow (raise to a power), and abs (absolute value).
gui.tutorials.cMathLib.include: Line 2: #include <math.h> — same shape as #include <stdio.h>, just a different header. Without this line, sqrt and pow below would be unknown to the compiler and you'd get a "function not declared" error. The .h files are HEADER FILES — they tell the compiler "these functions exist, here's how to call them". Most programs only #include what they need — fewer headers = faster compile times.
gui.tutorials.cMathLib.sqrt: Line 6: sqrt(x) where x is 16.0 — returns 4.0 (because 4 × 4 = 16). sqrt takes a double (decimal number) and returns a double. Print with %.2f to show 2 decimals — sqrt(16) = 4.00. Notice we declared x as double (16.0 not 16) — math functions want decimals, not whole numbers. If you passed an int, the compiler would silently convert it, but using doubles is the cleanest style.
gui.tutorials.cMathLib.pow: Line 7: pow(2, 8) — pow takes TWO arguments: the base and the exponent. pow(2, 8) means "2 to the power of 8" = 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 = 256. The result is a double. We use %.0f to show zero decimals (256 not 256.000000 — looks tidier). Try pow(10, 3) (1000), pow(2, 16) (65536), pow(3, 5) (243).
gui.tutorials.cMathLib.run: Click Run. Console: sqrt(16) = 4.00, pow(2, 8) = 256, abs(-5) = 5. abs is from stdlib (already included via stdio in this case) and works on int — returns the positive version of any number. Other math.h goodies you might explore: sin(angle), cos(angle), log(x) (natural log), log10(x) (base-10 log), floor(x) (round down), ceil(x) (round up), round(x). All take and return doubles.
gui.tutorials.cCalc.plan: Time to combine many things you've learned into one real program: a calculator that reads a math expression like "5 + 3" or "10 / 4" and prints the answer. You'll use variables (multiple types), scanf to read input, char to hold the operator, an else-if chain to pick the operation, and printf to format the output. By the end, in 14 lines of C, you'll have a working calculator that handles +, -, *, /, AND gracefully refuses unknown operators. Real software in 14 lines.
gui.tutorials.cCalc.vars: Lines 4 and 5: double a, b, result; on one line declares THREE variables of the same type — saves typing. Then char op; for the operator. Why doubles instead of ints? Because we want 10 / 4 to give 2.5, not 2 (remember the integer-division trap from the arithmetic chapter). char op holds the operator the kid types: +, -, *, or /. Putting all the variables together at the top makes the function easy to read.
gui.tutorials.cCalc.first: Lines 6 and 7: printf the prompt (no \n so the kid types right after), then scanf("%lf %c %lf", &a, &op, &b); to read three pieces in ONE call. %lf means "long float" = double (the format code for doubles is %lf, slightly different from %f for plain float). %c reads one character. The SPACES inside the format string skip whitespace, so the kid can type "5+3" or "5 + 3" or "5  +  3" — all work the same. scanf needs & in front of each variable (remember from the input chapter).
gui.tutorials.cCalc.op: Lines 8 to 12: an else-if chain that compares op to each of the four operator characters ('+', '-', '*', '/'). Match → do the right math, store in result. Notice the single quotes around the chars ('+' is a char, "+" would be a string). The final else handles the case where the kid types something weird (% or @ or #) — print an error and return 1 (non-zero exit code = "something went wrong", a real-world convention).
gui.tutorials.cCalc.second: Line 13: printf("%.2f %c %.2f = %.2f\n", a, op, b, result); — print the whole expression in nice format. %.2f shows 2 decimals (so 5 prints as 5.00, 12.5 as 12.50). %c prints the operator. Four format codes, four arguments — they line up in order. Example output: "5.00 + 3.00 = 8.00".
gui.tutorials.cCalc.checkpoint: Before you click Run, predict the flow: you'll type three things into the Console (a number, an operator character, another number, all separated by spaces or stuck together). The program reads all three at once, picks the right branch with else-if, computes the answer, prints it formatted. We'll try +, -, *, /, and one bad operator to see all paths fire.
gui.tutorials.cCalc.plus: Click Run. The Console shows the prompt and waits. Click in the Console area, type: 5 + 3, then press Enter. The program reads it, picks the + branch, computes 5 + 3 = 8, and prints: 5.00 + 3.00 = 8.00. The doubles printed with two decimals each, the operator printed in the middle. Your first calculator operation works.
gui.tutorials.cCalc.minusTimes: Run again, type: 10 - 7. The - branch fires, output: 10.00 - 7.00 = 3.00. Run again, type: 6 * 4. The * branch fires, output: 6.00 * 4.00 = 24.00. Same code, three different operations — the else-if chain picked the right one each time.
gui.tutorials.cCalc.divide: Run again, type: 10 / 4. Output: 10.00 / 4.00 = 2.50. NOT 2! Because a and b are doubles, the division KEEPS the decimals. If they were ints, you'd lose the .5 (we covered that trap in the arithmetic chapter). Picking the right type matters — when decimals matter, use double or float.
gui.tutorials.cCalc.fallback: Run again, type: 5 % 3 (using percent, which we don't support). Output: "Bad op". The final else caught the unknown operator. The program then exits with return 1 (signaling failure). Always handle the "weird input" case — real software does this constantly. Without the else, op would just keep its old value (or garbage) and the printf would show nonsense.
gui.tutorials.cCalc.run: 14 lines, 4 operations, error handling, formatted output. Every concept from the last 13 chapters — variables, types (double, char), scanf, if-else-if, printf with multiple format codes — combined into one useful program. This is what programming is: combining small concepts into bigger usable things. The next two chapters (guessing game, quiz game) push that further.
gui.tutorials.cNested.intro: Sometimes you need a loop INSIDE another loop. Think of a grid of cells: the OUTER loop counts the rows, and INSIDE each row the INNER loop counts the columns. This pattern shows up everywhere: tables, grids, multiplication times-tables, tic-tac-toe boards, calendar layouts, pixels in an image. The shape is always the same — outer loop runs N times, and each pass of the outer loop runs the inner loop M times. So total = N × M body executions.
gui.tutorials.cNested.outer: Line 4: for (int row = 1; row <= 3; row++). The OUTER for loops three times — once per row (rows 1, 2, 3). Each pass of the outer loop runs the entire INNER loop from start to finish. So if the inner loop runs 3 times per outer pass, that's 3 outer × 3 inner = 9 total executions of the innermost body. Different counter names (row, col) make it crystal clear what each loop is counting.
gui.tutorials.cNested.inner: Lines 5 to 7 are the inner for loop and its body. for (int col = 1; col <= 3; col++) { print (row, col) }. The inner loop runs FROM SCRATCH every time the outer loops — col starts at 1 each time, counts up to 3, exits. Inside the inner body, the printf has access to BOTH row (from the outer loop) and col (from the inner loop) — they print together as (1,1), (1,2), (1,3), (2,1), (2,2), (2,3), (3,1), (3,2), (3,3).
gui.tutorials.cNested.newline: Line 8: printf("\n"); — placed AFTER the inner loop's closing brace but INSIDE the outer loop. So after the inner loop finishes printing all three columns of one row, this printf drops the cursor to a fresh line, ready for the next row to print below. Without this line, all 9 cells would land on ONE long line — try it (delete line 8 and run). The newline is the small thing that turns a flat list into a 2D grid.
gui.tutorials.cNested.run:
Click Run. Console:
(1,1) (1,2) (1,3)
(2,1) (2,2) (2,3)
(3,1) (3,2) (3,3)
Three rows, three columns each. The outer loop counted rows 1-3; for each row, the inner loop printed columns 1-3, then the newline dropped to the next row. This nested-loop pattern is the foundation for working with 2D arrays (grids of data) — you'll see it again very soon.
gui.tutorials.cShort.why: In Scratch, the "change [score] by 1" block was a single drag — quick, easy. The long way in C would be: score = score + 1; — that works but it's ugly when you do it dozens of times. C gives you SHORTCUTS that mean exactly the same thing in fewer characters. The shortcuts are: += (add and store), -= (subtract and store), *= (multiply and store), /= (divide and store), ++ (add 1), -- (subtract 1). Today you'll see all four of the most common ones used on a single score variable. Once you learn these, you'll see them in pretty much every C program you ever read.
gui.tutorials.cShort.plusEq: Line 5: score += 5; — this is exactly the same as writing score = score + 5; just shorter. Read it as "increase score by 5" or "score plus-equals 5". After this line runs, score holds 15 (it was 10, plus 5). The same pattern works for any operation: -= subtracts, *= multiplies, /= divides. So count *= 2; doubles count, total /= 4; quarters total, etc. Use these shortcuts everywhere — they're standard C style and other programmers expect to see them.
gui.tutorials.cShort.minusEq: Line 7: score -= 2; — same shape as +=, opposite direction. score was 15, subtract 2, now it's 13. In Scratch you'd use "change [lives] by -1" when the player lost a life — same idea. Useful for scoring penalties, countdowns, paying for items in a game shop, anywhere a number needs to drop.
gui.tutorials.cShort.plusPlus: Line 9: score++; — adds exactly 1 to score. This shortcut is so famous that the language C++ is named after it (C++ is "C, with one added"). Why have a special shortcut just for +1? Because adding 1 happens all the time in loops — every time around a loop, you usually bump a counter by 1. score++ is shorter than score += 1, which is shorter than score = score + 1. They all do the same thing. After this line, score is 14. There is also score++ vs ++score (with the ++ in front) — they have a subtle difference, but for now think of them as the same. We'll dig in later if needed.
gui.tutorials.cShort.minusMinus: Line 11: score--; — subtracts exactly 1 from score. Same shape as ++, opposite direction. After this line, score is 13. Common use: lives--, time--, ammo-- in a game; or counting down in a loop ("for i from 10 down to 1, do something"). Together with ++, this pair (++ and --) is one of the most-typed pieces of code in all of C.
gui.tutorials.cShort.run: Trace it in your head before clicking: score starts at 10. After +=5 it's 15. After -=2 it's 13. After ++ it's 14. After -- it's 13 again. Click Run. The Console should show exactly that sequence: 15, 13, 14, 13. Each printf shows the value AT THAT MOMENT. These four shortcuts are everywhere in real C code — get comfortable with all of them.
gui.tutorials.cPtr.concept: Every variable you make in C lives somewhere in the computer's memory. Each "somewhere" has a unique address — like a house number on a street. A POINTER is a special kind of variable that holds an address instead of a value. Think of it as an arrow that points AT another variable's box. Pointers sound scary at first, but they unlock two superpowers: a function can CHANGE a variable from outside (like scanf does with &age — yes, you've been using pointers without realizing!), and your program can work with arrays and strings efficiently. Today: meet pointers, learn the two operators (& for "address of" and * for "value at"), and use a function that changes a number from the outside.
gui.tutorials.cPtr.addressOf: Look at lines 8 and 10. Line 8: int x = 10; — a regular int variable. Line 10: printf("x lives at %p\n", (void*)&x); — the &x is "the ADDRESS of x". %p is the format code for printing addresses (p for pointer). The (void*) cast just tells printf "this address is not tied to any specific type, just print it". When you Run, you'll see something like 0x7ffe... — that's a hexadecimal number, the actual location of x in memory. The exact value changes each run; that's normal. The point is: every variable has one.
gui.tutorials.cPtr.declare: Line 9: int *p = &x; — let's break this down piece by piece. (1) int * means "this is a pointer to an int" — so p will hold the address of an int variable. The asterisk * goes BETWEEN the type and the name (some books put it next to the name like int *p, others next to the type like int* p — same thing). (2) p is the name. (3) = &x assigns the address of x to p. So now p HOLDS the address of x. p is itself a variable (it has its own address!) but the value INSIDE p is x's address. The arrow analogy: p is an arrow pointing to x.
gui.tutorials.cPtr.pointsTo: Line 11: printf("p points to %p\n", (void*)p); — note no & this time. We just print p directly because p ALREADY holds an address. When you Run, this address will MATCH the one from line 10 — &x and p give the same number, because p was set to &x. They are two ways to talk about the same memory location.
gui.tutorials.cPtr.dereference: Line 12: printf("Value at p: %d\n", *p); — the *p in front of the variable is DEREFERENCING. It says "follow the arrow and grab the value at the other end". So *p is the same as x — both equal 10. Confusing fact: the * means TWO different things in C. In a declaration (int *p), the * says "this is a pointer". In an expression (*p), the * says "follow the pointer". Same symbol, different jobs depending on context. Read it carefully: int *p creates a pointer; *p uses a pointer.
gui.tutorials.cPtr.passToFunction: Lines 3-5 define doubleIt: void doubleIt(int *p) { *p = *p * 2; }. The parameter is int *p — the function takes a POINTER, not an int. Inside, *p = *p * 2 means: read the value at p, multiply by 2, store the result back at p. So if p is pointing at x, this changes x itself — not a local copy. Without pointers, the function would only get a COPY of x and any change would vanish when the function returned. Pointers are how you give a function the power to modify its caller's variables.
gui.tutorials.cPtr.callIt: Line 13: doubleIt(&x); — pass the address of x. Inside doubleIt, p becomes &x (the address). The function dereferences and writes — so x in main becomes 20. Notice scanf("%d", &age) works the same way: scanf takes a pointer, dereferences it, and writes the kid's number into your variable. Now you know WHY scanf needed the & — it needed a pointer to MODIFY the variable.
gui.tutorials.cPtr.run: Click Run. The Console shows four lines. (1) "x lives at 0x..." — some hex address. (2) "p points to 0x..." — the SAME hex address (p was set to &x). (3) "Value at p: 10" — *p dereferences and reads x's value. (4) "After doubleIt: 20" — x got doubled by the function call. The exact hex addresses change each run; the relationships don't. p == &x, *p == x, and a function with a pointer parameter can change the original.
gui.tutorials.cPrint.intro: Last chapter you READ a C program someone else wrote. Today YOU are going to write — multiple printf lines, special characters that change how text looks, and your first comment for future-you to read. The boring setup parts (#include and main) are already typed for you so you can focus on what you're actually learning. Look at the editor: line 1 has the #include we covered last chapter, line 3 has int main, lines 4 and 6 are the body. The line that says "// your code here" is just a placeholder to show you where to write — we'll replace it shortly. By the end of this chapter you will be comfortable writing printf statements with multiple lines, tab spacing, and quotes inside text.
gui.tutorials.cPrint.first: Click on line 4 (the line that says "// your code here") and select the whole line. Now type: printf("Hello!\n"); — exact spelling, exact brackets, semicolon at the end. Pay close attention to the small details: lower-case p-r-i-n-t-f, opening quote, Hello with capital H, exclamation mark, backslash n (NOT slash n), closing quote, closing parenthesis, semicolon. Forgetting the semicolon at the end is the number one mistake C beginners make — if you ever see a red error in the Log tab, check your semicolons first. The \n means NEW LINE — it pushes whatever prints next onto a fresh line. Without it, things would all jam together. Take your time typing this; speed will come later.
gui.tutorials.cPrint.second:
Press Enter at the end of line 4 to start a new line. Type: printf("My name is Sam.
"); — replace Sam with your real name. Each printf is its own complete instruction (called a STATEMENT in programming) and each one ends with its own semicolon. You can have as many printfs as you want — they will run in order, top to bottom, when the program runs. Notice that we put a 
 at the end of THIS printf too — that ensures whatever comes after it lands on a new line. If you forgot the 
 on the FIRST printf last step, the two messages would glue together: "Hello!My name is Sam." with no space. Each printf controls its OWN line ending.
gui.tutorials.cPrint.run:
Click the Run button. After it finishes, switch to the Console tab if you're not already there. You should see two lines: "Hello!" on the first line, then your name on a fresh line below. The 
 at the end of the first printf is what dropped the second message onto a new line. Try this experiment: remove the 
 from the FIRST printf (just delete the backslash and the n, leave the rest alone), then click Run again. Now both messages glue together onto one line: "Hello!My name is Sam." That's the difference 
 makes. Add the 
 back when you're done playing.
gui.tutorials.cPrint.tab: Add another line below the others: printf("Two\twords\tspaced\n"); — pay attention to the \t pieces between the words. \t is a TAB, similar to pressing the Tab key in a word processor. Tabs are bigger than regular spaces — they make the text jump to the next "tab stop" (usually about every 8 characters). When this line runs, you'll see the three words spread out with big gaps between them. Tabs are super useful when you want to line things up in columns, like a small spreadsheet of numbers. Just like \n means newline, \t means tab — both start with a backslash to tell C "this isn't the literal letter, it's a special code".
gui.tutorials.cPrint.quote: What if your printed text needs to include a QUOTE MARK in the middle? You might think "I'll just type it like printf("She said "Hi!"")". But C would get confused — the SECOND quote would END the string early, and the rest would be garbage. The fix is to put a backslash before each quote you want to be printed: \". Add this line: printf("She said \"Hi!\"\n"); — the \" pairs print as literal quote marks. The general rule: when a character has a special meaning to C (like " ending a string, or \ starting a special code), put a backslash in front to say "I literally mean this character". \" = print a quote, \\ = print a backslash itself, \n = newline, \t = tab.
gui.tutorials.cPrint.comment: Click at the very start of line 4 (the first printf) and press Enter to push it down, then go back up to the new empty line and type: // these prints test the escape codes. The two slashes // mark the start of a COMMENT — anything from // to the end of that line is for HUMANS to read; the compiler completely ignores it. Comments don't make your program do anything — they explain WHY you wrote things. As your programs grow longer (50, 100, 1000 lines), comments save you when you come back next week and wonder "why did I do this?" Good programmers leave comments for their future self and for teammates. Bad programmers don't — and pay for it later. Use them generously, but only where they actually help (don't comment "// add 1 to x" — the code already says that).
gui.tutorials.cPrint.runAll: Click Run one more time. Notice that the comment line you added didn't print anything to the Console — exactly right; C ignored it. The Console shows your three printfs in order: the greeting, your name, the tab-spaced words, and the quoted line. You've learned the basics of printing in C: multiple printfs, the \n newline, the \t tab, and how to print quote marks with \". You also know what comments are for. Next chapter we move on from printing fixed text to PRINTING VALUES — variables that can hold and remember numbers.
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.plan: The capstone tutorial. You'll build a quiz game that asks three questions, reads each answer, checks if the answer is right, and prints a final score out of 3. The program uses TWO PARALLEL ARRAYS (one for questions, one for answers), a for loop to ask each question, scanf to read replies, strcmp to check correctness, and the accumulator pattern for the score. Every concept from the last 31 chapters working together in one short program.
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.includes: Lines 1 and 2: stdio.h (for printf and scanf, as always) and string.h (for strcmp, to compare the kid's answer to the correct one). Two libraries, two reasons. Without string.h, strcmp wouldn't exist and we'd have to write our own letter-by-letter comparison loop — much more work.
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.ask: Lines 5 to 10: questions is a 2D char array — three rows, each row up to 30 characters long. Each row holds one question string. answers is the same shape, three rows of up to 20 characters. The arrays are PARALLEL — questions[0] goes with answers[0], questions[1] with answers[1], questions[2] with answers[2]. Same index, matching data. We saw 2D arrays in the grids chapter and char arrays as strings in the strings chapter — this combines both ideas.
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.show: Line 11: char reply[40]; — ONE buffer, reused for each question. We don't need three separate reply variables — after we check the answer, we throw the kid's reply away and reuse the buffer for the next question. 40 characters is plenty (the kid won't type a 40-letter answer for "Capital of France"). Sizing buffers safely is a good habit even when you "know" they'll be small.
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.data: Line 12: int score = 0; — the accumulator pattern again. Start at zero, add 1 for each correct answer as we go. After all 3 questions, score holds the final tally. (If we forgot to set score = 0, it would hold garbage and the final printf would show a weird number.)
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.counter: Line 13: for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) — three questions, so loop three times. The counter i serves as the index into BOTH arrays at the same time: questions[i] picks the current question and answers[i] picks the current correct answer. They stay in sync because they share the same index. Parallel arrays + same index = matched pairs.
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.loop: Lines 14 to 16, the loop body. (1) printf("%s", questions[i]); — print the current question (no \n on purpose so the kid types right after the prompt). (2) scanf("%s", reply); — read what the kid types into reply. (3) if (strcmp(reply, answers[i]) == 0) score++; — compare reply to the correct answer; remember strcmp returns 0 for SAME. If they match, bump the score. After 3 iterations, the score holds 0, 1, 2, or 3.
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.final: Line 18: printf("You scored %d / 3\n", score); — placed OUTSIDE the loop so it runs ONCE after all questions are answered. Prints something like "You scored 2 / 3". Notice the / 3 in the format string is just literal text — printf prints it as-is. Same trick as the %% pattern (a literal % in printf needs to be %%, but / has no special meaning so it's just /).
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.play: Click Run. Console asks "Capital of France? " — click in the Console and type Paris, press Enter. Then "2 + 2 * 3 = ? " — type 8 (math: multiplication first → 2 + 6 → 8). Then "Color of the sky? " — type Blue. Final line: "You scored 3 / 3". Try one wrong on purpose (type "London" for the first one) and see the score drop to 2.
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.reflection: Look at how many concepts you used in this short program: PARALLEL ARRAYS (questions and answers in lock-step), a FOR LOOP over a fixed count, SCANF to read input, STRCMP to compare strings, the ACCUMULATOR pattern for score, PRINTF with %s and %d format codes. Six C concepts woven into one useful program. You can read AND write all of them now.
gui.tutorials.cQuiz.end: You know enough C to write small useful programs — calculators, quizzes, games, simple text processors. The next big topics are: FILE I/O (fopen, fread to read from disk), STRUCTS (custom types like a Player with name, score, level), POINTERS (the deep magic that makes C powerful), and DYNAMIC MEMORY (malloc, free for arrays whose size you don't know upfront). Every advanced topic builds on what you've learned in these 32 chapters. You have the foundation; everything else is a step up from here.
gui.tutorials.cRecur.intro: A function in C is allowed to CALL ITSELF. That sounds weird — even paradoxical — but it works for problems where the answer is "the smaller version of this same problem, plus one step". Think of Russian nesting dolls: each big doll contains a smaller doll inside, which contains an even smaller doll, all the way down to the tiniest one. When a function calls itself, the technique is called RECURSION. Today you'll write a recursive function to compute FACTORIAL: 5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120. The recursive insight: 5! = 5 × 4! and 4! = 4 × 3! and so on, all the way down to 1! = 1.
gui.tutorials.cRecur.basecase: Lines 4 to 6: if (n <= 1) { return 1; }. This is the BASE CASE — the simplest version of the problem, answered directly. For factorial, 1! = 1 (and 0! is also 1 by mathematical convention). Without a base case, the function would call itself forever and forever, and your program would crash with a "stack overflow" error. EVERY recursive function MUST have a base case. The base case is the bottom of the chain — the smallest doll that doesn't open.
gui.tutorials.cRecur.recursive: Line 7: return n * factorial(n - 1); — the RECURSIVE call. To compute factorial(5), C asks "what's factorial(4)?". To compute factorial(4), it asks "what's factorial(3)?". And so on down to factorial(1), which hits the base case and returns 1 directly. Then the answers UNWIND back up: factorial(2) = 2 * 1 = 2, factorial(3) = 3 * 2 = 6, factorial(4) = 4 * 6 = 24, factorial(5) = 5 * 24 = 120. Each call waits for the smaller one to finish, then multiplies and returns.
gui.tutorials.cRecur.run: Click Run. Console: "5! = 120". The whole chain happened in microseconds. Try factorial(7) (= 5040), factorial(10) (= 3628800), factorial(12) (= 479001600). Try factorial(20) — you'll get a weird negative number, because 20! is too big to fit in an int. Numbers grow VERY fast with factorial — a classic gotcha. The recursion itself is correct; the int type just can't hold the result. (For bigger numbers, you'd use a long long type — beyond this chapter.)
gui.tutorials.cScope.intro: In Scratch, when you made a variable you had a choice: "For this sprite only" or "For all sprites". That's SCOPE — where the variable is visible. C has the same idea but with different words. Variables made INSIDE a function are LOCAL — only that function can see them, and they disappear when the function ends. Variables made OUTSIDE any function (at the top of the file) are GLOBAL — visible to every function, and they stick around for the whole program. Today you'll see both at the same time and watch the difference in behavior.
gui.tutorials.cScope.global: Line 3: int globalCount = 0; — declared OUTSIDE any function, at the very top of the file. This is a GLOBAL variable. Every function in this file can read it AND change it. It exists for the entire lifetime of the program — it's "born" when the program starts and "dies" only when the program ends. Like Scratch's "for all sprites" variable that all sprites can change.
gui.tutorials.cScope.local: Line 6: int localCount = 0; — declared INSIDE the bump function. This is a LOCAL variable. It is BORN every time bump is called (set to 0), it's only visible inside bump (main can't see it), and it DIES when bump returns. Every call to bump gets a FRESH localCount — nothing is remembered between calls. Like a sticky note you write inside a meeting and throw away when you leave the room.
gui.tutorials.cScope.run:
Click Run. Console:
local=1 global=1
local=1 global=2
local=1 global=3
Watch what happens. localCount RESETS to 0 every call (born fresh, dies at end of function), so it always prints 1 after the ++. globalCount keeps GROWING because it lives across all calls — each call adds 1 to the same shared variable. Three calls → global ends at 3.
gui.tutorials.cScope.advice: Globals are CONVENIENT (any function can change them anywhere) but they are also DANGEROUS (any function can change them anywhere — same property is both good and bad). With many globals, your program becomes hard to follow because you can't tell what changes the variable just by reading one function. Real-world C code uses locals heavily and globals sparingly. The rule of thumb: if a value only matters inside one function, keep it LOCAL. Only go global when truly needed (configuration, shared counters, etc.). Smaller scope = safer code.
gui.tutorials.cStrFn.intro: In Scratch you had handy string blocks: "length of [Hello]" gave you 5, "join [Hello] [world]" gave you "Helloworld", "letter (3) of [Hello]" gave you "l". C's string library has equivalent tools — strlen (length), strcat (concatenate / join), strcmp (compare), strcpy (copy). One #include line at the top brings them all in. Today you'll use the four most common ones in a single program — counting letters, comparing two strings, joining strings together.
gui.tutorials.cStrFn.include: Line 2: #include <string.h> — same shape as math.h or stdio.h, just a different toolbox. Without this line, strlen, strcmp, strcat, and strcpy would all be unknown to the compiler — you'd get "function not declared" errors. The .h header tells the compiler what these functions look like; the actual code lives in the C standard library.
gui.tutorials.cStrFn.strlen: Line 7: strlen(a) — returns how many CHARACTERS are in the string, NOT including the hidden '\0' terminator. For "Hello" that's 5 (H, e, l, l, o). The return type is a special "size_t" (basically unsigned long), so we use %lu in printf. (For most beginner code, %d works too but technically %lu is correct.) Try strlen on a longer string to see — strlen("CodeSkool") = 9, strlen("") = 0 (empty string).
gui.tutorials.cStrFn.strcpy: strcpy(dest, src) — COPIES the contents of src into dest. Note: dest is FIRST in the arguments, src second. (It's like writing dest = src; in pseudo-code.) Why do you need strcpy? Because you CAN'T write a = "Hello"; once a is declared — assignment doesn't work for arrays. To put a new string into an existing char array, use strcpy(a, "Hello");. Important: dest must be BIG ENOUGH to hold src plus the '\0' — if not, you write past the end and corrupt memory.
gui.tutorials.cStrFn.strcmp: Lines 8 to 12: if (strcmp(a, b) == 0) — strcmp returns 0 if the two strings are EXACTLY equal, non-zero otherwise. Surprising twist: == 0 means SAME, == non-zero means DIFFERENT. This catches everyone — you'd expect "true means same" but it's the opposite. (The non-zero return is actually the alphabetic difference, useful for sorting.) NEVER compare strings with == directly — that compares ADDRESSES, not contents, and almost always gives wrong answers.
gui.tutorials.cStrFn.strcat: Lines 13 and 14: strcat(a, " "); then strcat(a, b);. strcat(dest, src) APPENDS src to the end of dest. So after strcat(a, " "), a holds "Hello "; after strcat(a, b), a holds "Hello World". (Same as Scratch's "join [Hello] [World]" block, just two steps.) Important: dest must have ROOM for the new content — that's why we declared a[20] originally, plenty of space for "Hello World" plus the terminator.
gui.tutorials.cStrFn.pitfall: strcpy and strcat do NOT check sizes — they trust you to make sure dest is big enough. If dest is char x[5] and you copy "HelloWorld" into it, you write 11 characters into a 5-character box — you've overwritten 6 characters worth of memory you don't own. This is undefined behavior and a HUGE category of real-world security bugs (called "buffer overflows"). Always size your buffer larger than the largest string you'll put in it. The safer cousins strncpy and strncat take a max length and stop at the limit — use them for input you don't control.
gui.tutorials.cStrFn.recap: Click Run. Console: len(a) = 5, Different, Joined: Hello World. strlen counted "Hello" as 5. strcmp returned non-zero because "Hello" and "World" are different strings, so the else branch fired. strcat joined them with a space in between. Scratch's "length of", "=" check, and "join" all in one C chapter.
gui.tutorials.cStr.intro: In Scratch, "Hello world" was just a string — easy. C is fussier: a STRING in C is actually an ARRAY of chars. Each letter sits in its own slot, plus a hidden '\0' (the "null terminator") at the end so C knows where the string stops. So "Hello" in C takes 6 slots: 'H', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o', '\0'. The null terminator is invisible but critical — without it, functions like printf wouldn't know when to stop reading. Today you'll create a string, print it whole, peek at individual letters, and change a letter directly.
gui.tutorials.cStr.declare: Line 4: char name[20] = "CodeSkool"; — char is the type, name is the variable name, [20] is the BUFFER SIZE (room for 19 letters PLUS the hidden '\0'). The text in DOUBLE quotes "CodeSkool" gets stored in the array, including the automatic terminator at the end. Always size your buffer bigger than your worst-case string — if you might hold a 30-character name, declare char name[40] for safety. Forgetting room for the '\0' is a classic bug.
gui.tutorials.cStr.print: Line 5: printf("Name: %s\n", name); — %s is the format code for STRINGS (the s stands for string). printf reads characters from name one at a time and prints them, until it hits the '\0' terminator — then it stops. If the '\0' was missing, printf would keep going into garbage memory and you'd see random gibberish. This is why the null terminator is so important: it's the "stop sign" for string functions.
gui.tutorials.cStr.index: Line 6: name[0] is 'C' — the first letter, zero-indexed (just like regular arrays). %c is the format code for char (one character). So name[0] is 'C', name[1] is 'o', name[2] is 'd', name[3] is 'e', name[4] is 'S', and so on. Strings in C ARE arrays — same indexing rules. You can read or write any individual letter using the [index] syntax.
gui.tutorials.cStr.buffer: Line 7: name[0] = 'B'; — overwrite slot 0 with the letter B. SINGLE quotes around B because it's a char, not a string. After this line, the string in name is "BodeSkool" — the B replaced the C. The next printf will show the new version. You CAN'T do name = "BodeSkool"; — that doesn't work for arrays. To change letters you either edit them one at a time (like this) or use a function like strcpy (next chapter).
gui.tutorials.cStr.scanf: To read a string from the kid: char input[40]; then scanf("%s", input);. Two important details. (1) NO ampersand & in front of input — array names ALREADY are addresses in C, so scanf doesn't need the &. (2) scanf with %s reads only one WORD — it stops at the first space. So if the kid types "John Smith", only "John" gets stored. To read a whole line including spaces, you'd use fgets — for now stick to single-word inputs.
gui.tutorials.cStr.greetUser:
Click Run. Console:
Name: CodeSkool
First letter: C
Changed: BodeSkool
The third line shows the result of overwriting slot 0 — the B replaced the C, and the rest of the word stayed put. Try changing other slots: name[5] = 'X'; would change the S in the middle. Each slot is its own char; you can edit any of them.
gui.tutorials.cStr.recap: Three things to remember about C strings: (1) they are CHAR ARRAYS — each letter is one char in a numbered slot. (2) they end in a HIDDEN '\0' terminator that tells string functions where to stop. (3) %s prints the whole thing; %c prints one char at a time. Next chapter we'll meet the helper functions that work on whole strings: strlen (length), strcpy (copy), strcmp (compare), strcat (join). Together those let you do most string work without manually walking the array.
gui.tutorials.cSwitch.why: Imagine you have to check ONE variable against MANY specific values — like a menu choice (1, 2, 3, 4, or 5), a grade letter (A, B, C, D, F), a key press (W, A, S, D for movement). You COULD write a long else-if chain (if grade == 'A', else if grade == 'B', else if grade == 'C', ...) but it gets repetitive fast. C has a cleaner tool for this exact case: the switch statement. Same logic, less typing, and it tells the reader "I am picking from a list of fixed values" at a glance. switch is your friend whenever you have ONE value with MANY possible specific cases.
gui.tutorials.cSwitch.setup: Line 4: char grade = 'B'; — single quotes around B because grade is a char (one character). The switch coming next will look at this variable and pick a CASE based on what it holds. switch only works with whole-number-like types (int, char, enum) — not strings or floats. char counts because each character is internally a small number (A is 65, B is 66, etc.).
gui.tutorials.cSwitch.cases: Lines 5 to 17 are the switch block. The structure is: switch (variable) { case VALUE: do stuff; break; case ANOTHER_VALUE: do stuff; break; default: do fallback; }. Think of it like a vending machine: the switch looks at the input (grade), checks each case from top to bottom for a match ('A' first? 'B' second? 'C' third?), and runs the matching case. If nothing matches, default runs (like the else at the end of an if-else chain).
gui.tutorials.cSwitch.break: Notice the break; line at the end of each case. break tells C "I am done with this case, EXIT the switch entirely". Without break, C "falls through" to the NEXT case and runs that too — and the one after that. Forgetting break is one of the most common switch bugs. Try removing the break after case 'A' (delete line 8) and run with grade = 'A' — you'll see "Excellent!" AND "Good job." AND "Keep trying." all print. That fall-through behavior is sometimes useful but usually a bug. Always end each case with break unless you really mean fall-through.
gui.tutorials.cSwitch.run: Trace it: grade is 'B'. switch checks case 'A' — no match. Checks case 'B' — MATCH. Runs the body: prints "Good job." then break exits the switch entirely. The default case is skipped. Click Run to confirm — Console shows "Good job." Now change grade to 'A' and rerun → "Excellent!" Try 'C', then 'X' (unknown), then 'F' (unknown). The default catches anything that doesn't match any specific case.
gui.tutorials.cVar.why: So far every printf had the message baked right into the quotes. That works fine for fixed text, but real programs need to REMEMBER values that can change — like a score, a level number, the player's age, or how many lives are left. In Scratch, when you wanted to remember a number, you made a VARIABLE — that orange round bubble in the Variables panel. You set its value with "set [score] to (0)" and changed it with "change [score] by 1". C does the exact same thing, just typed instead of dragged. Today you'll create your first C variable, give it a starting value, print it, change it, and add a second variable too. By the end you will understand the basic shape that EVERY C variable uses.
gui.tutorials.cVar.declare: Replace the placeholder line (the // your variable goes here line) with this: int age = 12; — exact spelling, semicolon at the end. Let's break it down piece by piece. The word int means INTEGER — a whole number with no decimal point. C is fussier than Scratch; you have to TELL it what type of value the variable will hold. age is the NAME you picked for the variable — you choose names that make sense for what they hold. The = sign means "make this variable equal to" — it's ASSIGNMENT, not "is equal to" (we'll see what equal-equal looks like in a later chapter). 12 is the value being stored. The semicolon ends the line. The whole thing reads as: "Create a whole-number box called age, put 12 in it." In Scratch this was a "set [age] to (12)" block.
gui.tutorials.cVar.print: Add a new line below: printf("My age is %d\n", age); — there are TWO new things to notice here. First: inside the quotes is %d (percent-d). That is a PLACEHOLDER — a slot that printf will fill in with a number when the program runs. The d stands for "decimal integer" (i.e. a whole number). Second: after the closing quote, there is a comma and then the variable name (, age). That comma tells printf "use this value to fill in the placeholder". You can think of it like this: printf reads the quote string, sees the %d slot, and reaches into its arguments to pull out the next value. So "My age is %d" + age=12 becomes "My age is 12". In Scratch this was the "join [Hello] [world]" block — combining text and a variable.
gui.tutorials.cVar.run: Before clicking Run, predict in your head: when this runs, will the Console show "My age is %d" or will it show "My age is 12"? Decide first, then click Run. The Console shows "My age is 12" — the %d placeholder was REPLACED by the value of age. This is exactly like Scratch's "join" block stitching text with a variable, just written differently. printf is smart: every %d in the string gets matched up with the next variable in the comma-separated list after the quotes. We only have one %d here, so we only need one variable.
gui.tutorials.cVar.change: Edit line 4 — change the 12 to your own real age (or any number you like — pick something different from 12 so you can see the change). Click Run. The Console shows your new number — but notice you only had to change ONE place. The printf stayed exactly the same. That is the WIN of variables: you store a value once, use it many times by referring to its name. Imagine if your program printed the age 50 times — without a variable you'd have to update 50 lines. With a variable you update 1 line and all 50 printfs follow. This becomes huge as programs grow.
gui.tutorials.cVar.second: Variables stack — you can have as many as you want in one program. Below the existing two lines, add two more lines: int year = 2026; on one line, then printf("The year is %d\n", year); on the next. Same shape as before, different name and different value. Each printf has its own %d slot and its own variable to fill it. This is how you'd track a player's score, lives, level, time-left, ammo, and high-score all separately in a real game — each gets its own variable, each has its own purpose, each is updated independently.
gui.tutorials.cVar.runBoth: Click Run. Two lines now in the Console — your age, then the year (2026). Each printf used its own variable in its own %d slot. The order matches the order of the printfs. You can have as many variables and as many printfs as you want; the program runs them top to bottom. Try adding a third variable like int month = 11; with its own printf. The pattern is always the same: declare the variable on its own line (int NAME = VALUE;), then use it in a printf with %d wherever you want it to appear.
gui.tutorials.cVar.mismatch: Now let's break it ON PURPOSE so you understand the rule. On line 5, change %d to %f and click Run. The age line now prints something WEIRD — usually 0.000000 or a giant random decimal that makes no sense. C did NOT refuse to compile — it just trusted you blindly. %d means "the value here is a whole number"; %f means "the value here is a decimal number". They are stored differently in memory, so when you tell printf "treat this whole-number area as a decimal", it reads the bits wrong and shows garbage. This trips up everyone at least once. The lesson: %d goes with int, %f goes with float, %c goes with char. Always match the format code to the variable type. Change %f back to %d.
gui.tutorials.cTypes.intro: In Scratch, a variable could hold pretty much anything — a number, a word, a sentence — and Scratch figured out the type for you. C is fussier and asks you to be SPECIFIC. The reason: each type takes a different amount of memory, and the CPU does math differently on each type. Today you'll meet the three most common types in C. The first is int — INTEGERS, whole numbers like 42, -7, 0, 1000. The second is float — DECIMAL numbers like 3.14 or 12.5 (the f stands for floating point, which means the decimal point can "float" to wherever it needs to be). The third is char — a single CHARACTER like 'A', '!', or '7'. By the end of this chapter you'll have written and printed all three types in one program.
gui.tutorials.cTypes.float: Replace the placeholder with these two lines: int score = 90; on the first line, then float price = 12.5; on the next. The first line is just like last chapter — a whole-number variable named score holding 90. The second line uses a NEW type: float. float means "a number that can have a decimal point". int can't hold 12.5 — that's a float's job. If you tried to store 12.5 in an int variable, C would silently chop off the decimal and store just 12. Use float when you need decimals — prices, distances, percentages, averages, scientific measurements, anything that isn't a whole number.
gui.tutorials.cTypes.printFloat: Add two printfs: printf("Score: %d\n", score); on one line, then printf("Price: %.2f\n", price); on the next. Notice the format codes are DIFFERENT for each type. %d for the int (we know that). %.2f for the float — the f means "decimal number", and the .2 in front controls how many digits to show after the decimal point. So %.2f means "show 2 decimals", which prints 12.50. If you wrote %.0f, you'd get 13 (rounded). If you wrote just %f with no .2, you'd get 12.500000 (six decimals — the default). The .2 makes prices and money look right. Always pick the number of decimals that fits what you're showing.
gui.tutorials.cTypes.charVsString: Now we meet char (pronounced "car" or "char"). A char holds ONE single character — a letter, a digit, a symbol. Examples: 'A', 'B', '!', '7', ' ' (a space). Notice the SINGLE QUOTES around each. This is one of C's big rules to remember: SINGLE quotes for one character (char), DOUBLE quotes for a whole string of text. So 'A' is a char (one letter) but "A" is a string (a one-letter string, which is different in C). Mixing them up is a classic bug. char takes only 1 byte of memory — perfect for storing things like grades (A, B, C), menu choices (Y, N), or single-key answers in a quiz.
gui.tutorials.cTypes.addChar: Below the price line, add: char grade = 'A'; — note the single quotes around the A. char is the type, grade is the name, 'A' is the value. This stores the letter A in a one-byte variable called grade. char is great for grades (A, B, C, F), menu choices (Y or N), single-letter codes, or anywhere you only need one character. If you tried to write char grade = "A"; (with double quotes), the compiler would refuse — "A" is a string, not a char. Single quotes for char, always.
gui.tutorials.cTypes.printChar: Add a third printf: printf("Grade: %c\n", grade); — %c is the format code for char (c for character). So you now have three printfs: one with %d for int, one with %.2f for float, and one with %c for char. Each format code matches its type. Remember the lesson from last chapter: mixing them up gives garbage output. The compiler trusts you to match them, so it's YOUR job to get it right.
gui.tutorials.cTypes.run: Click Run. The Console shows three lines: Score: 90, Price: 12.50, Grade: A. Same printf trick you learned last chapter — just three different format codes for three different types. Once you know the four basic format codes (%d for int, %f for float, %c for char, %s for string — coming later), you can print pretty much anything. Try changing the values: edit price to 99.99, grade to 'B', score to 75, and Run again. The output reflects your changes immediately. Every C program from here on uses these types — int the most, then float, then char. Get comfortable with all three.
gui.tutorials.cIntro.welcome: You already know Scratch — you drag colored blocks together, click the green flag, and your sprite jumps and talks. C is doing the SAME job: telling the computer what to do. The big difference is that you TYPE the instructions instead of dragging blocks. Typing sounds harder, but it gives you way more power. C is one of the oldest and most respected programming languages in the world. Most of Windows is written in C. Most of Linux is written in C. Big chunks of the games you play, the apps on your phone, even parts of Python and JavaScript themselves — all built on top of C. By the end of these chapters, you will be able to read and write your own C programs. Don't worry about being slow at first; everyone is. The trick is just to keep going.
gui.tutorials.cIntro.pipeline: In Scratch, you click the green flag and the blocks just run — the Scratch app does all the hard work behind the scenes. C has one extra step you should know about. When you type C code, the computer can't actually run it directly — your typed words are just text, like a story. A special tool called the COMPILER reads your text and turns it into machine code (the secret language the CPU actually speaks). Only then can your program run. The good news: CodeSkool runs the compiler for you automatically every time you click Run. You don't do anything extra — but knowing this step exists will help you later when you see messages like "compiling..." in the Log tab. So the journey is: you type → compiler turns it into machine code → machine code runs → output appears in the Console.
gui.tutorials.cIntro.threeWays: Look at the picture: the same simple program — print the word Hello — written three different ways. On the left is the Scratch version (drag a "say [Hello]" block onto the script area). In the middle is Python (one short typed line). On the right is C (a few more lines, with #include and main and braces). All three programs do exactly the same thing when you run them. They look different because each language has its own rules and its own style — but the idea behind them is identical. The C version on the right looks the most complicated right now — that's normal. By chapter 5 it will feel completely natural. Stick with it.
gui.tutorials.cIntro.whereToWrite: Next chapter you'll click the C++ Editor card on the Welcome dialog. Don't be put off by the name — even though it says C++, that is the right environment for our C programs (C++ is C's big brother and runs C code just fine). The very FIRST time you click the Run button, it will take about 45 seconds before anything happens. That's because CodeSkool is quietly downloading the C compiler in the background. After the first run, every Run is instant. So if the first one feels slow, just wait — it only happens once per session. When you're ready, click Next to start your first real C program.
gui.tutorials.cWhile.intro: Imagine printing the numbers 1 to 5. You COULD write five separate printf lines — that works but it's ugly. What about 1 to 1000? Or 1 to a million? You need a LOOP — code that repeats. Scratch had three loop blocks: "repeat (10)" (do this exactly N times), "repeat until <condition>" (keep going UNTIL something becomes true), and "forever" (never stop). C's while loop is the closest match to "repeat until NOT condition" — it keeps going AS LONG AS the condition stays true. Today you'll write a while loop that prints 1 to 5, then accidentally make a forever loop and learn to escape it.
gui.tutorials.cWhile.counter: Line 4: int count = 1; — a regular int variable. By tradition, C loop counters are usually named i, j, k, or count. The counter holds the CURRENT iteration number — it changes each time around the loop. We start at 1 and plan to stop after 5. Setting the starting value BEFORE the loop is important — the loop will read this variable on the very first check.
gui.tutorials.cWhile.loop: Lines 5 to 8 are the while loop. The shape: while (condition) { body; }. Each pass through the loop, C does this: (1) check the condition. (2) if true, run the body once. (3) jump back to step 1. (4) if false, EXIT the loop and continue with the line after the closing brace. Inside the body, count++ is critical — without it, count would never change, the condition would never become false, and the loop would run FOREVER. The body has two lines: print the current count, then bump count by 1.
gui.tutorials.cWhile.run: Trace it step by step in your head before clicking. count=1, check 1<=5 → true, print "Count is 1", count becomes 2. count=2, check 2<=5 → true, print "Count is 2", count becomes 3. ... eventually count=5, check 5<=5 → true, print "Count is 5", count becomes 6. count=6, check 6<=5 → FALSE, exit loop, jump to the printf("Done!") line. Click Run. Console shows: Count is 1, Count is 2, Count is 3, Count is 4, Count is 5, Done!
gui.tutorials.cWhile.infiniteIntro: Loops have ONE big trap: if the condition NEVER becomes false, the loop runs FOREVER and your program never stops. The Stop button at the top right (the square icon next to Run) is your safety net — it kills the program when things go wrong. The most common cause of a forever loop is forgetting to update the counter inside the loop body. We're going to break the loop on purpose right now so you know exactly what an infinite loop looks like, and how to recover from one.
gui.tutorials.cWhile.runInfinite: Put // at the very start of the count++ line — the // turns it into a comment, and C ignores commented lines. Now count is never updated. Click Run. The Console fills rapidly with "Count is 1, Count is 1, Count is 1, ..." forever, because count stays at 1, and 1 <= 5 is always true. Click the Stop button (square icon, top right) to kill it. Now remove the // to restore the count++ line. The takeaway: every while loop needs SOMETHING inside it that eventually makes the condition false. If you forget, you get a forever loop.
gui.tutorials.cWhile.changeLimit: With count++ restored, change the condition to count <= 10 and Run. Now you get 10 lines instead of 5 — the only change was when the loop stops. Try count <= 100 — 100 lines, instant. Try count <= 1000 — 1000 lines, still fast. The loop is doing the same work each pass; only the stopping point changed. This is the power of loops: write the body ONCE, repeat it as many times as you want by changing one number.
gui.tutorials.html_comments.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlComments.first: Drag a text block inside the body. In the text field, type the comment markers exactly: less-than-sign exclamation dash dash space This is the page header space dash dash greater-than-sign. Anything between these markers is a comment.
gui.tutorials.htmlComments.heading: Drag a heading below the comment, still inside the body. Keep level 1 and type My Recipe Book.
gui.tutorials.htmlComments.second: Drag another text block below the heading, still inside the body. Type the same kind of comment markers around the words Favourite recipes.
gui.tutorials.htmlComments.list: Drag a ul below the second comment inside the body. Add two li items, each with a text block inside, and type Pancakes and Pizza.
gui.tutorials.htmlComments.viewTab: Click the View tab. You should see only the heading and the list — the comments are invisible to readers. Now click the HTML tab to see the comments in the source code.
gui.tutorials.htmlComments.recap: Comments are notes you leave in your code so you can remember what each part does. The browser ignores them. Real websites use comments all the time to organise their code.
gui.tutorials.html_css_box.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.div: Drag a div inside the body. Set its class to card. Drag a paragraph inside the div and type I am inside a box.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.style: Drag the style block inside the head.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.selector: Drag a selector inside the style. Replace the text with .card — the dot tells CSS to look for the class card.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.bg: Drag a background-color block inside the selector. Replace skyblue with lightblue. Now the box has a colour we can see.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.padding: Drag a padding block inside the selector. Replace 10 pixels with 20 pixels. Padding pushes the text away from the inside edge of the box.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.border: Drag a border block inside the selector. Replace the value with 2 pixels solid darkblue. The border draws a line around the box.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.margin: Drag a margin block inside the selector. Replace 10 pixels with 20 pixels. Margin pushes the box away from its neighbours on the outside.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.viewTab: Click the View tab. Look at your box — can you spot the colour inside, the line at the edge, and the gap on the outside?
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.recap: Every element on a page is a box with three layers — padding inside, border at the edge, margin outside. This is called the box model.
gui.tutorials.html_css_flex.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.row: Drag a div inside the body. Set its class to row. This will hold the three cards we'll arrange.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.cards: Drag three more divs inside the row, one below the other. Set each one's class to card.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.cardsText: Inside each of the three cards, drag a paragraph and type Card 1, Card 2, and Card 3 — one in each.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.style: Drag the style block inside the head.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.selectorRow: Drag a selector inside the style. Replace the text with .row.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.display: Drag a display block inside the .row selector. Pick flex from the dropdown. Normally elements stack vertically — flex arranges them side by side in a row.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.gap: Drag a gap block inside the selector. Replace 10 pixels with 20 pixels. Gap is the breathing room between flex items.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.cardStyle: Drag another selector below the first. Replace its text with .card. Inside it, add a padding set to 20 pixels and a background-color set to lightblue — so the cards become visible boxes.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.viewTab: Click the View tab. Are your three cards arranged side by side in a row, with space between them?
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.recap: Display flex turns a container into a flex row. Gap adds space between items. Together they're how real websites build rows of cards.
gui.tutorials.html_css_fonts.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.paragraph: Drag a paragraph inside the body. Type This text will look fancy. We'll style it next.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.style: Drag the style block inside the head.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.selector: Drag a selector inside the style. Replace the selector text with p — this picks every paragraph.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.size: Drag a font-size block inside the selector. Replace 16 pixels with 24 pixels. Bigger numbers mean bigger text.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.align: Drag a text-align block inside the selector below font-size. Pick center from the dropdown. The text now sits in the middle of the page.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.family: Drag a font block inside the selector. Pick family from the property dropdown and type Arial in the value field.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.viewTab: Click the View tab. Is the text bigger, centred, and in Arial font?
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.recap: Font-size sets how big the text looks. Text-align sets where it sits. Font family sets which letters it uses.
gui.tutorials.html_css_intro.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssIntro.heading: Drag a heading inside the body. Keep level 1 and type Hello CSS. We'll change its colours next.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssIntro.style: Drag the style block inside the head. The style block is where all your CSS rules live.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssIntro.selector: Drag a selector block inside the style. Click the selector field and replace the text with h1. The selector tells CSS which elements to change.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssIntro.color: Drag a color block inside the selector. Replace red with darkblue. This sets the text colour of every h1 on the page.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssIntro.bg: Drag a background-color block below the color block, still inside the selector. Replace skyblue with lightyellow. This is the box behind the text.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssIntro.viewTab: Click the View tab. Is your heading dark blue on a light yellow background?
gui.tutorials.htmlCssIntro.recap: CSS has two parts: a selector that picks elements, and properties that change how they look. Color sets the text. Background-color sets the box behind it.
gui.tutorials.html_divs.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlDivs.dragDiv: Drag the div block inside the body. A div is a generic container — it groups things together.
gui.tutorials.htmlDivs.setClass: Click the class field on the div. Replace the text with profile-card. The class is a label — we'll use it soon to style this div with CSS.
gui.tutorials.htmlDivs.heading: Drag a heading inside the div. Set the level to 2 and type Alex.
gui.tutorials.htmlDivs.paragraph: Drag a paragraph below the heading, still inside the div. Type I love adventures and exploring.
gui.tutorials.htmlDivs.viewTab: Click the View tab. The div itself is invisible — it's just a wrapper. The class name is waiting for CSS to make it stand out.
gui.tutorials.htmlDivs.recap: A div groups elements together. The class is a name we attach to a div so CSS can find it later and change how it looks.
gui.tutorials.html_forms.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlForms.dragForm: Drag the form block inside the body. A form collects what someone types or chooses on your page.
gui.tutorials.htmlForms.label: Drag a label block inside the form. Type Your name. The label tells the reader what to type.
gui.tutorials.htmlForms.input: Drag an input block below the label, still inside the form. Click the placeholder field and type Enter your name. The placeholder shows a hint inside the empty box.
gui.tutorials.htmlForms.button: Drag a button block below the input, still inside the form. Type Send. The button is what the reader clicks when they're done.
gui.tutorials.htmlForms.viewTab: Click the View tab. Try typing your name in the box. Do you see the placeholder hint disappear when you start typing?
gui.tutorials.htmlForms.recap: A form has three parts: a label that tells the reader what to type, an input box where they type, and a button to submit. Together they collect information from the reader.
gui.tutorials.html_headings.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlHeadings.addH1: Drag a heading block inside the body. Keep the level at 1 and type My Adventure.
gui.tutorials.htmlHeadings.addH2: Drag another heading below the first one inside the body. Click the level dropdown and choose 2. Type Chapter One.
gui.tutorials.htmlHeadings.addH3: Drag a third heading below the second one inside the body. Set the level to 3 and type The Forest.
gui.tutorials.htmlHeadings.viewTab: Click the View tab. Look at the page — which heading is biggest, and which is smallest?
gui.tutorials.htmlHeadings.recap: Headings have six levels, h1 to h6. h1 is the biggest and most important — used for the page title. You used the first three levels here. Use headings to organise your page like chapters in a book.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.intro: Every webpage on the internet — Google, YouTube, your friend's page — is HTML code under the hood. The browser reads that code and draws the page. Right-click any webpage and pick View Page Source to see it for yourself. Browsers like Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge are different programs, but they all read the same HTML — that's why we call HTML a web STANDARD. HTML itself was invented in 1989 by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN, the science lab. Same idea, same shape, still running the modern internet.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.blocksConcept: The HTML blocks you're about to use are a CodeSkool helper. Snap them together like LEGO and they generate the real HTML code for you — no typing angle brackets, no missing closing tags. The HTML tab on the right will show the code your blocks made; the View tab shows what the browser draws from that code.
gui.tutorials.html_images.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlImages.addHeading: Drag a heading inside the body. Keep the level at 1 and type My Favourite Animal.
gui.tutorials.htmlImages.dragImg: Drag the image block below the heading inside the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlImages.setSource: Click the source field on the image block, called src. Replace the text with h t t p s colon slash slash picsum dot photos slash i d slash two three seven slash four hundred slash three hundred.
gui.tutorials.htmlImages.setAlt: Click the alt field. Type A puppy looking at the camera. The alt describes the picture in words. It helps people who can't see the image understand it.
gui.tutorials.htmlImages.viewTab: Click the View tab. Do you see the puppy below your heading?
gui.tutorials.htmlImages.recap: An image has two parts: the source, called src, which tells where the picture comes from, and the alt, which describes it.
gui.tutorials.html_link_pages.boilerplat: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.header: Drop a header inside body. Inside header, drop an h1 with the text "My Adventure Site" — this title will appear on every page.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.nav: After the h1, drop a nav block. Inside nav, add three anchors with hrefs index.html, about.html, contact.html — and the texts Home, About, Contact. Right now those links don't go anywhere — we'll fix that by saving each page.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.section: After the header, drop a section. Inside it: an h2 "Welcome!" and a paragraph "This is my home page. Use the menu above to explore."
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.saveIndex: Click the Save button. Type "index" as the file name. Click Save. Your home page is now stored as index.html.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.newDoc: Click the New Document button — the page-with-plus icon. Pick HTML document. Your editor clears for a fresh page. The home page you just saved is safely stored — you'll see it again.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.about: Build the same structure as the home page — header with site title, nav with the three links, then a section. This time the section is "About Me" with text "I love exploring and building websites with CodeSkool."
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.saveAbout: Click Save. The dialog suggests "index2" because index.html already exists. Change it to "about". Click Save. Now you have two saved pages.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.contact: Click New Document again. Build the same header and nav. Section title: "Get in Touch". Paragraph: "Email me at: hello@example.com". Save as contact.html.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.files: Click the View Files button. You'll see index.html, about.html, and contact.html — three real pages stored in your browser.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.view: Open index.html and click the View tab. Click About in the menu — the page changes to your about page. Click Contact, click Home. Three pages, real navigation, no internet needed.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinkPages.recap: You built three pages, saved each one with its own filename, and the menu links between them work. That's a real multi-page website — every site you visit on the internet works exactly the same way under the hood.
gui.tutorials.html_links.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.heading: Drag a heading inside the body. Keep level 1 and type My Favourite Sites.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.dragAnchor: Drag a link block below the heading inside the body. The link block has a URL slot at the top.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.setUrl: Click the URL field at the top of the link block and replace the text with h t t p s colon slash slash en dot wikipedia dot org slash wiki slash Adventure.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.addText: Drag a text block inside the link — between the link's open and close. Type Wikipedia Adventure. This is the words your reader clicks.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.ul: Now let's turn many links into a menu. Drag a ul block below the link inside the body. The ul will hold each menu item.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.home: Drag a li inside the ul. Drag a link block inside the li, set its URL to index.html, and put a text block inside the link saying Home.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.about: Drag another li below the first inside the ul. Add a link with URL about.html and a text block saying About Us.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.contact: Add a third li inside the ul, with a link to contact.html and a text block saying Contact.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.viewTab: Click the View tab. You should see one Wikipedia link at the top, and a menu with Home, About Us, and Contact below it.
gui.tutorials.htmlLinks.recap: A link has two parts — the URL it goes to, and the text people click. Put many links inside a ul to build a menu. Real websites use this same recipe to connect their pages.
gui.tutorials.html_lists.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlLists.dragUl: Drag the list block, called ul, inside the body. This creates an empty list.
gui.tutorials.htmlLists.li1: Drag a list item block, called li, inside the ul. Drag a text block inside the li and type Tent. Each li becomes one bullet.
gui.tutorials.htmlLists.li2: Drag another li below the first one, still inside the ul. Drag a text block inside the li and type Compass.
gui.tutorials.htmlLists.li3: Drag a third li inside the ul, below the others. Drag a text block inside it and type Snacks.
gui.tutorials.htmlLists.viewTab: Click the View tab. Do you see three bullets stacked one below the other?
gui.tutorials.htmlLists.recap: A list has two parts: the ul holds the list, and each li is one item. Use ol for numbered lists when order matters.
gui.tutorials.html_make_it_happen.boiler: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.header: Drag a header inside the body. Inside the header, drag a nav block. Inside nav, drag four link blocks one after another, with text blocks inside saying Home, About, Projects, and Contact.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.hero: Drag a section below the header. Set its class to hero. Inside it, drag a heading and type Create Your Future, then a paragraph saying Build today, change tomorrow. Then drag a link block, set its class to btn, and put a text block inside saying Get Started.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.features: Drag a second section below the hero. Set its class to features. Inside it, drag three div blocks each with class card. In each card, add a heading at level 2 and a paragraph — Innovate / Ideas into action, Create / With purpose, and Inspire / Empower others.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.footer: Drag a footer below the features section inside the body. Drag a paragraph inside the footer and type Code today, change tomorrow.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.style: Drag the style block inside the head. Time to make this site shine.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.heroCss: Drag a selector inside style. Change its text to .hero. Inside it, add a background-color set to mediumpurple, a color set to white, a text-align set to center, and a padding set to 60px.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.btnCss: Drag another selector. Change its text to .btn. Inside it, add a background-color set to orange, a color set to white, and a padding set to 12px.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.featuresC: Drag another selector. Change its text to .features. Inside it, add a display set to flex, a gap set to 20px, and a padding set to 30px.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.cardCss: Drag a final selector. Change its text to .card. Inside it, add a padding set to 20px and a background-color set to lavender.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.viewTab: Click the View tab. This is your capstone — a real, modern-looking website with a navigation menu, a hero, three feature cards, and a footer.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.recap: You've gone from Hello Web to a full styled website. Every site you'll ever build uses the same recipe — HTML for structure, CSS for style, and your imagination for everything in between. The third layer is JavaScript — it adds BEHAVIOUR (buttons that DO things, animations, games that respond). That's a whole new track when you are ready. And when you want to look up exactly how a tag or property works, MDN (developer.mozilla.org) is the reference every web developer uses — bookmark it.
gui.tutorials.html_make_it_yours.boilerp: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItYours.header: Drag a header inside the body. Set the header's class to site-header. Drag a heading inside it and type My Website.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItYours.hero: Drag a section below the header. Set its class to hero. Inside it, drag a heading at level 2 and type Welcome! Then drag a link block, set its class to btn, and put a text block inside saying See My Work.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItYours.styleHeade: Drag the style block inside the head. Drag a selector inside style and change its text to .site-header. Drag a background-color block inside the selector and set it to navy. Drag a color block and set it to white. Add a padding block set to 20px.
