gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItYours.styleHero: Drag another selector below the first one. Change its text to .hero. Inside it, add a text-align block set to center and a padding block set to 40px.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItYours.styleBtn: Drag a third selector. Change its text to .btn. Inside it, add a background-color set to orange, a color set to white, and a padding set to 10px.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItYours.viewTab: Click the View tab. Do you see the navy header, the centred welcome message, and the orange button?
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItYours.recap: Classes like site-header, hero, and btn are labels you put on HTML blocks. CSS uses those labels to style each part of your site exactly the way you want.
gui.tutorials.html_paragraphs.boilerplat: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlParagraphs.addP1: Drag a paragraph into the body and type I love adventures.
gui.tutorials.htmlParagraphs.addP2: Drag another paragraph below the first one inside the body. Type I want to explore new places.
gui.tutorials.htmlParagraphs.addP3: Add one more paragraph below the second one inside the body. Type Tomorrow I will start my journey.
gui.tutorials.htmlParagraphs.viewTab: Click the View tab. Look at the page — are your sentences separated into blocks? Do you see space between them?
gui.tutorials.htmlParagraphs.recap: Each paragraph is a block of writing. The browser adds space between them so your text is easy to read. Use one paragraph for each idea.
gui.tutorials.html_save_page.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlSavePage.title: Drop a title block inside head. Type "My First Saved Page" — this is the name shown in the browser tab.
gui.tutorials.htmlSavePage.heading: Drop a heading inside body and type "Hello from my own webpage!".
gui.tutorials.htmlSavePage.paragraph: Add a paragraph after the heading: "I built this with CodeSkool and saved it."
gui.tutorials.htmlSavePage.clickSave: Up in the toolbar, click the Save button — the floppy-disk icon. Or press Ctrl+S. A naming dialog opens.
gui.tutorials.htmlSavePage.dialog: Type "index" as the file name. The dropdown next to it is already set to .html. Click Save. Your page is now stored in your browser as index.html.
gui.tutorials.htmlSavePage.viewFiles: Click the View Files button — the list icon in the toolbar. You'll see index.html in your saved files. Come back to this page anytime.
gui.tutorials.htmlSavePage.recap: You built a page, saved it as index.html, and opened the file list to see it. Saved pages are real .html files inside your browser — ready for the next tutorial where you'll link multiple pages together.
gui.tutorials.html_sections.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlSections.header: Drag a header inside the body. Inside the header, drag a heading and type My Awesome Website. The header sits at the top of every page.
gui.tutorials.htmlSections.nav: Drag a nav block below the heading, still inside the header. Inside the nav, drag three link blocks one after another with text blocks inside saying Home, About, and Contact.
gui.tutorials.htmlSections.welcome: Drag a section block below the header inside the body. Inside the section, drag a heading at level 2 and type Welcome! Then add a paragraph that says This is my first real-looking website.
gui.tutorials.htmlSections.whatIDo: Drag another section below the first one. Inside, drag a heading at level 2 and type What I Do, then a paragraph saying I build websites that help people.
gui.tutorials.htmlSections.footer: Drag a footer below the last section inside the body. Drag a paragraph inside it and type Built with care, 2026.
gui.tutorials.htmlSections.viewTab: Click the View tab. Do you see a header on top, a navigation menu, two sections in the middle, and a footer at the bottom?
gui.tutorials.htmlSections.recap: Header, nav, section, and footer are called semantic tags because their names describe what's inside. They help browsers, search engines, and screen readers understand your page.
gui.tutorials.html_tables.boilerplate: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlTables.dragTable: Drag the table block inside the body. A table holds data in rows and columns.
gui.tutorials.htmlTables.headerRow: Drag a tr block inside the table. tr stands for table row — this will be the header row.
gui.tutorials.htmlTables.headerCells: Drag a th block inside the tr. Drag a text block inside the th and type Name. Then drag another th beside it inside the same tr, drag a text block inside, and type Species. th stands for table header cell.
gui.tutorials.htmlTables.dataRow: Drag another tr block below the header row, still inside the table. This will hold one row of data.
gui.tutorials.htmlTables.dataCells: Drag a td block inside the second tr. Drag a text block inside it and type Pingu. Then drag another td beside it, drag a text block inside, and type Penguin. td stands for table data cell.
gui.tutorials.htmlTables.viewTab: Click the View tab. Do you see two rows and two columns of data?
gui.tutorials.htmlTables.recap: A table is built from rows. Each tr is a row. Inside each row, th is a header cell and td is a data cell. Tables show data in rows and columns.
gui.tutorials.html_text_formatting.boile: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlTextFormatting.heading: Drag a heading inside the body. Keep level 1 and type Bold, Italic, Underline.
gui.tutorials.htmlTextFormatting.bold: Drag the b block below the heading inside the body. Drag a text block inside the b and type This is bold text. Whatever sits inside b becomes thicker on the page.
gui.tutorials.htmlTextFormatting.italic: Drag an i block below the b block, still inside the body. Drag a text block inside the i and type This is italic text. Italic makes the letters slant.
gui.tutorials.htmlTextFormatting.underli: Drag a u block below the i block, still inside the body. Drag a text block inside the u and type This is underlined text. The u block draws a line under whatever is inside.
gui.tutorials.htmlTextFormatting.viewTab: Click the View tab. Do you see one bold line, one italic line, and one underlined line?
gui.tutorials.htmlTextFormatting.recap: Use b for bold, i for italic, and u for underlined. There are also strong and em tags that look the same as b and i but tell screen readers the words are important.
gui.tutorials.html_upload_image.boilerpl: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlUploadImage.heading: Drop a heading inside body and type "My Pet" — that's the title above your photo.
gui.tutorials.htmlUploadImage.dragImg: Snap an img block after the heading. Right now it points at a placeholder picture — let's swap it for one of your own.
gui.tutorials.htmlUploadImage.uploadClic: Up in the toolbar there's an Upload Image button — the icon with a picture and a plus. Click it. Your computer's file picker opens.
gui.tutorials.htmlUploadImage.pickFile: Pick any photo from your computer — your pet, your toy, a drawing — and confirm. CodeSkool saves it to your project.
gui.tutorials.htmlUploadImage.setSrc: In the img block, type the filename you just uploaded (like my-pet.jpg) into the src field. The picture you uploaded now points to your photo.
gui.tutorials.htmlUploadImage.alt: Type a short description into the alt field — "A photo of my pet". This shows up if the picture can't load and helps screen readers describe the image.
gui.tutorials.htmlUploadImage.view: Click the View tab. Your actual picture is on your webpage. That's a real photo from YOUR computer rendered inside the browser.
gui.tutorials.htmlUploadImage.recap: You uploaded an image, named it, used it in img src, and saw it on your page. Anything you can take a photo of can now go on a webpage you make.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.addBuzzer: Click the plus button again, choose Buzzer, set pin to 3, click Save. The virtual buzzer appears next to the sensor — same pin our tone and noTone blocks are sending to. Hardware ready. Code ready. Time to run it.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.addSensor: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose Distance Sensor. The dialog asks for two pins this time, not one — set trig to 9 and echo to 10, and click Save. The virtual ultrasonic sensor appears, wired up to the same two pins your code is talking to.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.buzzerPin: Drag a third pinMode block. Set pin to 3 and mode to output. Pin 3 is for the buzzer — that's the part that's actually going to make the beeping noise. Output, because we want to send a signal out to it. Three pinModes total — two for the sensor, one for the buzzer. Setup is done.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.closeBeep: In the inner else branch — the one that runs when distance is 30 or less — drop a single tone block on pin 3 at 1500 hertz. No delay, no noTone. The buzzer just holds one continuous high-pitched note. That's the stop right now scream. Three branches, three rhythms: slow beep when far away, fast beep when getting closer, solid tone when too close.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.echoPinMo: Drag a second pinMode block. Set pin to 10 and mode to input. Pin 10 is the echo pin — and yes, the names trig and echo are clues. One sends, one listens. Two pins, working as a pair.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.experimen: Try this: add an LED on pin 5, give it a pinMode, and turn it on inside the close branch — now you have a buzzer and a warning light when the wall is too near. Or, instead of three fixed branches, use the map block to turn distance into a delay value smoothly — the closer the wall, the shorter the gap between beeps, with no sudden jumps. Same hardware, smarter behaviour.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.fastBody: In this if half, tone on pin 3 at 800 hertz, delay 200 milliseconds, noTone, delay 200 milliseconds. Same shape as before, just much shorter pauses — that's what makes it sound urgent.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.fastBuild: Inside the else branch of the first if-else, drop another if-else. The new question: distance greater than 30. We already know it's not greater than 100 — that case was handled above — so this catches anywhere from 31 up to 100 centimetres.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.intro: You've heard it a hundred times — the beep that starts slow when you back into a parking spot, gets faster as the bumper gets closer to the wall, and turns into one solid scream right before you hit. Today you build that. Three behaviours from one sensor and one buzzer. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode, and the Distance Sensor extension is added in Sensor Manager. If you haven't done the earlier Arduino tutorials, finish Blink an LED and Button and LED first. If adding extensions is new to you, the Move a Servo tutorial walks you through Sensor Manager.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.loopDelay: At the very bottom of loop — after the if-else chain ends — drag a delay block and set it to 50 milliseconds. Without this, loop would run thousands of times a second, asking the sensor for distance more often than it can answer. 50 milliseconds gives the sensor time to chirp and listen. Twenty distance checks a second is more than enough to feel instant.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.makeVar: Open the Variables category and click Make a Variable. Name it distance. A variable is just a labelled box where the program can store a number and read it back later. Each loop, we're going to ask the sensor how far the wall is and stash that number in the distance box. Then the rest of the program reads from the box to decide what to do.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.moveWall: The distance sensor in the simulator has a slider — that's how you pretend a wall is moving closer or further. Slide it all the way out: slow beep, long pauses. Slide it into the middle range: fast beep, short pauses. Slide it close: one solid scream. Three rhythms from one sensor, just by reading distance and choosing a branch.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.mystery: Hold on a second. Your eyes work because light bounces off things and lands in them. But the Arduino has no eyes, no camera, no lens. So how on earth is it about to tell us how far away the wall is? The names trig and echo were the hint. Think for a moment before the next step gives it away.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.readDista: First, register the sensor in setup. Make a variable called sensorInit. Drag a 'set sensorInit to' block under the buzzer pinMode and from the Sensors category drop a distance sensor reporter into the value slot — set trig to 9 and echo to 10. The reporter creates a sensor and your variable now holds a reference to it. Now inside loop, drag a set distance to block, and from Sensors drop the measure distance reporter into its value slot, again with trig 9 and echo 10. Every loop the sensor fires a chirp, listens for the echo, and the answer in centimetres lands inside the distance box.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.realWorld: Every modern car with reverse parking sensors does exactly this. So does the touchless soap dispenser, the automatic door at the supermarket, the robot vacuum that doesn't bump into chairs. Sensor checks distance, program decides what to do, output reacts — same shape, different jobs. Once you've built it once, you start spotting it everywhere.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.recap: Look at what you just learned. Ultrasonic sensing — chirp out, echo in, time-of-flight gives distance. Multi-branch decisions — if-elseif-else lets your program pick from three or more paths instead of just two. Tone variation — same buzzer, different frequencies and different delay rhythms make wildly different sounds. And sound-based sensing — a real, working alternative to eyes that you can program in twenty steps. Every parking sensor on every car on every road runs on this idea. You can build it now.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.reveal: It uses sound. The trig pin shouts a tiny chirp — too high-pitched for you to hear, but a real chirp. The chirp travels through the air, hits the wall, and bounces back. The echo pin listens for the bounce. The Arduino measures the time between shout and echo, multiplies by the speed of sound, and divides by two — because the chirp went there and back. That's the distance. Bats and dolphins navigate this exact way; we just borrowed their trick.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've done this a few times now — setup runs once at the start, loop runs forever while the Arduino has power. Everything we build today goes inside one of those two slots.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.slowBody: In the if branch, drop a tone block on pin 3 at 500 hertz, then a delay of 800 milliseconds, then a noTone block on pin 3, then another delay of 800 milliseconds. Beep — long pause — beep — long pause. That's the relaxed, you've-got-room rhythm.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.slowBuild: Drag an if-else block under the set-distance line. In the diamond, build the question distance greater than 100 — meaning the wall is more than 100 centimetres away. The next step fills in what to do when the answer is yes.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds to spin up. The simulator goes live.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.threeRang: Pause. We want three different sounds based on how far the wall is. Far away — beep slowly, plenty of room. Getting closer — beep faster, you should slow down. Too close — solid tone, stop right now. Three ranges of distance, three rhythms. We'll build them with a chain of if-else blocks: ask one question, and if the answer is no, ask the next.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.trigPinMo: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 is going to be our trig pin — short for trigger. We'll see what it triggers in just a moment, but the program needs to know up front that pin 9 is going to send something out.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.addLed: Click the plus button again, choose LED, set pin to 9, click Save. A real fan would be a motor — but the simulator uses an LED as a stand-in. Brightness will represent speed: dim LED equals slow fan, bright LED equals fast fan. We'll come back to the real-motor story at the end. The code works the same either way.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.addPot: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose Potentiometer. In the pin dialog, choose A0 and click Save. A potentiometer is the proper name for a knob — a turnable dial that gives back a different number depending on where it's pointing. The virtual knob appears in the simulator, wired to the same A0 pin your code is reading.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.analogWrit: Drag an analogWrite block below the set fanSpeed line. Set the pin to 9 and drop the fanSpeed variable into the value slot. analogWrite is the same block from led-glow-effect — it tells a PWM pin to be partly on. 0 means fully off, 255 means fully on, and every number in between is a different brightness, or in our case, a different fan speed.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.delay: Drag a delay block at the bottom of loop. Set it to 250 milliseconds. A loop that runs flat-out with no pause works the Arduino harder than it needs to. 250 milliseconds is fast enough that the fan still feels responsive when you turn the knob, and slow enough that the loop has room to breathe. Every tutorial loop you write should have one of these at the end — it's a habit worth keeping.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.everywhere: Once you spot the map pattern, you start seeing it everywhere. A game controller's joystick reports raw numbers — the game maps them into where your character should look. A thermostat reads a temperature sensor and maps it into how hard the fan should blow. A stage light dimmer reads a slider and maps it into bulb brightness. Different sensors, different outputs, same idea — translate one range into another.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.experiment: Try this. Swap the last two map numbers — change 0 and 255 to 255 and 0. Now the knob works backwards: turn it right, the LED dims; turn it left, it brightens. Or change the output range to 0 and 100 — the LED never gets fully bright, no matter how far you turn the knob. Or set the output range to 0 and 50 to make the LED stay very dim. Same five blocks, totally different feel.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.intro: Every ceiling fan with a dimmer, every old radio volume knob, every car stereo — they all do the same thing. You twist a knob, and something speeds up or slows down. Today you'll teach an LED to do that. Turn the knob — the light dims and brightens, smooth as a sunrise. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done the led-glow-effect tutorial yet, do that one first — you'll need analogWrite and PWM ideas from there.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.makeKnob: Open the Variables category and click Make a Variable. Name it knobValue. A variable is a labelled box that holds a number we can read and change. Each loop, we'll read the knob's position and stash it into knobValue, so the rest of the program has something to work with.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.makeSpeed: Open Variables again and Make a Variable named fanSpeed. This box will hold the translated number — the version that fits the 0 to 255 range that analogWrite needs. Two variables, two jobs: knobValue holds the raw reading, fanSpeed holds the version we'll send to the fan.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.meetMap: There's a block built exactly for this — it's called map. Think of map as a range translator. You hand it a number from one range, and it scales it into a matching number in a different range. With map, 0 stays 0, the top of the input range becomes the top of the output range, and halfway in becomes halfway out. The map block takes five inputs: the number to translate, the lowest input, the highest input, the lowest output, and the highest output.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.mismatch: Here's a problem. analogRead gives us a number from 0 to 1023. But analogWrite — the block that controls the fan's speed — only accepts 0 to 255. The ranges don't match. If we just feed the knob's number straight into analogWrite, the fan would max out at one quarter of the way through the knob's travel, and stay at full speed for the rest. The knob would feel broken.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.motorCavea: One last thing. A real fan motor pulls more power than an Arduino pin can give it directly — so on a real desk fan, you'd add a small helper chip called a transistor between the pin and the motor. The Arduino tells the transistor what to do, and the transistor handles the heavy lifting. We use an LED here because the simulator can show brightness changing and that's enough to learn the idea. The code you wrote today is identical to the code that would drive a real fan. Same five blocks, same logic — just a different thing on the other end of pin 9.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.pinMode: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 is one of the special PWM pins — you saw this in led-glow-effect, the tutorial where the LED breathed in and out. PWM lets a pin be more than just on or off — it can be anywhere in between. That's exactly what we need to make a fan slow, medium, or fast.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.readKnob: Drag a set knobValue block into loop. Drop an analogRead block into its value slot, and set the pin to A0. analogRead is the sensing block we used in night-lamp — it reads a sensor and gives back a number from 0 to 1023. With a knob on A0, all the way left is 0, all the way right is 1023, and every position in between is somewhere in that range.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.recap: Look at what you just built. analogRead reads a knob's position as a number from 0 to 1023. map translates that number into a different range. analogWrite uses PWM to set a pin anywhere between fully off and fully on. Three blocks, working together, turn a knob into a smooth dimmer. Range translation is the secret ingredient of almost every program that talks to a sensor. You'll use map again and again.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.setSpeed: Drag a set fanSpeed block under the set knobValue line. Drop a map block into its value slot. Drop the knobValue variable into map's first slot. Set the next two slots to 0 and 1023 — that's the input range, what analogRead can give us. Set the last two slots to 0 and 255 — that's the output range, what analogWrite expects. Read it as one sentence: take knobValue from the range zero to one-thousand-twenty-three, and squeeze it into the range zero to two-hundred-fifty-five.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've done this a few times now — setup runs once at the start, loop runs forever. The fan-speed program lives almost entirely inside loop, because we need to keep checking the knob over and over.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.turnKnob: Click and drag the potentiometer in the simulator. Turn it slowly from one end to the other. The LED dims and brightens smoothly with the knob — slow at one end, full bright at the other, every shade in between. That's analogRead reading the knob, map translating the range, and analogWrite setting the brightness — about four times every second.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.whyMap: Try a thought experiment. If we'd skipped the map block and sent knobValue straight into analogWrite, what would happen? analogWrite tops out at 255 — feed it a bigger number and the LED just stays at full brightness. So the fan would race up to full speed by the time you'd turned the knob just one quarter of the way, and then stay stuck at full for the other three quarters. The knob would feel broken. map is what makes the whole knob useful.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.addSensor: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose PIR. We're using the PIR sensor as a stand-in for an IR hand sensor — same idea for the program: pin goes high when something is detected, low when not. Set pin to 2 and click Save.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.addServo: Click the plus button again, choose Servo, set pin to 10, click Save. The virtual servo appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same pin your servo write blocks are commanding.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.cooldown: Drag another delay block, still inside the if, below the second servo write. Set it to 2000 milliseconds — that's two seconds. Imagine someone leaves their hand under the nozzle. Without this pause, the loop would race round and round, firing a hundred squirts a second and emptying the bottle. Two seconds gives the user time to pull their hand away before the next squirt.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.dropEquals: Open the Operators category. Drag an equals block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if. The equals block compares two things; we'll fill in what to compare in the next two steps.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.dropHigh: Still in the Arduino category, drag a high block. Drop it into the right slot of the equals block. Your question now reads: 'is pin 2 high right now?' When the sensor sees a hand, it sets pin 2 to high — the answer becomes yes, and the if's inside runs.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.dropRead: Back in the Arduino category, drag a digitalRead block. Drop it into the left slot of the equals block. Set the pin to 2. digitalRead checks what state pin 2 is in right now and reports back high or low.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.hold: Drag a delay block right after the servo write, still inside the if. Set it to 500 milliseconds. The servo needs a moment to actually swing all the way down and squeeze a proper squirt out. Without this pause, the next line would run instantly and the pump would barely move.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.if: We want the pump to react when the sensor sees a hand, and stay still when it doesn't. That's an if-block — same one you've seen in Scratch. Open the Control category and drag an if-block into the loop section. Whatever question we put in its diamond slot decides whether the inside runs.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.intro: Remember those automatic hand sanitizer dispensers that popped up everywhere during COVID? You wave your hand under the nozzle and a squirt of sanitizer lands in your palm — no touching, no pressing, no germs passed between hands. Today you'll build that exact behavior. A sensor watches for a hand. When it sees one, a servo presses the pump. Wave away — pump rests. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode, and the Servo Motor extension is added in Sensor Manager. If you haven't done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first. If adding extensions is new to you, the Move a Servo tutorial walks you through Sensor Manager.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.loopDelay: Drag one more delay block, this time at the very bottom of loop, outside the if. Set it to 50 milliseconds. This is the small heartbeat that keeps the loop tidy — checking the sensor about twenty times a second. Plenty fast for spotting a hand, but not so fast that the program is hammering the sensor pointlessly.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.mystery: Here's the puzzle. The sensor never touches your hand. Your hand never touches the sensor. So how does the program know a hand is there? Buttons need a press. Switches need a flip. This sensor needs neither. Hold that question — we'll answer it on the next slide.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.pinModeSenso: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and mode to input. Pin 2 will listen for the sensor — input means the pin is listening for voltage coming in, not sending it out.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.press: Drag a servo write block into the if branch. Set pin to 10 and angle to 45. When the if's question answers yes — sensor sees a hand — the servo arm swings to 45 degrees, pressing the pump down.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.recap: Look what you just put together. A sensor watches the world and reports back. An if-block decides whether to act. A servo from an earlier tutorial gets reused — no need to re-learn it, just place and command. Two delays shape the timing: one holds the pump down long enough to actually squirt, the other prevents a hundred squirts on a single hand. This trigger-and-actuate pattern — sense something, decide, do something — is the heart of automatic doors, motion lights, alarm systems, traffic sensors, and yes, every touchless dispenser you've ever waved at. You can write them now.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.rest: Drag a second servo write block into the if branch, below the delay. Pin 10, angle 0. The servo arm swings back up — pump released, ready for the next squirt.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.reveal: The trick is invisible light. The sensor quietly shines a beam of IR light — light your eyes can't see — out into the air. When nothing is in front of it, the beam just travels off into the room. When a hand gets close, the beam bounces off your skin and comes back to a tiny detector on the sensor. The detector sees the bounce and the sensor flips its output pin to high. Same idea as the television remote in your living room — that little dark window on the front is also IR. Press a button on the remote and IR light flies across the room to your television. Different job, same invisible light.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.running: Click the sensor to toggle motion on. Watch the servo arm swing down to forty-five degrees, hold for half a second, swing back to zero, then sit still for two seconds. Click the sensor again to turn motion off. Click it back on and the cycle repeats. You just built a touchless reactive program — the same trick is inside every automatic faucet, every airport hand-dryer, every supermarket sliding door.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.servoAttach: Same pattern as Move a Servo. Make a variable called servo. Drag a 'set servo to' block into setup and drop a Servo reporter from the Servo category into the value slot — the reporter creates a new servo and your variable now holds a reference to it. Drag a servo attach block right under and set the pin to 10. The Arduino now knows a servo lives on pin 10.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Setup runs once when the program starts — that's where we'll prepare our pins. Loop runs forever — that's where we'll watch for a hand and press the pump.
gui.tutorials.handSanitizer.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.addLed: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LED. In the pin dialog, choose pin 9 and click Save. The virtual LED appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same PWM pin your code is feeding numbers to.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.analogWriteD: Drag another analogWrite block inside the new repeat. Set pin to 9. Drop the brightness variable into the value slot, just like before. When this second repeat starts, brightness will already be at 255 from the end of the fade-up — so the first thing this block does is shine the LED at full brightness.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.analogWriteU: Back to the Arduino category. Drag an analogWrite block inside the repeat. Set its pin to 9. For the value, drag the orange brightness variable block from Variables and drop it into the value slot. Now the line reads: send the current brightness number to pin 9. First time through the repeat, brightness is 0 — LED is dark. We're about to make it climb.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.breathing: Look at the LED in the simulator. It glows from dark, climbs slowly to fully bright, then fades back down to dark, over and over. No flicker. No steps you can see. Just a calm, breathing glow — even though the pin underneath is only ever fully on or fully off, switching between them faster than your eye can catch.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.changeDown: Drag a change brightness by block underneath the analogWrite. This time, change the value from 1 to negative 1. Just type a minus sign before the 1. Adding negative one is the same as subtracting one — so the brightness box now counts down: 255, then 254, then 253, all the way back to 0.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.changeUp: From the Variables category, drag a change brightness by block underneath the analogWrite, still inside the repeat. Leave the value at 1. Each time around the repeat, this block adds one to the box. So the brightness climbs: 0, then 1, then 2, then 3, all the way up to 255.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.delayDown: Drag one more delay block underneath, still inside the second repeat. Set it to 8 milliseconds, just like before. Same reason — give your eyes time to follow the fade. Now the loop will fade up for about 2 seconds, then fade down for about 2 seconds, then wrap around and do it forever. The blocks are done — let's plug in an LED.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.delayUp: Drag a delay block underneath the change-by block, still inside the repeat. Set it to 8 milliseconds. Why so small? The Arduino is fast — without a pause, all 256 brightness steps would finish in a blink and we'd see nothing. 8 milliseconds per step times 256 steps gives roughly 2 seconds for a smooth fade-in. Just slow enough for our eyes to catch the climb.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.intro: Have you ever noticed how the little light on a phone or laptop slowly fades in, then fades out, like it's breathing? That gentle glow is everywhere — sleep buttons, mood lamps, indicator lights on speakers. Today you'll teach an LED that exact trick: fade up smoothly to full brightness, fade back down to dark, again and again. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.makeVariable: Open the Variables category. Click Make a Variable. Type the name brightness and click ok. A new set of orange blocks appears — these are how the program writes to the box, reads from it, and changes its value.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.mystery: Stop and think for a second. digitalWrite only has two settings: high (LED bright) and low (LED dark). There's no in-between. So how do you get half-bright? Or one-tenth bright? With only on and off to work with, fading should be impossible. But your phone's breathing light proves it isn't. So what's the trick?
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.pinMode: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. We're picking pin 9 on purpose — not every pin on the Arduino can do what we're about to do. Pin 9 is one of a handful of pins with a special power, and you'll find out why in a moment.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.pwm: Here's the trick. The pin really is only on or off — but it switches between them thousands of times per second. If it's on most of the time, the LED looks bright. If it's on only a tiny fraction of the time, the LED looks dim. Your eye can't follow the flicker, so it averages everything together into one steady glow. This is called PWM, short for pulse-width modulation. There's a special block for it: analogWrite. You give it a pin number and a brightness from 0 to 255. Zero means fully off. 255 means fully on. 128 means halfway. Only PWM-capable pins like pin 9 can do this — that's why we picked it.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.recap: Five minutes ago, fading an LED looked impossible. Now it's easy. You learned PWM — the trick of pulsing a pin so fast that on and off blur into in-between brightness, controlled with analogWrite from 0 to 255. You learned variables — a named box the program uses to remember a number across loops. You learned the repeat block — say it once, the Arduino does it many times. And you learned fade math — climb the number up, then walk it back down, and what you get is a smooth breathing glow. PWM is everywhere: phone screen brightness, RGB lights, fan speed control, motor speed in toys and drones — all the same trick you just used.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.repeatBlock: Open the Control category. Drag a repeat block into the loop section of setup-and-loop. Change its number from 10 to 256. Anything we put inside its mouth will run 256 times before the loop wraps around.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.repeatDown: Drag another repeat block into loop, just below the first repeat. Change its number to 256 as well. We're going to fill its mouth with the same kinds of blocks as before, but with one important change in the third one.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.repeatIntro: We want to fade from 0 all the way up to 255 — that's 256 brightness steps. Imagine writing 256 nearly-identical blocks by hand. Painful. Lucky for us, there's a block that says 'do this thing 256 times'. It's called repeat. You drop it once, put your action inside it, and the Arduino does the work.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.reverseIntro: We've taught the LED to climb from dark to full bright. But for it to breathe, it has to come back down too. So we'll add a second repeat right after the first — same idea, just walking the brightness number back down from 255 to 0. Same shape, opposite direction.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.setToZero: Drag a set brightness to block into setup, just below the pinMode. Type 0 in the slot. We start the box at 0 — the LED will be fully dark before the fade begins. We put this in setup, not loop, because we only want to start at zero once when the program turns on.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've seen this before: setup runs once when the program starts, and loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.variablesInt: To fade smoothly, we don't want to jump from 0 to 255 in one go — we want to go 0, then 1, then 2, then 3, all the way up to 255. That means the program has to remember which number it's on right now, so the next loop knows to add one. A box that remembers a number for us is called a variable. Let's make one.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.addLeft: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose Push Button. Set pin to 2 and click Save. The simulator doesn't have an actual IR sensor component, so we use a push button as a stand-in for the left eye. Pressing the button is the same as the eye seeing the black line — it pulls pin 2 low. Not pressing means the eye is over white floor.
Click the plus button and choose LED. Pin 9, Save. This LED is the left motor stand-in. Bright glow means the motor is running at speed 200. Dark means the motor is stopped — robot turning.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.addRigh: Click the plus button again and choose Push Button. Set pin to 3, click Save. That's the right eye — same trick, different pin. Two buttons in the simulator standing in for two IR sensors on the real robot.
One more LED — plus button, choose LED, pin 10, Save. Right motor stand-in. Two buttons for the eyes, two LEDs for the motors. The whole robot is on screen.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.buildLe: Open Control and drag a plain if block below the two analogWrites. Then build its diamond slot the way you've done before — drop an equals block in, put digitalRead pin 2 on the left, and a low block on the right. The question reads: is the left eye seeing the line right now?
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.buildRi: Drag a second if block below the first. Build its diamond the same way — equals, digitalRead pin 3 on the left, low on the right. This question is the mirror image of the left-eye one: is the right eye seeing the line right now?
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.concept: Pause for a moment. If the left eye is reading low, that means it's sitting over the dark line — and the line has drifted to the left side of the robot. So the robot needs to steer left to follow it. We do that by stopping just the left motor; the right motor keeps spinning, and the robot pivots around its left wheel. The next step writes that into code.

Same shape as before, mirrored. If the right eye is reading low, the line has drifted to the right side of the robot — so we steer right by stopping the right motor. Left motor keeps spinning, robot pivots around its right wheel. Next step writes that.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.delay: Drag a delay block at the very bottom of the loop. Set it to 50 milliseconds. That's a tiny pause — twenty times per second, the robot reads the eyes, picks a direction, and adjusts the motors. Fast enough to follow curves, slow enough not to peg the Arduino. Every loop should have at least a small delay.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.driveSt: Drag an analogWrite block into the top of the loop. Set pin to 9, value to 200. analogWrite sends a number from 0 to 255 — zero is stopped, 255 is full speed. We're picking 200, which is a nice cruising speed. This sets the left motor running every loop, before any decisions get made.

Drag a second analogWrite right below the first. Pin 10, value 200. Both motors running at speed 200 — left and right matched. When both wheels spin the same speed, the robot rolls straight ahead. Now we'll add the steering logic underneath.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.intro: Picture a little robot, no remote, no driver — just rolling along a black line painted on the floor. It steers around curves, stays on track, and never falls off. Today you'll teach one to do exactly that. Two eyes underneath, two motors, and a handful of decisions in code. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you've finished the earlier tutorials in this series, you've already met every block we'll use today — we're just putting them together in a new shape.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.motorSt: A quick heads-up about the simulator. Real motors need a special chip called a motor driver — the Arduino can't power them directly. To keep the focus on the programming and not on wiring chips, the simulator swaps the motors for LEDs. Bright LED means the motor is spinning fast. Dim LED means it's slowing down. Dark LED means the motor is stopped. The code you write today is exactly the same code that would drive a real two-wheeled robot — only the components in the simulator are swapped.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.mystery: Quick puzzle. The robot has no camera. It has no eyeballs. So how does it actually see the line on the floor? It can't take a photo, it can't recognise shapes — and yet somehow it knows which way to turn. Hold that question for a moment. The answer is simpler than you'd guess, and it's coming up next.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.pinMode: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and mode to input pullup. Pin 2 is the robot's left eye — it'll listen for what the sensor underneath the robot is reporting. input pullup keeps the reading stable, the same way it did for the button in the earlier tutorial.

Drag a third pinMode. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 is the left motor. We picked pin 9 because it's a PWM pin — that lets us control motor speed, not just on or off. You met PWM in the LED glow tutorial; same idea here, but driving a motor instead of fading a light.

Drag another pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 3 and mode to input pullup. Pin 3 is the right eye. Two sensors, two pins — the robot will compare what each one reports and steer based on the difference.
One more pinMode. Set pin to 10 and mode to output. Pin 10 is the right motor — also a PWM pin. Two eyes listening on pins 2 and 3, two motors talking on pins 9 and 10. Setup is done.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.reveal: Here's the answer. The robot has two tiny IR sensors pointing down at the floor. Each one shines invisible infrared light at the ground and reads how much bounces back. Black floor absorbs the light — almost nothing bounces back, so the sensor reads low. White floor reflects the light — lots bounces back, so the sensor reads high. That's it. No camera, no AI — just two sensors saying bright or dark, many times every second. The Arduino reads those two numbers and decides where to steer.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.running: Press neither button. Both LEDs glow at the same brightness — the robot is driving straight. Now press and hold the left button. The left LED goes dark while the right one stays glowing — the left motor stopped, the right motor kept spinning, so the robot is turning left. Let go, press the right button — right LED goes dark, robot turns right. You just built a line follower. Real robots scale this up — a third eye in the centre for sharper turns, a camera to spot intersections, a distance sensor to brake before obstacles. The same shape every time: sense, decide, drive. Five minutes ago you saw a black line on a floor and wondered how a robot could possibly follow it. Now you've written the brain that does it. Two sensors, two motors, two if blocks, and one delay — that's a self-driving robot.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.setupLo: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You know the drill by now — setup runs once when the program starts, loop runs forever after that. Our robot's brain lives inside loop: read the eyes, decide which way to go, drive the motors, repeat — many times every second.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.stopLef: Drag an analogWrite into the if branch — the slot inside the if block. Pin 9, value 0. That stops the left motor. The right motor is still spinning at 200, so the robot pivots: right wheel pushing forward, left wheel still — the robot rotates to the left, exactly what we want when the left eye spots the line.
gui.tutorials.lineFollowingRobot.stopRig: Drag an analogWrite into the second if branch. Pin 10, value 0. Right motor stops, left motor keeps spinning — robot pivots to the right. Two ifs, mirror images of each other. Each loop: drive both motors at 200, then check each eye and stop the matching motor if that eye sees the line. That's the entire steering brain.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.addSensor: Click the Add Sensor button at the bottom of the dialog. The dialog closes and a new Servo category shows up in your block toolbox on the left, full of servo-specific blocks ready to drag. From now on, the routine for adding any extension is the same: Board menu, Sensor Manager, Arduino tab, click the card you want, click Add Sensor. Five clicks. Done once, used forever.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.addServo: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose Servo. In the pin dialog, set the pin to 10 and click Save. The virtual servo appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same pin your code is talking to.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.arduinoTab: At the top of the Sensor Manager dialog there are three tabs — Blocks, Arduino, and MicroPython. Click the Arduino tab. That tells Sensor Manager you want the C++ Arduino versions of these extensions. The grid of cards updates to show only the ones that work in Arduino mode.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.attach: Open Variables and click Make a Variable. Call it servo. Drag a 'set servo to' block into setup. Now open the Servo category — this is a new one — and drop a Servo reporter block into the value slot. The Servo reporter creates a new servo behind the scenes; assigning it to your variable means servo now holds a reference to that servo. Drag a servo attach block right under and set the pin to 10. From here on, anything you do to servo goes through pin 10.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.boardMenu: Before we can drag any servo blocks, we have to add the Servo extension to the toolbox. The toolbox starts with just the core Arduino blocks; sensors and special motors live in their own extensions. Open the Board menu at the top of the screen — it's the menu where you set the board, the upload mode, and any sensors you want to use.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.delay1: Drag a delay block under the servo write. Set it to 1000 milliseconds — that's one full second. Without this pause, the next servo write line would run instantly and the servo would never have time to actually get to 0. The delay holds the program still while the motor catches up.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.delay2: Drag another delay of 1000 milliseconds underneath. Same reason — give the servo a second to actually arrive at 90 before we tell it the next angle.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.delay3: Drag another 1000 millisecond delay. Same idea — give the servo time to land at 180.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.delay4: Drag a final 1000 millisecond delay at the bottom of the loop. This one matters for a different reason — every loop needs at least a small delay so the Arduino isn't running flat out at full speed for no reason. One second between sweeps gives the motor a moment to settle before the next round starts.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.experiments: Try this. Make the delays smaller — 200 milliseconds — and the servo whips between angles fast. Or sweep in smaller steps: 0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180 — a smoother arc. Even better, add a potentiometer on pin A0, then read the knob with analogRead and use map to turn the 0-to-1023 reading into a 0-to-180 angle. Now the servo follows your knob in real time — same idea you used in Fan Speed Control, but the output is an angle instead of brightness.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.intro: Most motors just spin — round and round, as fast as you let them. A servo is different. A servo goes to a specific angle and stays there. Tell it 90 and it goes to 90. Tell it 180 and it swings to 180. Robot arms, the steering on a remote-control car, the eyes and eyebrows on animatronic puppets — those are all servos. Today you'll make one sweep through three positions. And you'll learn something new along the way — the Servo blocks aren't loaded by default, so step one is to add them through Sensor Manager. Once you've done it here, every later tutorial that needs a sensor or a special component will just say 'add this extension' and you'll know exactly what to do. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done the earlier Arduino tutorials, finish Blink an LED and Control an LED with a Button first.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.mystery: Pause for a second. A regular motor just spins — you give it power and it goes round. So how do you tell a motor to stop at 90 degrees? Or to swing exactly halfway? Spinning is easy. Stopping at a precise angle and holding there — that's the trick. Inside a servo, something clever is happening. We'll see what in the next step.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.pickCard: Find the Servo Motor card in the grid. You can scroll, or type 'servo' in the search box at the top to jump straight to it. Click the card once — a coloured border lights up around it to show it's picked. Picking it doesn't add it yet; that's the next step.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.recap: A few minutes ago, telling a motor to stop at exactly 90 degrees sounded impossible. Now you've done it. Here's what you used. The Servo library — a bundle of helper code that handles tricky timing for you, so your program stays simple. servo attach — the line in setup that tells the library which pin the servo is plugged into. servo write — the line that sends the servo to a specific angle and holds it there. And the idea of angle-as-data — a number in your code becomes a real position in the real world. That same trick runs anything with a finger-arm motion: robot grippers, drone wings flexing, the camera gimbals on phones, the little flap inside a 3D printer that switches between filaments. You name an angle, the library handles the pulses, the motor gets there. That's it.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.reveal: Here's the secret. Inside every servo there's a tiny circuit that listens for very short electrical pulses on its signal wire. The length of each pulse tells the servo what angle to go to. A short pulse means swing one way. A longer pulse means swing the other. The Servo library writes those pulses for us, many times a second, with the exact lengths needed. We never see them. We just say 'go to 90' and the library does the rest. That's why we needed servo attach in setup — that's the library getting ready to do its pulse work.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.running: Watch the servo. It swings to 0 — pause. Around to 90 — pause. All the way to 180 — pause. Back to 90 — pause. Then the loop wraps and it starts again at 0. Four numbers in your code, four positions in the real world. The Servo library is doing the pulse work; you're just naming the angles.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.sensorManager: From the Board menu, click Sensor Manager. This is the door to every sensor, motor, display, and special component the Arduino can use. Every time a tutorial says 'add the such-and-such extension', this is where you go. A dialog opens up with a grid of cards.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've seen this before — setup runs once when the program starts, loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.write0: Drag a servo write block into the loop. Set the angle to 0. This is the line that does the work — servo write 0 tells the library to send the pulses for 'go all the way to the 0 degree position'. The servo swings there and holds.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.write180: Drag a third servo write block. Set the angle to 180. All the way around. The servo swings from 90 to 180 — the maximum a standard servo can do. Most servos cover a 180 degree arc, not a full circle.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.write90: Drag another servo write block below the delay. Set the angle to 90. Halfway. The library now sends a different pulse length, and the servo swings from 0 around to 90 degrees and holds there.
gui.tutorials.moveServo.writeBack90: One more servo write — set the angle back to 90. Now the servo swings from 180 back to the middle. When the loop wraps around to the top and runs servo write 0 again, the sweep keeps going forever — 0, 90, 180, 90, 0, 90, 180, 90, on and on.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.addLed: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LED. In the pin dialog, choose pin 9 and click Save. The virtual lamp appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same pin your code is writing high and low to.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.addPhoto: Click the plus button again and choose Photoresistor — that's the simulator's name for the LDR. Set the pin to A0 and click Save. A new component appears with a slider underneath it. The slider is how you fake a sunrise or a sunset in the simulator: drag it up for bright, drag it down for dark.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.delay: Drag a delay block to the bottom of the loop, after the if-else. Set it to 200 milliseconds. Every loop should have at least a small pause — it stops the Arduino from spinning at full speed for no reason, and 200 milliseconds is fast enough that the lamp still feels instantly responsive when the room dims.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.dropAnalogRead: Now go to the Arduino category and drag an analogRead block. Drop it into the value slot of the set-variable line. Set its pin to A0. Now every loop, the program asks the LDR what it sees and parks that number into the lightLevel box.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.experiments: Try this: change 500 to 200 and run again. The lamp now waits much longer before switching on — only the deepest darkness triggers it. Then try 800 — the lamp comes on almost any time the room dims. Or add a second LED on pin 10, with its own pinMode and its own if-else using a different threshold. Below 700, the dim-light LED comes on. Below 300, both come on — a two-stage night light that gets brighter as the room gets darker.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.ifElse: Same if-else block you used in Button and LED. Open the Control category and drag an if-else into the loop, just below the read-LDR step. The diamond slot is where our question goes. The if branch runs when the answer is yes, the else branch when the answer is no.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.intro: Bedside lamps that switch themselves on at sunset. Garden lights that wake up at dusk. Streetlamps that come alive when the sky goes orange. They all share one trick: a tiny sensor that watches the light, and a program that decides when it's dark enough. Today you'll build that exact program. If you haven't done Blink an LED and Button and LED yet, finish those first — this tutorial assumes you've met pinMode, digitalWrite, if-else, and the simulator already.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.lessThan: Open the Operators category and drag the less-than block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if-else. On the left side, drop the lightLevel variable from the Variables category. On the right side, type 500. The question now reads: is lightLevel less than 500? If yes, it's dark. If no, it's bright.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.makeVar: We need somewhere to put the LDR's number so we can use it in the next step. Open the Variables category, click Make a Variable, and name it lightLevel. A variable is just a labelled box — every loop, we'll drop the latest reading into it.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.meetAnalogRead: Here's the answer: the LDR reports a number. Not high or low — an actual number from 0 to 1023. 0 means pitch dark. 1023 means full sun. A dim room might be 200. A sunny window might be 900. Anywhere in between, anywhere on the scale. The block that reads this is called analogRead — analog meaning a smooth scale, not just two states. digitalRead gives you one of two answers; analogRead gives you one of 1024. That's the whole mystery solved.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.mystery: The light sensor we'll use is called an LDR — short for light-dependent resistor. Now think for a second. A button has two answers: pressed or not pressed. high or low. A neat little yes-or-no. But light isn't a yes-or-no. A room can be pitch dark, or sunny, or anywhere in between — dim, twilight, cloudy afternoon, lamp-lit evening. So when our program asks the LDR what it sees, what could the answer possibly be? high and low aren't enough.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.pinMode: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output — pin 9 will send voltage out to the LED. Notice we only need pinMode for the LED. The light sensor will go on an analog pin, and analog inputs configure themselves automatically. One pinMode line, that's it.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.recap: You learned three big things today. analogRead — the block that reads a sensor on a smooth scale from 0 to 1023, instead of just high or low. The threshold pattern — pick a number, compare against it, decide. And the shape underneath all of this: sensor, decision, output. Read the world. Compare. Act. That same shape runs every thermostat, every soil-moisture irrigation system, every smart streetlamp, every battery-low warning on your phone. Once you see the pattern, you'll spot it everywhere — and you can write it now.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.setVar: Drag a set-variable block into the loop. Click its dropdown and pick lightLevel — that's the box we just made. Right now the value slot just has a 0 in it; the next step swaps that for the live sensor reading.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.setupLoop: You've seen this twice now. Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Setup runs once when the program starts. Loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.slider: Click and drag the slider under the photoresistor. Push it down — the LED comes on the moment the number passes 500. Push it back up — the LED goes dark again. You're simulating the sun setting, then rising. The same code, the same threshold, every time. You just built a streetlamp.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.threshold: Now we have to make a decision. lightLevel is somewhere between 0 and 1023, but the lamp only has two states — on or off. So we have to draw a line. We pick a number — a threshold — and say: below this number, it's dark, switch the lamp on; above this number, it's bright, switch it off. We'll start with 500 — roughly the middle of the scale. Below 500 means dark, lamp on. This pattern — pick a threshold, compare against it — is one of the most common shapes in all of programming.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.why500: Here's the honest truth: 500 was a guess. It's halfway between 0 and 1023, which felt reasonable. But your bedroom isn't average — maybe your nights are very dark and 500 makes the lamp turn on too early. Maybe your curtains are thin and morning light leaks in, and 500 makes it turn off too late. In real life you'd test in your actual room and tune the number. A bigger number means the lamp comes on sooner, while there's still some light. A smaller number means the lamp waits for it to get really dark before switching on. The number isn't magic — it's a knob you adjust to your taste.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.writeHigh: Drag a digitalWrite block into the if branch — the top half. Set pin to 9 and value to high. When lightLevel is below 500, this line runs: 5 volts on pin 9, the LED lights up. The room is dark, the lamp comes on.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.writeLow: Drag another digitalWrite into the else branch — the bottom half. Pin 9, value low. When lightLevel is 500 or above, this line runs instead: 0 volts on pin 9, LED dark. The room is bright, the lamp stays off.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.addDisplay: Click the plus button again and choose 7-Segment. In the pin dialog, set the clock pin to 3 and the data pin to 4 — matching what your setup block declared. Click Save. The virtual display appears next to the virtual Arduino, ready to show numbers.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.addSensor: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose Push Button. The simulator doesn't yet have a real IR break-beam sensor component, so we'll use a push button as a stand-in. The behaviour is identical to a real beam: pin 2 reads high when nothing is pressing the button — that's the beam clear, doorway empty. Pin 2 drops to low when the button is pressed — beam broken, person walking through. In the pin dialog, set pin to 2 and click Save.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.delay: At the very end of the loop, drag a delay block. Set it to 50 milliseconds. This gives the loop a steady rhythm — twenty checks per second, plenty fast to catch someone walking past, but slow enough that the Arduino isn't pegging itself for no reason. Every loop should have a small pause at the end. The blocks are done — let's wire up the simulator.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.edgeConditio: Now the heart of edge detection. Drag an if block into the loop, below the sensor read. Drop an and block from the Operators category into its diamond slot. The and block needs both halves to be true before the if runs. Build the left half: currentState equals low. Build the right half: lastState equals high. In plain words: if the beam is broken right now and it was clear last time — somebody just walked through.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.edgeDetectio: Here's the smart idea. Don't count when the beam is broken. Count only when the beam JUST got broken — when the sensor transitioned from high to low. That single transition is one person stepping into the doorway. After that, even if they stand there forever, the sensor stays low but it isn't transitioning anymore, so we don't count again. This trick has a name: edge detection. The moment of change is called the edge. Once you've learned this pattern, you'll see it everywhere — every button click, every doorbell, every keypress, every step counter on a phone — they all use edge detection underneath.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.increment: Inside the if branch, drag a set count to block. Drop an addition block from the Operators category as its value. Put the count variable on the left side of the plus, and the number 1 on the right. So: set count to count plus 1. Whatever count was, add one to it. This line only runs when the edge condition is true — exactly once per person walking through.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.intro: Walk into any big shop and you'll see a tiny sensor pointing across the doorway. By the end of the day, the manager knows exactly how many people came in. Forty-seven. One thousand and twelve. Whatever the number is. Today you'll build that exact thing — a sensor that watches a doorway, a display that shows the count. And along the way you'll meet edge detection, which is the single most important pattern in all of embedded programming. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode, and the TM1637 7-Segment extension is added in Sensor Manager. If adding extensions is new to you, the Move a Servo tutorial walks you through Sensor Manager.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.makeCount: Open the Variables category and click Make a Variable. Name it count. Then drag a set count to block into setup, and leave the value as 0. The shop opens for the day with zero people counted — every counter starts at zero.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.makeCurrentS: One more. Click Make a Variable, name it currentState. We don't need to set it in setup — it'll be filled in every loop with the freshest sensor reading. Three boxes done: count, lastState, currentState.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.makeLastStat: Click Make a Variable again. Name this one lastState. Drag a set lastState to block into setup, and drop a high block from the Arduino category as its value. We start by assuming the doorway is empty — beam clear, sensor high — so lastState begins as high.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.naive: Here's the obvious way to write a counter. Every time loop runs, check the sensor. If it's low — beam broken — add one to the count. Done, right? Wrong. Loop runs thousands of times per second. If someone stands in the doorway for two seconds, the beam stays broken the whole time — and your count goes up by hundreds or thousands. One person walking past would register as a stampede. So the obvious way is broken. We need a smarter idea.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.pinModeSenso: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and mode to input pullup. Pin 2 will listen for the doorway sensor. The sensor we're using is an IR break-beam — it shines invisible infrared light across the doorway. When the doorway is empty, the beam is clear and pin 2 reads high. When someone walks through, they break the beam and pin 2 drops to low.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.readSensor: Drag a set currentState to block into the loop section. Drop a digitalRead block from the Arduino category as its value, with pin set to 2. Now every time loop runs, currentState gets the freshest reading from the doorway sensor — low if someone is breaking the beam, high if the doorway is clear.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.recap: Look at what you just learned. Edge detection — the pattern that handles every did-this-just-change problem in embedded code. Multiple variables working together — count holding the total, currentState holding now, lastState holding before. State — the idea that a program can remember what the world looked like a moment ago and compare it to the world now. The 7-segment display — how programs put numbers in front of human eyes. The shape of this tutorial — sensor, edge, count, display — runs every footfall counter in every shop, every turnstile in every metro station, every tap counter on every kitchen sink, every step counter in every fitness band. You can write them now.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.rememberLast: This step is the most important one in the whole tutorial — miss it and the counter breaks. Drag a set lastState to block into the loop, after the if block, not inside it. Drop the currentState variable as its value. So at the end of every loop, whatever the sensor was reading right now becomes the new lastState for next time. Without this line, lastState stays low forever, and the counter would never stop ticking up. With it, lastState tracks currentState exactly one step behind — which is what makes edge detection work.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.running: Press the push button in the simulator and release — that's one person walking through the beam. The display ticks up to one. Press and release again — second person. The display says two. Each press adds exactly one to the count, no matter how long you hold the button. Try holding the button for ten seconds — the count stays put, because there's no new edge while the button is held. The next press fires another edge. That's the magic of edge detection working.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.setupDisplay: We need somewhere to show the count. A 7-segment display is one of those red-glowing digit panels you've seen on microwaves and digital clocks — each digit is built from seven little bars that light up in different combinations. Drag the setup 7-segment block into setup. Set the clock pin to 3 and the data pin to 4. Those two pins are how the Arduino tells the display which digits to show.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.setupLoop: You know the drill by now. Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Setup runs once when the program starts. Loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.showCount: Still inside the if branch, drag a display 7-segment show block. Drop the count variable into its number slot. Every time the count goes up by one, this line pushes the new total out to the display. The little red digits update — 1, 2, 3, 4 — as people walk past.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The display lights up showing zero. The sensor sits quietly, waiting for someone to walk past.
gui.tutorials.peopleCounter.twoBoxes: To detect a transition, we need to compare two things: what the sensor is reading right now, and what it was reading the last time loop ran. If now is low and before was high — that's the edge. That's the moment a person crossed the beam. So we need two memory boxes. One called currentState for what the sensor says now. One called lastState for what it said last loop. We'll also need a third box to hold the actual count. Three boxes in total.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.addLcd: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LCD 16x2. The pin dialog asks for sda and scl — leave those at A4 and A5, the standard I2C pins on an Arduino Uno. Click Save. The virtual LCD appears, ready to display whatever your code prints to it.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.addReset: One more component. Plus button, Push Button, pin 3, Save. This is the Reset button. Two buttons and an LCD — that's all a real petrol pump screen really is.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.addStart: Click the plus button again, choose Push Button, set pin to 2, click Save. This is the Start button — tap it to begin pumping, tap it again to pause.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.delay: Last block — drag a delay at the bottom of the loop, set to 100 milliseconds. A tenth-of-a-second pause keeps the loop from racing. Every Arduino loop should have a small pause; without one, the program checks the button thousands of times per second and the counter ticks up too fast to read. Blocks are done — let's wire up the simulator.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.flipPump: Inside the if branch, drag a set pumping to block. In its value slot, drop a not block from Operators, and inside the not block put a pumping reporter. The whole line reads: set pumping to not pumping. If pumping was false, this flips it to true — the pump starts. If pumping was true, this flips it to false — the pump pauses. One button, toggle behaviour, exactly like a television remote's power key.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.freshTap: Now into the loop. Drag an if block. In its diamond slot, we want to ask two questions joined by and — is digitalRead pin 2 equal to low right now, and was lastStart equal to high a moment ago? Use an and block from Operators with two equals blocks inside it. Left side — digitalRead 2 equals low. Right side — lastStart equals high. The whole question reads: is the button pressed now, and was it not-pressed a moment ago? That's how we catch a fresh tap and ignore a held finger.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.intro: You've seen it a hundred times — the screen on the petrol pump. The attendant clicks a button, fuel starts flowing, and a number on the screen ticks up and up. Click again, it pauses. Reset, and it goes back to zero. Today you'll build that exact screen — two buttons, one LCD, and a program that knows when to count and when to stop. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected, you're in Upload mode, and the I2C 16-by-2 LCD extension is added in Sensor Manager. If adding extensions is new to you, the Move a Servo tutorial walks you through Sensor Manager.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.lcdInit: Drag the LCD init block right below. This is the wake-up call — it tells the LCD 'get ready to receive text'. Without this, the screen stays blank no matter what we send. Two LCD setup blocks done; now the screen is alive.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.lcdPrint: Drag the LCD print block into the loop, below the three ifs. In its text slot, drop a total reporter. Every loop, the LCD shows whatever number is in the total box right now. When pumping ticks total up, the screen ticks up too. When Reset zeros it, the screen zeros.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.makeLastSt: One more variable. Make it, name it lastStart. Drag a set lastStart to block into setup, and drop a high block into its value slot. This is fact three — what did the Start button look like the last time we checked? Because of input pullup, an unpressed button reads high, so we start lastStart at high. We'll use this in a moment to spot the exact instant the button transitions from not-pressed to pressed.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.makePumpin: Open the Variables category and click Make a Variable. Name it pumping. Then drag a set pumping to block into setup and set the value to false. This is fact one — is the pump running right now? At the start, no — the pump is idle. So pumping starts as false.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.makeTotal: Make another variable, name it total. Drag a set total to block into setup, set the value to 0. This is fact two — how much fuel has flowed. Every loop while the pump is running, this number ticks up. The LCD shows whatever's in this box.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.mystery: Here's something strange we'll need to handle. The Start button is just one button. But to make it behave like a real petrol pump, we'll need three separate variables. Three boxes for one button. That sounds like too many — why three? Hold the question. We'll answer it in the next step.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.pinModeRes: Drag a second pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 3 and mode to input pullup. Pin 3 will be the Reset button — one press and the total goes back to zero. Two buttons, two pins, both listening.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.pinModeSta: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and mode to input pullup. Pin 2 will be the Start button — the attendant taps it to start pumping, taps it again to pause. input pullup keeps the reading stable when nothing is pressed, just like in the button-and-LED tutorial.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.reset: Drag a second if block below the first one, still inside the loop. The condition asks: is digitalRead pin 3 equal to low? That's the Reset button being pressed. Inside this if branch, drag a set total to block and set it to 0. One press of Reset, the total goes back to zero — fresh customer, fresh fill-up.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.reveal: The trick is that one button has to tell us three different things. Fact one — is the pump running right now? That's a yes or no. Fact two — how much fuel has flowed so far? That's a number that grows. Fact three — did the button just transition from not-pressed to pressed? That's edge detection, the same trick from the people-counter tutorial. Three different facts, three different boxes. One pumping flag, one total counter, one lastStart memory of what the button looked like a moment ago.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.run: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator and give it a few seconds to load. Now press the Start button on the breadboard — the LCD starts ticking up, one count per tenth of a second. Press Start again — it freezes. Press it once more — it ticks up from where it stopped. Press the Reset button — the LCD jumps back to zero. You just built a real-world appliance. Five concepts working together — the pumping flag is a state machine that remembers running-or-idle, the lastStart variable is edge detection that tells a fresh tap apart from a held finger, the change-total-by-one is counter accumulation, the LCD print is screen output, and the delay paces the loop. The same pattern runs every parking meter, every transit-card top-up screen, every coffee-shop loyalty counter you've ever seen.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.saveLast: Drag a set lastStart to block at the very bottom of the loop. In its value slot, drop a digitalRead block with pin 2. Every loop, after we've done all our checking, we remember what the Start button looks like right now so the next loop can compare. This is the heartbeat of edge detection — without this line, lastStart never changes, and the fresh-tap check from earlier breaks.
