gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.setupLcd: Drag the setup LCD block into setup, below the two pinModes. The LCD is the little screen on the pump that shows the total. We're using a 16-by-2 LCD — sixteen letters wide, two rows tall — and it talks to the Arduino over a special two-wire connection called I2C, at address hex 27. The block knows the defaults; you just drop it in.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Same start as every Arduino program — setup runs once, loop runs forever.
gui.tutorials.petrolPumpMeter.tickUp: Drag a third if block below. Its condition is just the pumping reporter — true means the pump is running, false means it's idle. Inside this if branch, drag a change total by 1 block. Every loop where pumping is true, total grows by one. Every loop where pumping is false, this branch is skipped and total stays put. That's the difference between flowing and idle, expressed in code.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.addPump: Click the plus button again. The simulator doesn't have a tiny water pump either — so we'll use an LED on pin 9 as a stand-in. When the LED lights up, picture the pump whirring and water flowing into the pot. Same code, same wiring — only the visible part is different from a real garden setup.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.addSoil: Open the Simulator and click the plus button. The simulator doesn't have a real soil-moisture probe — so we'll borrow the photoresistor and use it as a stand-in. Add a Photoresistor on pin A0. Pretend its slider is the soil dampness — slider down means dry dirt, slider up means damp dirt.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.condition: Open Operators. Drag a less-than block into the diamond slot of the if-else. Drop the moisture variable on the left side. Change the right side to 400. The condition reads: 'if the moisture reading is below 400, the soil is dry — turn the pump on'.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.delay: Drag a delay block below the if-else. Set it to 500 milliseconds. The Arduino now checks the soil twice every second — plenty fast for a plant, gentle on the loop.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.experiment: Try this: change 400 to 200 — your plant only waters itself in really bone-dry soil. Or change to 700 — it waters at the slightest hint of dryness, like a fussy plant parent. Even better, swap the digitalWrite for an analogWrite and use two thresholds: very dry below 200 means a fast pump at full speed, dry below 400 means a gentle slow pump. One sensor, two reactions, even friendlier to the plant.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.ifElse: Drag an if-else block below the set moisture block. The if branch will handle the dry case — pump on. The else branch will handle the damp case — pump off. Same if-else block you've used in Scratch and in the night lamp.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.intro: Forget to water your plant for a week and the leaves go sad and droopy. What if the plant could just take care of itself? Today you'll build exactly that — a soil sensor that watches the dirt, and a pump that turns on the moment the soil goes dry. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you are in Upload mode. If you have not done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.makeVar: Open Variables and click Make a Variable. Name it moisture. This will hold the latest reading from the soil sensor — a number we'll check every loop to decide whether the plant is thirsty.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.mystery: Hold on a second. A sensor on A0, an output on pin 9, an if-else picking between them based on a number… didn't we just do this with the night lamp? It really does feel the same. Let's stop and ask the question out loud — is this the same tutorial wearing a costume?
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.pattern: The reason the night lamp and the plant feel identical is that they are identical, deep down. Sensor reads a number. Program compares the number to a threshold. Output reacts. That same shape runs the thermostat in your house, the water-level alarm in a tank, the brightness sensor in a phone screen, the smoke detector on the ceiling. Once you've built it once in code, you've already built the bones of all of them. The work after that is just plugging in different sensors and different outputs.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.pinMode: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and leave mode on output — that's the pump. The soil sensor lives on pin A0 — analog inputs like A0 configure themselves automatically, so they don't need a pinMode line. Only outputs do.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.pumpOff: Drag another digitalWrite into the else branch. Keep pin on 9 but change value to low — the pump shuts off when the soil is damp enough on its own. No flooding the plant.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.pumpOn: Drag a digitalWrite block into the if branch. Set pin to 9 and keep value on high. When the soil reads below 400 — too dry — the pump kicks in.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.readSoil: Drag a set moisture block into the loop. For the value, drag an analogRead block from the Arduino category and drop it in place of the 0. Set analogRead's pin to A0. Same block you used for the night lamp's light sensor — analogRead gives back a number from 0 to 1023, no matter what kind of analog sensor is on the pin.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.recap: Two tutorials, same skeleton: analogRead pulls a number off a sensor, a threshold turns that number into a yes-or-no decision, digitalWrite makes an output react. You used it for light. You used it for moisture. The next time you meet a problem that smells like 'watch a sensor and react when it crosses a line', you already know the shape of the answer. That's pattern reuse — and it's the most powerful thing in programming.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.reveal: Yes — exactly the same shape. Sensor reads a number. The program compares the number to a threshold. Based on the answer, an output turns on or off. Light becomes moisture. Lamp becomes pump. The pattern transfers. Once you've seen this shape once, you'll start spotting it everywhere — in thermostats, alarms, level monitors, automatic taps. Same three steps, different parts plugged in.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.running: Drag the sensor slider down low — that's our 'soil is dry' signal. The LED comes on, meaning the pump is running. Slide it back up high — 'soil is damp' — and the LED switches off, pump stops. The plant is now in charge of its own watering.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Setup runs once at the start. Loop runs forever — checking the soil over and over, all day long.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.start: Click Start. Your program compiles and starts watching the soil.
gui.tutorials.plantIrrigation.threshold: For the soil sensor, a lower number means drier dirt. A higher number means damp dirt. We pick a threshold — a line in the sand. Below this number the soil is too dry and the plant needs water. Let's pick 400 to start. You can tune it later, just like the night lamp's brightness number.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.addBuzzer: Click the plus button again, choose Buzzer, set pin to 9, click Save. The buzzer appears next to the PIR. Two components wired up: one that senses, one that reacts.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.addPir: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose PIR. In the pin dialog, choose pin 2 and click Save. The virtual PIR appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same pin your code is reading from.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.delay: Drag a delay block to the very bottom of the loop, after the if-else. Set it to 250 milliseconds. Every loop needs at least a small pause — without one, the Arduino re-checks the sensor millions of times per second for no good reason. 250 milliseconds means the alarm still reacts within a quarter-second while letting the Arduino breathe between checks. Get into this habit: every loop you ever write, end it with a delay of at least 250 milliseconds.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.dropEquals: Open the Operators category. Drag an equals block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if-else — that's where the yes-or-no question goes. The equals block compares two things; we'll fill in what to compare in the next two steps.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.dropHigh: Still in the Arduino category, drag a high block. Drop it into the right slot of the equals block. Now your question reads: is pin 2 high right now? Or in plain words: is the PIR seeing motion right now? When the answer is yes, the if branch runs. When the answer is no, the else branch runs.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.dropRead: Back in the Arduino category, drag a digitalRead block. Drop it into the left slot of the equals block. Set the pin to 2. digitalRead checks what state pin 2 is in right now and reports back high or low. That's how the program asks the PIR: anything happening?
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.experiments: Try this: change the tone frequency from 1000 to 2000. The buzzer goes from a steady note to a sharper, more urgent shriek. Or add a second pinMode for an LED on pin 13 and a digitalWrite block in each branch — high in the if, low in the else. Now motion lights up the LED and sounds the buzzer at the same time. A flashing alarm light to go with the alarm sound.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.ifElse: An alarm needs a decision: motion seen — sound it. No motion — stay quiet. That's an if-else, the same one you've seen in Scratch and in the Button-controlled LED tutorial. Open the Control category and drag an if-else block into the loop section. Whatever question we put in the diamond slot decides which branch runs.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.intro: Imagine a sneaky burglar tip-toeing past your bedroom door at night. You can't see them in the dark, and a regular button is no help — they're not going to politely press it. So we'll build something cleverer: a sensor that watches silently, and a buzzer that screams the moment anything moves. A real security alarm, made of two components and a few blocks. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done the Blink an LED and Button-controlled LED tutorials yet, finish those first — we'll build on what they taught.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.meetTone: The buzzer needs more than a steady high or low to make a sound. It needs the pin to vibrate — flip from high to low to high to low, fast. That's exactly what the block called tone does. The tone block takes two inputs: a pin number and a frequency. Frequency means how many vibrations per second. Set the pin input to 9 and the frequency input to 1000, and pin 9 flips a thousand times every second. The buzzer hears those pulses as a steady note. There's also a sister block called noTone — written as one word, capital T — which stops the vibration and leaves the buzzer silent.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.mystery: A button is obvious — a finger pushes it down, a spring pushes it back. You can see the mechanism. But a motion sensor has no moving parts. Nothing presses anything. It just sits there on the wall and somehow knows when a person walks past. So what's actually happening inside? How does a tiny black dome on a board notice that a person crossed a doorway five metres away?
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.noTone: Drag a noTone block into the else branch — the bottom half. Set pin to 9. When the question's answer is no — no motion — pin 9 stops vibrating and the buzzer falls silent. Together the two branches give you the full alarm logic: tone when something moves, noTone when it doesn't.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.pinModeBuzze: Drag a second pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 will send signals out to the buzzer — that's how the alarm makes its sound. Listening on one pin, speaking on another. Sense and control, side by side.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.pinModeSenso: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and mode to input. Pin 2 is going to listen — it'll wait for the motion sensor to tell it whether anything is moving. Same idea you used with the button on pin 2 last time, just a different kind of sensor plugged in.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.recap: Five minutes ago you didn't know any of this. Now you do: a PIR sensor reads moving heat — body warmth crossing its view makes pin 2 go high. tone makes a pin vibrate at a frequency you choose — that's how programs make sound. noTone shuts the vibration off. And the whole alarm is built on the same shape you already know: sense the world with digitalRead, decide with if-else, react with an output block. Sense, decide, react. Burglar alarms, factory machine guards, automatic doors, hand-dryer faucets — every one of them runs this exact pattern. You can write them now.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.revealPir: The sensor is called a PIR — passive infrared sensor. Every warm thing — your body, a cat, a candle — gives off invisible infrared. The PIR sits quietly and watches for that warmth to move across its view. Stand perfectly still and the PIR sees nothing — to it, you're invisible. Take one step and the warm patch shifts. The PIR notices the shift, and pin 2 reads high. Stop moving and pin 2 drops back to low. That's all it does — sense moving heat.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've done this in every Arduino tutorial so far — setup runs once when the program starts, loop runs forever while the Arduino has power. The alarm will live inside loop, checking and re-checking the sensor as long as the Arduino is on.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live and the alarm starts watching.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.tone: Drag a tone block from the Arduino category into the if branch — the top half of the if-else. Set pin to 9 and frequency to 1000. When the if-question's answer is yes — when the PIR has spotted motion — this line runs and pin 9 starts vibrating a thousand times per second. The buzzer screams.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.trigger: Find the PIR in the simulator. It has a Motion toggle — flip it on. The buzzer screams immediately, because pin 2 is now high and the if branch is firing tone every loop. Flip the toggle off. Silence — pin 2 is back to low and the else branch is running noTone. You just built a working motion alarm.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.addKeypad: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose Membrane Keypad. The dialog asks for eight pins — four rows and four columns. Set the row pins to 2, 3, 4, 5 and the column pins to 6, 7, 8, 9 — exactly the same numbers you typed into the setup keypad block. Click Save. A virtual 4 by 4 keypad appears next to the Arduino.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.addServo: Click the plus button again, choose Servo, set the pin to 10, click Save. The virtual servo appears — a little arm that swings to whatever angle your code asks for. In a real lock it would be glued to a sliding bolt; in the simulator we just watch the arm move.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.chainDigits: Real keypad locks don't open on a single digit — they want a sequence, like one-two-three-four. To do that, the program has to remember the last few keys in order and compare the whole sequence to the secret code. That needs a list of values held over time — a tool called an array. We'll meet arrays in a later tutorial; for now, one key opens, one key closes, and that's enough to see the idea.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.delay: Drag a delay block to the very bottom of the loop. Set it to 50 milliseconds. Every loop body should end with a small pause — it gives the Arduino breathing room and stops the keypad from registering a single press as dozens of presses in a row.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.equalsOne: Open Operators and drag an equals block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if. On the left side, drop the key variable from Variables. On the right side, type the digit one. The keypad gives us a single character each time, so we're asking: 'is the pressed key the digit one?'
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.equalsStar: Drag another equals block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the second if. Drop the key variable on the left, and on the right type the star. The question reads: 'is the pressed key the star?'
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.ifLock: Drag a second if block into the loop, below the first one. Two ifs in a row means the program checks both questions every loop, one after the other. The first if handles unlocking; this one will handle locking.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.ifUnlock: Open Control and drag a plain if block — not if-else this time, just if — into the loop, below the set-key line. We'll fill in the diamond slot with the question 'is the pressed key the unlock key?' in the next two steps.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.intro: Hotel rooms, office entrances, gym lockers, smart safes — the metal key is gone. You walk up, tap a code on a keypad, and the lock swings open. Today you'll build a tiny version of that. A keypad to type on, a servo to swing the bolt, and a few lines of code to tie them together. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode, and the Membrane Keypad and Servo Motor extensions are added in Sensor Manager. If you haven't done Blink an LED, Control an LED with a Button, and Move a Servo yet, finish those first — this tutorial builds on all three. Move a Servo also walks you through Sensor Manager if extensions are new to you.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.makeKey: Open the Variables category and click Make a Variable. Name it key. This is where we'll store whichever key the user just pressed — could be a digit, could be the star, could be nothing at all if no key is held.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.mystery: Pause for a moment. Back in the Control an LED with a Button tutorial, one button used one pin. Easy. But this keypad has sixteen buttons and we only gave it eight pins — four rows and four columns. So how does the Arduino know which of the sixteen keys you actually pressed? Hold that question; the next step is the answer.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.oneKey: Take a moment to look at what you've built. Press the digit one and the door unlocks. Press anything else — two, five, the hash, the star, whatever — and nothing happens. There's no else branch, so the program just keeps reading the keypad. Right now the door has only one rule. Next we'll add a second rule so we can lock it again.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.readKey: Drag a set variable block into the loop. Pick key on the dropdown. Then drag the read keypad reporter from the Keypad category and drop it into the value slot. Now every time the loop runs, key gets refreshed with whatever is being pressed right now.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.recap: Look at what you just put together. A keypad — sixteen buttons read by only eight pins, thanks to the library scanning rows and columns thousands of times a second. A variable named key that holds whichever character was pressed. The equals block comparing characters to decide what to do. Two ifs in a row, each with its own action. A servo whose angle stands in for two states: zero degrees means locked, ninety means unlocked. Programs that read input, decide, and move something in the world are everywhere — keyless car entry, ATM keypads, smart safes, hotel room doors. You can write them now.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.reveal: Here's the trick. The keys sit in a grid — every key is at a place where one row wire crosses one column wire. The Arduino checks the grid thousands of times every second: row by row, column by column, asking 'is anything pressed at this crossing?' If a key is held down, exactly one row and one column light up together — and that pair tells the Arduino which key it was. The keypad library does all that scanning for us. We just ask 'what was pressed?' and we get back a single character.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.running: Click the digit one on the simulated keypad. The servo arm swings to ninety degrees — unlocked. Click the star. The servo swings back to zero — locked. Press any other key and nothing happens, because no if branch matches. You just built a working keypad-controlled lock.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.servoAttach: Same servo pattern as Move a Servo. Make a variable called servo. Drag a 'set servo to' block below the keypad setup and drop a Servo reporter from the Servo category into the value slot — the reporter creates a new servo and your variable now holds a reference to it. Drag a servo attach block right under and set the pin to 10. Now when we say 'go to ninety degrees' the library knows which pin to drive.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.servoClose: Drag another servo write block into the second if branch. Keep the pin on 10 and set the angle to 0. When the user presses the star, the servo swings back to zero — the bolt slides closed, the door is locked again. Two keys, two angles, two states: unlocked and locked.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.servoOpen: Drag a servo write block into the if branch — the body that runs when the question's answer is yes. Set the pin to 10 and the angle to 90. When the user presses the digit one, the servo swings to ninety degrees — the bolt slides open, the door is unlocked.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.setupKeypad: Open the Keypad category and drag the setup keypad block into setup. Leave the type on 4 by 4 — four rows of four buttons, sixteen keys in total. Set the row pins to 2, 3, 4, 5 and the column pins to 6, 7, 8, 9. That's eight pins for sixteen keys. Hold on to that thought — we'll see the trick to it in a moment.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've done this several times now — setup runs once when the program starts; loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds to build. The simulator goes live.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.addDht: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose DHT22. In the pin dialog, choose pin 2 — that's the pin we told dht22.attach about. Click Save. The virtual sensor appears next to the virtual Arduino, complete with a slider you'll use in a moment to fake a hot day.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.addLed: Click the plus button one more time, choose LED, set pin to 9, click Save. A real fan needs a motor and a driver chip — a bit much for a beginner project. So in the simulator we use an LED to stand in for the fan: when the LED is bright, imagine the fan is spinning. When the LED is dark, the fan is off. The code is identical for a real fan once you add the motor driver.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.addOled: Click the plus button again and choose SSD1306 — that's the chip name for our little OLED screen. The pin dialog will ask for SDA and SCL pins. These are the two wires the screen uses to talk to the Arduino. Set SDA to A4 and SCL to A5 — those are the standard pins on Arduino Uno for this kind of communication. Click Save. The OLED appears, blank and ready.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.clickStart: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino and the simulator goes live. The OLED lights up with whatever the sensor is reading right now.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.compare: Open the Operators category. Drag a greater-than block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if-else. On the left side of the greater-than, drop in the temp variable from Variables. On the right side, type 25. The question now reads: is temp greater than 25? When the room is warmer than our threshold, the answer is yes.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.delay: Drag a delay block at the very bottom of the loop, after the if-else. Set it to 1000 milliseconds — one full second. Why? The temperature doesn't change quickly. Reading it ten thousand times a second is wasteful and would also flood the OLED with redraws. Once a second is plenty fast for a thermostat, and it gives the Arduino breathing room. Every loop you write should have some delay — never leave a loop running flat out. The blocks are done — let's wire up the simulator.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.dhtAttach: Now drag a DHT22 attach block into setup. Set its pin to 2. This tells the sensor library which pin the temperature sensor is wired to, so when we ask for a reading later, it knows where to look. Notice we did not use pinMode this time — the DHT22 library handles all of that for us behind the scenes.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.experiment: Try this. Change the threshold from 25 to 30 — the fan kicks in only when the room gets really hot. Or add a second LED on pin 10 wired to a colder threshold like 18 — now you have a heater that switches on when the room gets cold AND a fan when it gets hot. Two thresholds, two outputs, one sensor. That's a real climate-control loop.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.fanOff: Drag another digitalWrite into the else branch — the bottom half. Pin 9, value low. When the room is at or below twenty-five, this line runs instead: pin 9 goes low, the fan stops. Sense, decide, react — same shape you've seen in night-lamp and button-led, just with degrees instead of light or button-presses.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.fanOn: Drag a digitalWrite block into the if branch — the top half of the if-else. Set pin to 9 and value to high. When the answer to is-temp-greater-than-25 is yes, this line runs: pin 9 goes high, the fan switches on.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.heatRoom: The DHT22 in the simulator has a temperature slider. Drag it slowly upward. Watch the OLED — the number ticks up live. The moment the slider crosses 25 degrees, the LED turns on. Drag it back down past 25 — the LED clicks off. Sense, decide, react. The thermostat is alive.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.ifElse: Open the Control category and drag an if-else block into the loop, below the OLED print. Same block you've used many times now. Whatever question goes in its diamond slot decides which branch runs — the if branch when the answer is yes, the else branch when the answer is no.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.intro: Every air conditioner, every fridge, every electric kettle has the same trick inside: a sensor watches the temperature, and the moment things cross a line, something switches on or off. Too hot? Fan kicks in. Cool enough? Fan stops. Today you'll build that exact brain. A temperature sensor will read the room, an OLED will display the number live, and an LED standing in for a fan will switch on when things heat up. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode, and the Temp & Humidity (DHT11) and U8g OLED extensions are added in Sensor Manager. If adding extensions is new to you, the Move a Servo tutorial walks you through Sensor Manager.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.makeVar: Open the Variables category. Click Make a Variable and call it temp. A variable is a labelled box you can store a number in and read back later. We need one because the temperature is going to be used twice in every loop — once to print on the OLED, and once to compare against our threshold. Reading the sensor twice would be slower and the two readings might even disagree slightly. So we read once, store in temp, and use that one value everywhere.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.mystery: Think back to the night-lamp tutorial. The LDR returned a number from 0 to 1023 — totally raw, with no units. That number meant nothing on its own; we had to pick a threshold and guess what it stood for. But a thermostat needs degrees. Twenty-five degrees Celsius, thirty degrees Celsius — real numbers a kid in any classroom would recognise. So how on earth do we get from a raw 0-to-1023 reading to actual degrees?
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.oledClear: Drag a firstPage block from the U8g OLED category, just under the set-temp line. The OLED draws to a hidden buffer in chunks called pages. firstPage rewinds to the top of the screen and gets ready for a fresh redraw — without it, every new reading would stack on top of the old one.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.oledDispla: Drag a do-while-next-page block under firstPage. This is a special C-shape — anything you drop inside its mouth gets repeated for every page chunk on the screen. firstPage starts the cycle, this picture loop walks through each chunk, and once it finishes the screen actually shows what you drew.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.oledPrint: Drag a print block into the picture loop, just below setPrintPos. In its value slot, build a join: the temp variable joined with the text space-C. Now every loop, the OLED writes something like twenty-five C. firstPage cleared the old reading, picture loop redraws — your display is now a live thermometer.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.oledSetCur: Drag a setPrintPos block INTO the mouth of the picture loop. Set x to 0 and y to 22. That's the top-left, with y measured from the top of the letter down to its baseline — 22 leaves room for the unifont's tall characters. The next print starts right there.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.oledSetup: Drag a setFont block from the U8g OLED category into setup, below the DHT22 attach. Pick unifont — that's a clean, readable typeface. The OLED is just dots, so to draw letters it needs a font. After this line runs once at startup, every print on the OLED uses that font.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.pinModeFan: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 is going to send voltage out to the fan — well, to an LED standing in for the fan. When pin 9 is high the fan runs; when it's low the fan stops.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.recap: You just built a feedback loop — sense, decide, act, wait, repeat. The DHT22 is a smart digital sensor: it does its own measuring and hands you Celsius directly. The OLED is a buffered display: write, then push to screen with display. Threshold comparison is the same shape as the night-lamp and the plant-irrigation tutorials — sensor reads a number, code compares to a line, output reacts. That feedback-loop pattern runs every air conditioner, every fridge, every electric kettle, every greenhouse heater. You named the sensor, set the line, picked the action. The Arduino does it forever.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.reveal: Here's the secret. The DHT22 is not a dumb sensor like the LDR. It is a smart sensor — it has its own tiny brain inside the little case. It measures the temperature, does all the conversion maths internally, and hands your Arduino a number that is already in degrees Celsius. We don't read raw values. We don't pick scaling factors. We just ask the sensor for the temperature and it tells us. That's the whole reveal — some sensors are raw, some are smart, and the DHT22 is the smart kind.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.setTemp: Drag a set variable block into the loop section. Pick temp from its dropdown. Now open the Temp and Humidity category — that's where the DHT blocks live — and drag a DHT22 read temperature block. Drop it into the value slot of the set block. Each time the loop runs, this asks the smart sensor what the temperature is right now, and parks the answer in our temp box.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.setupLoop: You've seen this block plenty of times by now. Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Setup runs once when the program starts. Loop runs forever after that, over and over, while the Arduino has power.
gui.tutorials.smartThermostat.threshold: Time for a decision. We need to pick a threshold — a temperature where the fan switches on. In a real house, comfortable room temperature sits around twenty-two to twenty-four degrees Celsius. Anything above twenty-five starts feeling warm. So twenty-five is our line in the sand. Above twenty-five — fan on. At or below twenty-five — fan off. You can tune that number later for your own taste.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.addLed: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LED. In the pin dialog, pick pin 9 and click Save. The virtual LED appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same pin your code is writing high and low to.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.dash1: Now the letter O — three dashes. A dash is the same shape as a dot, just longer. Drag digitalWrite pin 9 high. Then delay 600 milliseconds — that's the dash. Three times longer than a dot, so it looks clearly different to anyone watching. Then digitalWrite pin 9 low. Then delay 200 milliseconds — same small gap as inside the S.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.dash2: Same four blocks again — the second dash. digitalWrite pin 9 high, delay 600, digitalWrite pin 9 low, delay 200. The pattern is starting to feel familiar. That's the point — Morse is built from a tiny set of pieces repeated.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.dash3: Third dash. digitalWrite pin 9 high, delay 600, digitalWrite pin 9 low, delay 200. The letter O is done — three long blinks. Halfway through the SOS message. Now we need another between-letters pause before the second S.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.dot1: Time to write the first dot. Drag a digitalWrite block into loop. Set pin to 9 and value to high — that turns the LED on. Below it drag a delay block, set to 200 milliseconds — that's how long the LED stays on for one dot. Then another digitalWrite, pin 9, value low — LED off. Then a delay of 200 milliseconds — the small gap before the next dot. That whole stack is one dot.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.dot2: Add the same four blocks again below the first dot. digitalWrite pin 9 high, delay 200, digitalWrite pin 9 low, delay 200. That's the second dot of S. Notice the rhythm — every dot is the same shape. We could shorten this with a loop later, but for now we're typing out the pattern so you can see it.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.dot3: One more time — the third dot. digitalWrite pin 9 high, delay 200, digitalWrite pin 9 low, delay 200. That completes the letter S. Three short blinks in a row. Now we need a slightly longer silence before the next letter starts.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.intro: S, O, S. Three letters that have saved lives at sea for over 100 years. Lighthouse keepers, sailors lost in storms, mountaineers stuck on a cliff — when they had nothing but a flashlight, they sent these three letters. The light itself was the message. Today you'll teach an LED to blink that exact signal. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done the Blink an LED or Button-LED tutorials yet, do those first.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.letterGap1: Drag one delay block, set it to 400 milliseconds. Why? In Morse code, the gap between letters has to be longer than the gap between dots inside a letter — otherwise the receiver can't tell where one letter ends and the next begins. 200 inside a letter, 400 between letters. Twice as long. Now the listener knows: the S is finished, something new is coming.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.letterGap2: Drag another delay block, 400 milliseconds. Same idea as before — the longer gap that says 'a letter just finished'. Without it, the last dash of O would run straight into the first dot of the next S, and a watcher would see one blurry letter instead of two clean ones.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.longPause: One last delay — 1500 milliseconds at the very end of loop. That's the long silence between full SOS bursts. Real distress signals repeat over and over, with a noticeable pause between each repetition, so a rescuer scanning the horizon catches the rhythm. Without this pause, loop would slam straight from the last dot back to the first dot, and the message would look like a continuous flicker. The long pause is what makes it readable.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.mystery: Here's the puzzle. A button is just pressed or not pressed. An LED is just on or off. Two states. So how do you spell out a whole letter — never mind a whole alphabet — with one little light that only knows on and off? Sailors had to solve this with nothing but a lantern. The answer is older than computers, and it's beautiful.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.pinMode: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 is going to send voltage out to an LED — that LED is our signal lamp. output tells the Arduino: this pin will be sending voltage out, not listening for it.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.recap: Watch the LED. Short, short, short — long, long, long — short, short, short. Long pause. And it starts again. That's a ship calling for help. Real distress lamps flash this same pattern for hours, all night, until rescue arrives. Look at what you just learned. A pattern is just data — dots, dashes, and silences arranged in time. Repetition is how short pieces build a long message. And timing is information: the same LED says different things depending on how long you hold it on. The same idea runs every blinking turn signal, every aircraft beacon, every ambulance light. You can write them now.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.reveal: Morse code. You make patterns out of time. A short blink is called a dot. A long blink is called a dash. Different combinations of dots and dashes spell different letters. The letter S is three short blinks — dot, dot, dot. The letter O is three long blinks — dash, dash, dash. So SOS is dot-dot-dot, dash-dash-dash, dot-dot-dot. We'll use 200 milliseconds for a dot and 600 milliseconds for a dash. Same light, just different lengths of on-time. That's how one LED becomes a whole alphabet.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.secondS: Time for the closing S. You know the shape now. Drag three more dots, one after the other — each one is digitalWrite pin 9 high, delay 200, digitalWrite pin 9 low, delay 200. Three dots stacked. That finishes the full SOS — dot dot dot, dash dash dash, dot dot dot.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've seen this before. setup runs once when the program starts. loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power. Our SOS message will live inside loop, so the Arduino sends the signal again and again.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The signal lamp comes alive.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.addGreen: One more time. Plus button, LED, pin 11, Save. Now you have three virtual LEDs sitting side by side — one for each phase your code controls. Three pins, three lights, ready to go.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.addRed: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LED. In the pin dialog, choose pin 9 and click Save. The first virtual LED appears next to the virtual Arduino, wired to the same pin your code is driving high during the red phase.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.addYellow: Click the plus button again, choose LED, set pin to 10, click Save. The second LED joins the first. This is the yellow light — the one that flicks on briefly between green and red as a warning.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.delayRed: Drag a delay block right below the three digitalWrites. Set its value to 5000 — that's 5000 milliseconds, or 5 seconds. delay is what makes a phase actually last. Without it, the Arduino would race through to the next set of digitalWrites in microseconds and you'd never see red at all. The delay is the phase.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.experiments: Try this: change the red delay to 8000 and the green to 3000 — now it's a busy intersection where the side road barely gets a turn. Or swap which pin lights up in each phase to make a totally different signal pattern. Or add a fourth LED on pin 8 as a pedestrian walk light, and turn it on only during the red phase so people cross when cars are stopped.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.finalDelay: Drag one more delay block at the very bottom of loop and set it to 500. Half a second. This is a habit worth building: every loop body should end with at least a small delay. Without one, the Arduino spins as fast as it possibly can with nothing useful to do — wasted work. Half a second is plenty before the cycle restarts on red.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.greenDelay: Drag another delay block and set it to 5000 milliseconds. Green also holds for 5 seconds — the delay is what turns one moment of digitalWrite into a whole phase of the cycle.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.greenWrites: Drag three more digitalWrite blocks below the first delay. Pin 9 low (red off), pin 10 low (yellow still off), pin 11 high (green on). Same shape as the red phase, just different pins.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.intro: Every junction you've ever crossed has one. Red holds you. Green sends you. Yellow warns you the change is coming. Today you'll build that exact rhythm with three little lights and one Arduino. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done so already, finish the Blink an LED and Button-controlled LED tutorials first — this one builds straight on top of them.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.mystery: Pause for a second. We've told the Arduino about three pins, but we haven't told it anything about when to switch the lights. A real traffic light holds red for a long time, then suddenly it's green, then yellow, then red again. Three lights, one Arduino — how does it know when to change? Watch — you're about to see how.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.othersOff: Drag two more digitalWrite blocks below the first. Set the second to pin 10, value low. Set the third to pin 11, value low. This forces yellow and green off so only red is showing. Why bother? Because loop runs over and over and we don't want yesterday's state bleeding into today's. Be explicit: red on, yellow off, green off.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.phasesReveal: And there it is — the answer to the mystery from earlier. A traffic light is just three timed phases stacked one after the other. Each phase is a few digitalWrites and a delay. The loop block runs your program forever, so when it finishes the yellow phase, it wraps right back to red and the cycle starts over. Three phases, one loop, forever.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.pinModeGreen: Drag a third pinMode block. Pin 11, mode output. That's the green light. All three pins are now configured as outputs — the Arduino knows pin 9, pin 10, and pin 11 will all be sending voltage out to LEDs.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.pinModeRed: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 will be the red light. Just like before, output means this pin will be sending voltage out — to the red LED in this case.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.pinModeYellow: Drag a second pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 10 and mode to output. Pin 10 will be the yellow light. Same idea as before — we're telling the Arduino that pin 10 is going to send voltage out, this time to the yellow LED.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.realWorld: This same pattern — timed phases on multiple outputs — runs every traffic junction, every railway signal, every airport runway light, every stop-go robot at a construction site. Christmas-tree light controllers do it. Stage lighting does it. Once you know the shape, you'll spot it everywhere: a list of states, each with a duration, looping forever.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.recap: Look what you just built. You drove three separate output pins from one program. You used delay to make each phase last a real, visible amount of time. You stacked the phases inside loop so the Arduino cycles through them forever. And you ended loop with a small delay so it doesn't run hotter than it needs to. Multi-pin output, sequencing, phase-based timing — that's the whole pattern behind a huge family of real devices. You can write them now.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.redOn: Drag a digitalWrite block into the loop section. Set pin to 9 and value to high. This is the red phase — 5 volts on pin 9, the red LED lights up. You've used digitalWrite before with the button-LED tutorial; same block, just on a different pin.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.running: Watch the lights. Red holds for 5 seconds. Then red goes dark and green comes on for 5 seconds. Then green goes dark and yellow flickers on for 2 seconds. Then back to red, and the whole sequence repeats. The Arduino will keep doing this forever — that's loop, doing exactly what its name says.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've done this twice now — setup runs once when the program starts, loop runs over and over while the Arduino has power. Today setup will configure three pins, and loop will run the traffic-light cycle forever.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live and the cycle begins.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.yellowDelay: Drag a delay and set it to 2000 milliseconds. Yellow is shorter than red and green because it's only a warning — just two seconds. Same shape as the other phase delays, smaller number.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.yellowWrites: Drag three more digitalWrites. Pin 9 low (red off), pin 10 high (yellow on), pin 11 low (green off). Yellow on, the others off.
gui.tutorials.htmlComments.commentMarker: A comment has four parts. Open it with less-than, exclamation, two dashes. Close it with two dashes, greater-than. Whatever you put between them is YOUR note. The browser sees the markers and skips everything inside.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssBox.elementVsClass: CSS selectors come in flavours. A bare tag name like p picks EVERY paragraph on the page. A name with a dot like .card picks ONLY elements with that class. Knowing which kind to use is half of CSS.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.gapConcept: Gap is the breathing room BETWEEN flex children. With gap: 20 pixels, every pair of neighbours gets 20 pixels of space — no matter how many children, no extra code.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFlex.stackedVsRowVi: Without display: flex, divs stack on top of each other — one per line, top to bottom. Add display: flex to the parent and they switch to side by side in a row. Same boxes, different layout.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssFonts.families: Different font families have different personalities. Arial is clean and modern. Comic Sans MS is playful and round. Georgia is serif and bookish. Pick the family that matches the feeling of your page.
gui.tutorials.htmlCssIntro.selectorPicks: A CSS selector is a pointer. h1 means EVERY h1 on the page. p means EVERY paragraph. The styles inside the curly braces apply to every element the selector picks.
gui.tutorials.htmlForms.buttonReload: Heads up — clicking Send right now reloads the page. That's because the form has no action address yet. Without action, the form doesn't know where to send the data, so the browser just refreshes the page.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.htmlTabAnnotated: Look at the real HTML your blocks made. Every C-shape block became ONE tag pair — an open tag with angle brackets, your content in the middle, and a matching close tag with a slash. Same shape, every time.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.titleInBrowserTa: The title tag is special. Its text does NOT appear on the page itself — it appears in the BROWSER TAB at the very top of the window. That's how you name your page.
gui.tutorials.htmlImages.altFallback: Alt text shows when the image can't load — broken connection, wrong filename, anything. It also helps screen readers describe pictures to people who can't see them. Even when everything works perfectly, the alt is doing its job invisibly.
gui.tutorials.htmlMakeItHappen.featuresT: Your page now has a tree shape — header at top, hero below, features inside, then footer. Each section is a div with a class so we can style it next. Bigger sites all follow this same shape.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogicSmartDec: Parentheses group conditions so Python knows which ones belong together — just like in math. They make the order of and / or / not clear at a glance.
not is the flip switch. It turns True into False and False into True — the opposite of whatever it's given.
or is the lenient operator — it's True if EITHER side is true. Like sharing one umbrella: as long as one of you brought it, you both stay dry.
An f-string is a sentence with slots. The f before the quote turns on the magic, and Python fills each {variable} slot with its value — no + signs, no str.
The plus-glue way means stitching every chunk together with + and wrapping numbers in str. It works, but it's a lot of tape — easy to lose a quote or miss a +.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.elifD: elif is like a row of doors — Python checks each condition top to bottom and walks through the first one that's true. The rest are skipped.
gui.tutorials.pythonHelloWorld.sayVsPrin: say puts a speech bubble on the stage; print sends your message somewhere else entirely. Same idea — different destination.
gui.tutorials.pythonHelloWorld.interpret: Python reads your code like a recipe — top to bottom, one line at a time. That's why the order of lines matters: Python doesn't peek ahead.
gui.tutorials.pythonIndexingAndSlicing.r: A negative step flips the direction. Step -1 walks the list backwards — the shortest way to reverse anything in Python.
gui.tutorials.pythonIndexingAndSlicing.s: A step says how many positions to jump each time. Step 2 means take one, skip one — every other item.
A slice picks a chunk from start to stop — but the stop number is where Python STOPS, not the last item to include. So [1:4] gives you positions 1, 2, 3.
gui.tutorials.pythonIndexingAndSlicing.b: A list is a row of numbered boxes. Python counts from 0 going forward, and from -1 going backwards from the end.
gui.tutorials.pythonKeepItAfterRestart.n: The \n is the newline marker — it tells the file "start a new line here". Without it, every item would smush together on one line.
gui.tutorials.pythonListsManyThingsOneNa: for f in fruits walks the list directly — each turn, f IS the next item. No range, no index, no counting — just every item, one by one.
append is a method — a built-in command that belongs to a list. The dot says "run this command ON this list", and append tacks a new item onto the end.
gui.tutorials.pythonMathModulePowerTools: ceil rounds UP to the next whole number; floor rounds DOWN. Handy when you need a guaranteed whole answer no matter what the decimal is.
gui.tutorials.pythonSetsNoDuplicates.ven: & between two sets gives the intersection — the values that appear in BOTH. Like the overlapping middle of a Venn diagram.
gui.tutorials.pythonSetsNoDuplicates.fil: set takes any collection and keeps only the unique values — duplicates are dropped automatically. Same names, only one copy each.
gui.tutorials.pythonSmartShoppingBill.ra: range(n) makes a number line from 0 up to n-1 — exactly n numbers. for i in range(n) means "do the indented block n times".
gui.tutorials.pythonSmartShoppingBill.ru: A running total is like an empty jar that starts at 0 and gets a coin added every loop. "Start at zero, add as you go" shows up everywhere — counting, scoring, summing.
gui.tutorials.pythonStoreDataWithMeaning: A dictionary is like a filing cabinet — each entry has a labelled key and the value tucked behind it. Curly braces hold it all together, and the key tells you where to find each value.
gui.tutorials.pythonStringsControlYourTe: in is the "is it inside?" operator. It returns True or False — perfect for an if to route the program based on what's there.
replace finds every copy of one piece and swaps in another. It returns a NEW string — the original is left untouched, just like strip.
The dot says "this method belongs to this value". strip is a string method — it returns a NEW clean string, but the original text stays exactly as it was.
A string is a sequence of characters — even spaces count. Stray spaces at the ends are part of the value too, which is why real input often needs cleaning.
gui.tutorials.pythonTryExceptHandleError: finally always runs — whether the try succeeded, the except caught an error, or both. Perfect for cleanup like closing files or saving progress.
try wraps risky code. If anything inside crashes, Python doesn't quit — it jumps straight to the except block instead.
A tuple packs a few values together as one — like the red, green, blue trio that describes a colour. Round brackets make a tuple; square brackets make a list.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.concatGlu: The plus sign glues two pieces of text into one — that's called concatenation. "Hello, " is fixed text and name holds what the kid typed.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.tool: import random loads Python's random toolbox. Once imported, dice-roll helpers like random.choice become available for your game.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.thre: random.choice picks ONE item from a list at random — like rolling a 3-sided die labelled rock, paper, scissors.
gui.tutorials.pythonBeatTheComputer.wins: There are three winning combos: rock smashes scissors, paper covers rock, scissors cut paper. Each elif uses and to check BOTH player and computer.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.sor: sorted gives back a NEW list in order, smallest to largest. The original list stays untouched — sorted returns a fresh sorted copy.
gui.tutorials.pythonBuiltInFunctions.typ: type tells you what KIND of value something is — int, str, bool, list. Super useful for debugging when a value isn't what you expected.
gui.tutorials.pythonDateAndTime.fromImpo: from datetime import datetime pulls one specific tool out of a module. The from version saves you from writing datetime.datetime every time.
gui.tutorials.pythonDateAndTime.strftime: strftime turns a datetime into pretty text using a recipe of percent codes — %A is weekday, %d is day, %B is month, %Y is year.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.oneCheckRare: You know and, or, and not from Scratch's boolean blocks. JavaScript spells them differently. Two ampersands for AND. Two pipes for OR. An exclamation for NOT. Same logic, new symbols.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.setupVars: Drag a let block. Name it age, set to fifteen. Another let. Name it hasPass, set to true — no quotes. true and false are JavaScript's two boolean values.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.andOperator: Drag an if block. Slot — age greater-than ten AND hasPass. Body — log Allowed. Both halves must be true. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears. Allowed is printed — age fifteen is greater than ten, and hasPass is true. Both halves true, AND is true.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.orOperator: Add a second if. Slot — age greater-than eighteen OR hasPass. Body — log Welcome. Either half true is enough. Click Run. The age check is false, but hasPass is true. Welcome prints.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.notOperator: Add a third if. Slot — exclamation hasPass. That is NOT. It flips true to false. Body — log Buy a pass first. hasPass is true, so NOT hasPass is false. Nothing prints.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.flipRerun: Change hasPass to false. Click Run. Buy a pass first now prints. NOT flipped false into true.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.truthTables: Three simple rules. AND — both halves true. OR — at least one half true. NOT — flip. Same rules you used in Scratch. The only thing that changed is the spelling: two ampersands, two pipes, one exclamation.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.combining: Real checks combine all three — age greater-than eighteen AND hasPass OR isVip. Parentheses group them when you mix, just like maths.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.takeFunctio: Scratch lists do not have anything like these. Arrays come with tools that take a small function and run it on every item. The three big ones — forEach, map, and filter. They are the heart of modern JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.setupNumber: Make an array called numbers with one, two, three, four, five.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.forEach: Drag a numbers dot forEach block. Use n as the item name. Inside the body, drop a log to console block that prints n. forEach is like for-of but with one extra twist — you hand it a small function and it calls that function once for every item. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears. Each number prints on its own line. forEach walked through every item for you.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.callback: Here is a new idea. The small block you handed to forEach is called a CALLBACK — a function you hand to another function so it can call yours back later. Lots of real JavaScript uses this pattern. forEach is the simplest example.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.map: Drag a let block named doubled. Set it to numbers dot map with a callback that returns n times two. Log doubled. Click Run. The console shows two, four, six, eight, ten — a brand-new array. map turns every item into a new item, without changing the original.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.filter: Drag a let block named bigOnes. Set it to numbers dot filter with a callback that returns n greater-than two. Log bigOnes. Click Run. The console shows three, four, five — only the items the callback returned true for. filter keeps; map transforms; forEach just visits.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.threeToolsJ: forEach visits each item. map transforms each into a new one. filter keeps only items that pass a test. Each takes a callback.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.shapeOfMode: Real JS code reads like English with these. Take the users. filter the active ones. map their names. log them. Chain them together and a five-line problem fits on one line.
gui.tutorials.jsArrays.oneNameMany: You already use lists in Scratch — add things to list, item one of list, length of list. JavaScript calls them ARRAYS. Same idea, different word, with a few surprises.
gui.tutorials.jsArrays.makeArray: Make an array called fruits with three items — apple, banana, mango. Each fruit goes in quotes.
gui.tutorials.jsArrays.printWhole: Drop a log to console block. Pass it fruits. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsArrays.openConsole: Click the Console button next to Run. The whole array prints — brackets, commas, all of it.
gui.tutorials.jsArrays.howManyLength: Log fruits dot length. Click Run. The answer is three. dot length is JavaScript's version of Scratch's length-of-list.
gui.tutorials.jsArrays.indexFromZero: Big surprise. Scratch lists start at position ONE — item one is the first thing. JavaScript arrays start at ZERO. The first item is fruits zero. The second is fruits one. The third is fruits two. This trips up every Scratch kid for the first week.
gui.tutorials.jsArrays.readByIndex: Log fruits at index zero. Then fruits at index two. Click Run. You see apple and mango — first and third items.
gui.tutorials.jsArrays.addPush: Call fruits dot push with kiwi. That sticks kiwi on the end — same as Scratch's add to list. Log fruits again. Four items now.
gui.tutorials.jsArrays.threePowers: Same three list jobs you know from Scratch — read by index, count, add — just spelled fruits zero, dot length, dot push. Remember zero-based.
gui.tutorials.jsArrowFunctions.shorterSh: JavaScript has a shorter way to write functions. Modern JS uses these constantly — especially for callbacks. The shape uses an arrow — equals greater-than. Same idea — input in, value out — but tighter.
gui.tutorials.jsArrowFunctions.arrowAdd: Drag an arrow function block. Name it add. Inputs are a and b. Expression is a plus b. The arrow reads as gives-back. a and b give back a plus b.
gui.tutorials.jsArrowFunctions.noCurly: When the body is one expression, you skip the return word — JavaScript adds it for you. Less typing, same result.
gui.tutorials.jsArrowFunctions.trySquare: Make an arrow function called square. Input is x. Expression is x times x. Log square called with four. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsArrowFunctions.openConso: Click the Console button next to Run. You see sixteen.
gui.tutorials.jsArrowFunctions.withCurly: When the body needs many lines, use the arrow function with a body block. Make greet — input is name, body logs Hi space joined with name.
gui.tutorials.jsArrowFunctions.bothShape: Arrow for short callbacks. Classic function for bigger logic with a name. You will see both everywhere in real code.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.talksBack: In Scratch you use ask and wait to grab an answer from the user. JavaScript has prompt — a popup that asks a question and waits for the kid to type. The block here is called ask user. Same idea, browser-style. In this tutorial you will ask for a name and then greet the person by name.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.askTheirName: Drag a let block. Name it name. Set the value to an ask user block that asks What is your name. Different from Scratch — the answer goes straight into the variable, not into a separate answer reporter.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.greetThem: Drag a log to console block. Inside, join Hello comma space — in quotes — with the variable name, no quotes. The plus sign sticks two strings together — JavaScript's version of Scratch's join block.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.runIt: Click Run. The popup appears over the preview. Type a name. Click OK.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears at the bottom-right.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.whatIsGluing: Sticking strings with the plus sign is called concatenation — a long word for end-to-end join. JavaScript hands back one combined string.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.alwaysText: One warning — whatever the user types comes back as TEXT. Even if they type the digit five, you get the text five, not the number. The Numbers tutorial teaches the fix.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.askStoreUse: Ask. Store the answer in a variable. Use the variable later. The same shape you used in Scratch, written in JavaScript. In the next tutorial you will learn how JavaScript handles numbers and arithmetic, and why ask user returning text occasionally causes a surprise.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.mostUsedLine: Scratch has hat blocks like when this sprite clicked. JavaScript does the same with addEventListener. The when click on block is JS's way of saying — when this button is clicked, run these blocks. The most-used pattern on the web.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.pageReady: The page sprite already has a button with id go and text Click me, and a p with id counter and text Clicks zero. You edit the script sprite to wire the click.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.clickScript: Click the script sprite. JavaScript blocks in the flyout. Drag the script hat.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.grabBoth: A const named btn with find element by id and the text go. A const named counter with find element by id and the text counter.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.letCount: Drag a let block. Name it count. Value is zero. This remembers how many clicks have happened.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.listenerShap: Drag a when click on block. Element is btn. Event is click. The blocks inside the C shape run every time the button is clicked — like a Scratch when this sprite clicked hat block, but for any HTML element.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.readLikeSent: Read the line aloud — when btn is clicked, run these blocks. Pros read code this way to spot bugs.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.bumpCount: Inside the click body, drag a change by block. Variable is count. Amount is one. Just like Scratch's change variable by.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.showCount: Still inside the click body. Drag a set text of block. Element is counter. Use a join to glue Clicks space and the count variable. The paragraph updates on every click.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.saveAndClick: Click Save. Default name is script. Click Save. Then View Files, open index.html. Click the button. The counter goes up. Click again. Up again.
gui.tutorials.jsButtonClick.threeStepPat: Grab the element. Listen for an event. Run blocks. This three-step pattern lives on every interactive site — every like button, every menu, every search box.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.puttingToge: Capstone day. Today you build a real quiz. Arrays, functions, the DOM, click events — all wired together. By the end you have a working quiz that scores the player.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.pageReady: The page sprite already has a p with id q, an input with id a, a button with id next and text Next, and a p with id score and text Score zero. You edit the script sprite to wire the quiz.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.clickScript: Click the script sprite. JavaScript blocks in the flyout. Drag the script hat.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.questionsLi: A const named questions. Inside, an array of three objects. Each object has a question and an answer property. Two plus two — four. Capital of France — Paris. Legs on a spider — eight. Arrays of objects is how real apps store data.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.stateRefs: A let named index with value zero. A let named score with value zero. Four const blocks — qEl, aEl, nextBtn, scoreEl. Each uses find element by id with the matching id.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.defineShow: Drag a function block. Name it showQuestion. No parameters. It draws the current question on the page.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.endGuard: Inside showQuestion. An if block. Condition — index is not less-than the length of questions. Inside — set text of qEl to Quiz over. Set style display of aEl to none. Same for nextBtn. Then a return. return exits the function early.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.drawCurrent: After the if, still inside showQuestion. A set text of block. Element is qEl. Text is the question text at position index from the questions array. Then a set text of block. Element is aEl. Text is empty. The input clears between questions.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.wireNext: Drag a when click on block. Element is nextBtn. Event is click. The body runs on every Next click.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.checkAnswer: Inside the click body. An if block. Condition — the value of aEl made lowercase triple-equals the answer at position index made lowercase. If true, change score by one. Lowercase makes the check forgiving.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.advance: After the if, still inside the click body. Set text of scoreEl to a join of Score, a space, and score. Change index by one. Then call showQuestion. The next question appears.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.firstCall: Outside the listener, at the bottom of the script, add one call to showQuestion. That draws the first question when the page loads. Without it, the page starts blank.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.saveAndPlay: Click Save. Default name is script. Click Save. Then View Files, open index.html. Type an answer. Click Next. The score updates and the next question appears. Three rounds, then Quiz over.
gui.tutorials.jsCapstoneQuiz.listAppShap: A list of data. A function that draws one item. A button that advances. That shape powers Twitter showing tweets, Spotify showing songs, every comment thread on every site. Capstone done — you can now build real JavaScript apps.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.restyleLive: In Scratch you change a sprite's costume or colour to restyle it live. On a webpage you change CSS. Two ways from JavaScript — set a single style, or add a CSS class. Both work.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.threeSprites: Today the sprite selector has three. page already has the h1 with id title and the p with id message and a link to style.css. style is the CSS sprite. script is the JS sprite. You edit script first, then style.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.clickScript: Click the script sprite. JavaScript blocks in the flyout. Empty workspace ready.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.grabTitle: Drag the script hat. Add a const named title with find element by id and the text title.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.setColor: Drag a set style block. Property is color. Element is title. Value is tomato. Behind the scenes this is title dot style dot color equals tomato.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.camelCase: Small gotcha. CSS uses dashes — font-size, background-color. JavaScript cannot have dashes in names, so they become fontSize, backgroundColor — camelCase. The browser still understands.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.setFontSize: Add another set style block. Property is fontSize. Element is title. Value is 48px. The heading will grow when you save and open the page.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.addClass: Add a const named message with find element by id and the text message. Drag a class on element block. Action is add. Class name is highlight. Element is message.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.clickStyle: Click the style sprite. The workspace switches to CSS blocks. Same project — just a different file.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.defineHighli: Drop a rule for the highlight class. Background yellow, padding ten pixels. The CSS rule waits in style.css for the JS to add the class.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.saveAndView: Click Save on the style sprite. Default name is style, extension .css. Click Save. Switch back to script and Save it too. Then View Files, open index.html. The h1 is tomato and big, and the message paragraph glows yellow.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.classWins: One or two inline styles is fine. For many styles, write them once in a CSS class and just add the class from JavaScript. Pros pick class almost every time.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeStyle.twoWays: set style for quick tweaks. class on element for organised styling. Both update the page live.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeText.grabAndChange: Last chapter you grabbed an element. Now you change what is inside it — the JS lines textContent and innerHTML. The page updates instantly.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeText.sameTwoSprite: Same shape as last time. The page sprite already has the h1 with id title and the p with id message. You edit the script sprite to change them from JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeText.clickScript: Click the script sprite. JavaScript blocks in the flyout. Empty workspace ready for code.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeText.grabTitle: Drag the script hat. Add a const named title with find element by id and the text title.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeText.setText: Drag a set text of block. Element is title. Text is Changed by JavaScript. Behind the scenes this is title dot textContent equals Changed by JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeText.saveAndView: Click Save. Default name is script. Click Save. Then View Files, open index.html. The h1 now reads Changed by JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeText.setHtml: Add a const named message with find element by id and the text message. Drag a set HTML of block — this is dot innerHTML in real JS. Element is message. Type Now with a real b tag around the word bold. Open-angle-bracket b close-angle-bracket bold open-angle-bracket slash b close-angle-bracket.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeText.seeBold: Save the script. Reopen index.html. The word bold appears in actual bold. set HTML treats the value as HTML and parses the tags inside.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeText.textVsHtml: set text treats the value as plain text. set HTML reads tags inside the value. Pick set text by default — set HTML only when you really mean to inject markup.
gui.tutorials.jsChangeText.pageNowAlive: Your first visible JavaScript effect on a real page. Like changing a sprite's say bubble, but on a webpage instead of the Scratch stage.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.sixTools: You know greater-than, less-than, and equals from Scratch. JavaScript has six comparison operators total — greater-than, less-than, greater-than-or-equal, less-than-or-equal, triple-equals for equal, and exclamation-equals for not-equal. Every comparison returns either true or false.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.setupScore: Drag a let block. Name it score. Wrap a Number block around an ask user block that asks Score. We will run three checks against the answer.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.perfectScore: Drag an if block. Inside the slot, score triple-equals one hundred. Inside the body, log Perfect. Click Run. Type one hundred.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears. Perfect is printed. Run again and type any other number — nothing prints, because the condition was false.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.passMark: Add a second if. Slot — score greater-than-or-equal fifty. Body — log Pass. Click Run, type seventy — Pass. Type forty-five — nothing.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.belowFifty: Add a third if. Slot — score less-than fifty. Body — log Fail. Click Run, type forty-five. Now Fail prints.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.whyThreeEqua: JavaScript also has double-equals. But double-equals is loose — it says the text five and the number five are equal. Triple-equals is strict — same value AND same type, or false. Always use triple-equals. The pros do.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.sixToolsOneT: Six comparisons, every one returns true or false. Same shape as the boolean blocks you used in Scratch's if blocks — just spelled in JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.someNever: In Scratch every variable can be changed any time. JavaScript lets you lock some down. Use let for values you plan to change. Use const for values that should never change. Locking values down stops you from accidentally changing something that was supposed to stay the same.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.makeConstant: Drag a const block. Name it pi. Set it to three point one four. JavaScript now refuses any attempt to change pi.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.normalRadius: Radius can change for different circle sizes. Drag a let block, name it radius, set it to five.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.useTogether: Drag a log to console block. Inside it, pi times radius times radius — the area of a circle. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears at the bottom-right with the title Console.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.tryToBreak: Add a line that tries to set pi to four. Click Run. JavaScript will refuse.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.seeError: Open the console. A red error appears — assignment to constant variable. That is the safety const gives you. radius can still change. pi is locked.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.defaultToCons: Real JavaScript uses const by default. Reach for let only when you plan to reassign. Locking things down catches bugs early.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.growPage: Scratch has clone — make a new sprite at runtime. JavaScript can do the same to HTML — create brand-new elements from code and stick them on the page. Today you build a to-do list.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.pageReady: The page sprite already has an input with id itemInput, a button with id add, and an empty ul with id list. You edit the script sprite to wire them up.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.clickScri: Click the script sprite. JavaScript blocks in the flyout. Drag the script hat.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.grabThree: Three const blocks. itemInput with find element by id and the text itemInput. addBtn with the text add. list with the text list.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.wireClick: Drag a when click on block. Element is addBtn. Event is click. Body runs on every click.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.createLi: Inside the click body, add a const named li. Value is create element with the tag li. Behind the scenes — document dot createElement. The new tag exists in memory only. It is NOT on the page yet.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.fillLi: Drag a set text of block. Element is li. Value is a get value of block with itemInput inside. That reads what the user typed into the input.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.appendIt: Drag an append to block. Child is li. Parent is list. Behind the scenes — list dot appendChild. Now the new li attaches to the ul, and the browser paints it instantly.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.clearInpu: Still inside the click body. Add a set text of block. Element is itemInput. Text is empty. That clears the input so the user can type the next item.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.saveAndTr: Click Save. Default name is script. Click Save. Then View Files, open index.html. Type Apples. Click Add. The word appears as a list item. Type Bananas. Click Add. The list grows.
gui.tutorials.jsCreateElements.threeStep: Create element. Set its text. Append. Three steps and you built a working to-do list. The same shape powers every comment thread and every list app.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.threeJobs: You know repeat ten times from Scratch. JavaScript's for loop is the same idea but you write three things on one line — start the counter, the check, and how to step. It is the most-used loop in JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.forShape: Drag a for block from the Control section. It has four slots — name, start, end, step. Set them to i, one, five, one. The letter i is the traditional name for a loop counter. The Code panel now reads for open-paren let i equals one semicolon i less-than-or-equal five semicolon i plus-plus close-paren.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.plusPlus: Step is how much i changes each round. One adds one. Two skips every other number. Minus one counts down. Scratch's repeat block can only count up by one — JS lets you pick.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.addBody: Inside the for, drop a log to console block. Print Round space joined with i. i is the loop counter you can read inside the body — Scratch's repeat block does not give you that.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.run: Click the Run button at the top-right.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears. Round one through Round five prints — one line per round.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.forVsWhile: Two patterns. Use a for loop when you know the count in advance — like Scratch's repeat block. Use a while loop when the end depends on a condition that changes during the loop — like Scratch's repeat-until. Both are useful in different situations.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.mostUsed: The for loop is the workhorse loop in JavaScript. Counting up, counting down, walking through a list.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.madeForArrays: In Scratch you walk a list with a repeat plus item-i-of-list. JavaScript has a shortcut — the for-of loop. It hands you each item one by one. No counter, no index, no length check.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.setupArray: Make an array called fruits with four items — apple, banana, mango, kiwi.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.forOfShape: Drag a for-of block from Control. Use f as the item name. For the array, drop in the variable fruits. The Code panel reads for open-paren const f of fruits close-paren open-brace close-brace. Each round, the variable f holds one fruit from the array.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.bodyLikeEach: Inside the body, log I like space joined with f. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears. Four lines — I like apple, I like banana, I like mango, I like kiwi — one per item.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.nameIsYours: We used f for fruit. Pick whatever name reads well — fruit, item, thing.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.forOfVsClassic: for-of when you just need each item. Classic for when you also need the position number.
