gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.cleanerLoops: for-of is the cleanest way to walk an array in JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctionsReturn.inputsOu: In Scratch, Make-a-Block has parameters and reporter blocks can report a value. JavaScript has the same — INPUTS called parameters, and the keyword RETURN to send a value back.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctionsReturn.function: Drag a function block. Name it add. Inputs are a and b. Inside the body, return a plus b. The return keyword sends the value back to whoever called add.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctionsReturn.paramete: a and b are placeholders — same as Scratch Make-a-Block parameters. When you call add with five and three, a becomes five, b becomes three, and the answer is eight.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctionsReturn.callStor: Set result to add called with five and three. Log result. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctionsReturn.openCons: Click the Console button next to Run. You see eight. The function returned eight, and you stored it.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctionsReturn.useCallD: You do not have to store the answer. Log add called with ten and twenty. Click Run. add returns thirty, log prints thirty — all in one line.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctionsReturn.passAnyw: A returned value can go anywhere a value can go. Print it, store it, pass it to another function, use it in maths.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctionsReturn.paramsIn: Parameters in. return out. Same shape as a Scratch reporter Make-a-Block, written in JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctions.nameForChunk: You already use Make-a-Block in Scratch to wrap reusable code. JavaScript calls these FUNCTIONS. Same idea — define once, call many times — different word.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctions.defineFunction: Drag a function block. Name it greet. Leave the inputs empty. Inside the body, log Hello with an exclamation mark. Just like Scratch's define-greet hat block.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctions.definingNotRun: Click Run. Nothing prints. You defined the function but never called it. Same as Scratch — a define hat block on its own does nothing until something calls it.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctions.callIt: Drag a call block for greet — three times in a row.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctions.openConsole: Click the Console button next to Run. Hello prints three times. Three calls, one definition.
gui.tutorials.jsFunctions.wrapRepetition: Same rule as Scratch — anything you write twice belongs in a function. Real programs are mostly small functions calling other functions.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.threeFriends: Webpages are built by three friends. HTML is the bones — the shape. CSS is the paint — the colours. JavaScript is the muscle — the actions. You already made sprites move in Scratch. Now you will make a webpage do things, with JavaScript blocks that look just like Scratch blocks.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.twoWays: JavaScript has two ways to show a message. The CONSOLE is a hidden panel only the developer sees — that's where you debug. An ALERT is a popup that every visitor sees and it freezes the page until they click OK. Today we will use BOTH. Let's start by adding the JavaScript blocks to our project.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.addExtension: Click the Add Extension button at the bottom-left of the screen. This opens the Extension Library — that's where all the special block sets live, including the JavaScript blocks we are about to use.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.progFilter: The Extension Library shows a lot of cards. To find JavaScript faster, click the Programming tab at the top. Now only programming-language extensions are visible — HTML 5, CSS, Python, C, and JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.selectJs: Click the JavaScript card. This adds the full set of JavaScript blocks to your project — log-to-console, show-alert, variables, loops, all of it. The card closes and you are back on the workspace, now with a JavaScript category in the blocks panel.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.uploadMode: Look at the toggle at the very top-right of the screen. Adding the JavaScript extension already turned this on, so you should be in Upload mode now — the Code panel is on the right side. If for some reason you're not in Upload mode, click this toggle to switch into it. The Code panel and View tab only show up in Upload mode.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.jsCategory: Look at the blocks panel on the left — the JavaScript category is already selected for you, and the flyout shows every JavaScript block you can use. Right at the top, under the Entry label, is the script block — the one shaped like a hat. Every JavaScript program starts with that. If you ever click away to another category, just click JavaScript again to come back.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.dragHat: Find the script block at the top of the flyout — the one shaped like a hat. DRAG it into the middle of the empty workspace. This is your HAT block — it marks where your JavaScript program starts. Once you drop it, look at the Code tab on the right — it shows the start of your JavaScript program. As you add more blocks underneath, more code appears in the Code tab in real time.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.dragLog: Now find the log-to-console block in the flyout and DRAG it under the script hat. You will see a faint shadow showing where it will snap — drop it there. The two blocks now stick together, just like a Scratch script. The hat alone does nothing; the log block underneath is what actually prints.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.typeMessage: Click inside the text slot on the block where it says message and type: Hello JavaScript. You can put anything in there — your name, a joke, anything. You only type the text — no quotes. Now look at the Code tab on the right: the line reads console.log open-quote Hello JavaScript close-quote. The block automatically wrapped your text in quotes for you. If you were typing real JavaScript by hand, you would have to add the quotes yourself — that's how JavaScript knows the value is text, not a number — but the block does that work for you.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.codePanel: Look at the Code panel on the right side. It already shows the REAL JavaScript text your block generated: console dot log open-bracket quote Hello JavaScript quote close-bracket semicolon. That is the same line working developers around the world type a thousand times a day. Your block is just a shortcut for typing it. There is also a View tab next to Code — that one shows the actual web page, but it stays blank until you click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.clickRun: Click the Run button at the top-right — it's the triangle play icon. Like the flag in Scratch, this runs your code. Watch the View pane in the middle — it stays BLANK. That's right — nothing showed up on the page. Don't worry, your message DID get sent — just not to the page. It went to the console.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.openConsole: See the little >_ button right next to Run? That toggles the Console pane. Click it. A panel appears at the bottom-right of the View area with the title Console — that's it. Real developers keep this open all day long when they are building websites.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.consoleResult: There it is — Hello JavaScript — printed in the console by your very own program. You wrote, you ran, you saw it work. Now let's try the OTHER way to show a message — the loud one that the visitor sees too.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.dragAlert: Open the JavaScript category in the flyout again and find the show-alert block. Drag it into the workspace and snap it UNDER your console-log block. The two blocks now form a stack — JavaScript will run the top one first, then the bottom one. Click the text slot on the alert block and type: Hi there!
gui.tutorials.jsHello.alertPops: Click Run again. This time your BROWSER itself pops up a small dialog box at the top of the page saying Hi there! — that's the alert. The whole page is frozen until you click OK on it. That's the LOUD way to show a message — the visitor cannot ignore it. The console message also printed, but you cannot read it until you dismiss the alert. Go ahead — click OK on the alert.
gui.tutorials.jsHello.twoVoices: You just used two voices. log-to-console is quiet — only the developer sees it, perfect for debugging. show-alert is loud — every visitor sees it AND the page freezes, so use it sparingly. From now on, every JavaScript chapter starts by ADDING the JavaScript extension, switching to Upload mode, and using these same blocks. You have everything you need to build the next program.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.twoFiles: Every real website lives in at least two files. An HTML file — that is the PAGE you can see. And a JavaScript file — that is the SCRIPT that makes the page do things. The HTML file has one special line inside it that says: when the browser loads me, also load the JavaScript file. That is how the two files work together: HTML shows the page, the page loads the script, the script makes the page move.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.lookAtSprites: The dialog lists two rows. The first row shows index dot html with the HTML icon. The second row shows script dot js with the JavaScript icon. Click a row to switch to that sprite. The workspace, the flyout, and the code panel all follow.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.clickPage: Click the index dot html row. The dialog closes. The workspace is now on the index dot html page sprite. The flyout shows HTML blocks. The code panel switches to HTML. Same project, different file.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.openHtmlCatego: On the left, click the HTML category. The flyout fills with every HTML block you need to build a page. Whenever you come back to the index dot html sprite, click HTML here first.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.buildPage: Drag html, head, and body. Inside body, drop an h1 — id title, text My Page. Drop a p — id message, text Loading. At the end of body, drop a script src block with the URL script dot js.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.savePage: Click Save. The file name is already index and the extension is already dot html — the sprite is HTML, so Save already knows. Click Save.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.clickScript: Open the sprite switcher dialog again. Click the script dot js row. The workspace is now on the script dot js script sprite — empty workspace, JavaScript blocks in the flyout, code panel waiting. Same project, different file.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.openJsCategory: On the left, click the JavaScript category. The flyout fills with every JS block you need. Whenever you come back to the script dot js sprite, click JavaScript here first.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.grabAndChange: Drag the script hat. Now drag a const block. Name it title. For its value, drop in a find element by id block and set its id to title — that grabs the h1 from your HTML page. Next, drag a set text of block. Element is title, text is Hello from JavaScript. This will change the h1 you wrote on the page.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.saveScript: Click Save. The default name is script and the extension is already dot js — the script dot js sprite is JavaScript, so Save already knows. Click Save. Two files now, sitting next to each other.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.openIndex: Click View Files. Click index dot html. The browser loads the page, sees the script src tag, fetches script dot js, runs it. The h1 changes from My Page to Hello from JavaScript before your eyes.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.patternForever: One project. One sprite per file. Open the sprite switcher dialog to move between files. Save each sprite. Switch to the index dot html page sprite and click View to see them run together. Every DOM tutorial from here on uses this pattern — you only edit the script dot js sprite, then jump back to the page sprite to see the result.
gui.tutorials.jsKeyboardEvents.beyondMou: Scratch has when key pressed hat blocks — one per key. JavaScript has ONE event, keydown, that fires for any key. Inside, you check WHICH key. Every browser game uses this pattern. Today you move a sprite with arrow keys.
gui.tutorials.jsKeyboardEvents.pageReady: The page sprite already has a div with id sprite — a tomato 40 by 40 box sitting at left 100, top 100. You edit the script sprite to make it move.
gui.tutorials.jsKeyboardEvents.clickScri: Click the script sprite. JavaScript blocks in the flyout. Drag the script hat.
gui.tutorials.jsKeyboardEvents.setupStat: A const named sprite with find element by id and the text sprite. A let named x with value 100. A let named y with value 100. Two numbers track position — just like Scratch sprite x and y.
gui.tutorials.jsKeyboardEvents.keydownLi: Drag a when keydown on block. Element is document — the whole page. Event is keydown. The body runs every time ANY key is pressed.
gui.tutorials.jsKeyboardEvents.whichKey: Inside the listener, the event key reporter gives the name of the pressed key. The value is a string — ArrowRight, ArrowLeft, ArrowUp, ArrowDown, a, b, space, Enter. That is how you tell which key fired.
gui.tutorials.jsKeyboardEvents.fourIfs: Inside the keydown body, four ifs. If event key equals ArrowRight, change x by ten. ArrowLeft, change x by minus ten. ArrowDown, change y by ten. ArrowUp, change y by minus ten.
gui.tutorials.jsKeyboardEvents.applyPosi: After the four ifs, still inside the listener, two set style blocks. Property left on sprite, value is join x with px. Property top on sprite, value is join y with px. Setting CSS left and top is how you move an element on a webpage — different from Scratch where you set x and y directly.
gui.tutorials.jsKeyboardEvents.saveAndPl: Click Save. Default name is script. Click Save. Then View Files, open index.html. Click into the preview so the page has focus. Press an arrow key. The sprite moves.
gui.tutorials.jsKeyboardEvents.threeLine: Listen for keys. Read which key. Update the position. Every keyboard-controlled browser game uses this shape.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.programsDeci: You know if and if-else from Scratch. JavaScript has the same two — just spelled if and else. Today you write the JS shape.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.askTheAge: Drag a let block. Name it age. Wrap a Number block around an ask user block asking How old are you. The Number block converts the text answer into a number for comparing.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.ifShape: Drag an if block from Control. The slot is the condition — age greater-than-or-equal ten. The body is what runs if the condition is true — a log to console block saying You can join. Same C-shape you used in Scratch.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.indentation: Look at the code panel. Lines inside the if body are indented by four spaces. Curly braces — open brace, close brace — mark where the body starts and ends. The indent is just to make it readable. Every JavaScript codebase indents this way.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.elseHalf: Replace the if block with an if-else block. In the else body, log You are too young. Same as Scratch's if-else — two branches, exactly one runs depending on the condition.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.runWithTwelv: Click Run. Type twelve. The check is true.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears at the bottom-right. You can join is shown — the if branch ran.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.runWithSeven: Click Run. Type seven. The check is false. The console shows You are too young. The else caught it.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.twoPathsOneR: Same shape you know from Scratch — exactly one branch runs. The JS syntax is parentheses round the condition, curly braces round the body.
gui.tutorials.jsMeetTheDom.pageIsTree: In Scratch you control sprites. On a webpage, you control HTML elements. The browser turns the HTML into a tree of objects called the DOM. JavaScript can grab any element from that tree and change it. That is how webpages come alive.
gui.tutorials.jsMeetTheDom.twoSprites: Two sprites in the sprite selector. page already has the HTML — an h1 with id title and a p with id message. script is the empty JavaScript sprite. You only edit script today.
gui.tutorials.jsMeetTheDom.clickScript: Click the script sprite. The workspace switches to JavaScript blocks. The code panel switches to JS mode. Same project, different file.
gui.tutorials.jsMeetTheDom.grabTitle: Drag the script hat. Drag a const block. Name it title. Value is find element by id with the text title. You just asked the page for the h1 with that id. This is the JS line — document dot getElementById.
gui.tutorials.jsMeetTheDom.logTitle: Drag a log to console block. Drop title inside. The console will print the real h1 element.
gui.tutorials.jsMeetTheDom.saveAndOpen: Click Save. Default name is script, extension is already .js. Click Save. Then click View Files and open index.html. The browser fetches script.js and runs it.
gui.tutorials.jsMeetTheDom.openConsole: Click the Console button next to Run. The pane shows the h1 element printed, not plain text. You grabbed a live piece of the page.
gui.tutorials.jsMeetTheDom.grabMessage: Add another const block. Name it msg. Value is find element by id with the text message. Log msg. Save and reopen index.html. Two element references in hand.
gui.tutorials.jsMeetTheDom.idMustMatch: One trap. The id in the block must match the id in your HTML exactly. Title with capital T will not find id title with small t. The page returns nothing.
gui.tutorials.jsMeetTheDom.foundationOfE: Every interactive page starts by grabbing an element. Once you have a reference, you can change its text, its style, listen for clicks. That comes next.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.fiveTools: You already know the maths operators from Scratch. JavaScript spells them plus for add, minus for subtract, star for multiply, slash for divide, and percent for the remainder after dividing. The only one with a surprise is plus — when both sides are text, plus glues the strings instead of adding. This tutorial walks through that surprise and how to fix it.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.askAge: Drag a let block. Name it age. For the value slot, drop in an ask user block from Dialogs that asks How old are you. Just like Scratch's ask and wait — but watch what happens when we try to do maths with what comes back.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.tryToAddOne: Drag a log to console block. Inside, join the words Next year you will be with age plus one. Click Run. Type ten.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears at the bottom-right.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.whyOneOhOne: Big JavaScript surprise. The ask user block hands back TEXT, not a number — even when you type ten. In JavaScript, plus between text glues. So ten and one became the text one-zero-one. In Scratch the answer block just works as a number; JavaScript does not.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.convertWithNumbe: Wrap the ask user block in a Number block. That converts the text to a real number. Click Run. Type ten.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.openConsoleAgain: Open the console. Now the answer is eleven. The Number block fixed it.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.moreMaths: Try one more — add a second log to console: the text In ten years you will be, plus age, plus ten. Click Run and type ten. The console now prints two lines. The first is eleven; the second is twenty. Both worked because age is a real number, not text.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.fiveToolsOneRule: The one rule to remember — wrap every ask user in a Number block before doing maths. Without it, plus glues text. With it, plus adds.
gui.tutorials.jsObjects.labelsNotPositio: Scratch lists are positional — item one, item two, item three. JavaScript has another shape called an OBJECT. Inside, each value has a LABEL — player dot name, player dot score, player dot level. Scratch has nothing like this.
gui.tutorials.jsObjects.makePlayer: Make an object called player with three pairs — name is Riya, score is eighty-five, level is three. Each pair is a label and a value. In JS this writes as curly braces with key colon value pairs inside.
gui.tutorials.jsObjects.readWithDot: Log property name of player. Then property score of player. In real JS, this is player dot name and player dot score. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsObjects.openConsole: Click the Console button next to Run. You see Riya and eighty-five. The property block reads a value by its label.
gui.tutorials.jsObjects.dotVsBracket: Arrays reach in by number. Objects reach in by name. Pick array for ordered same-shape data. Pick object for one labelled thing with named parts.
gui.tutorials.jsObjects.updateProperty: Set property score of player to ninety. Log player. Click Run. The whole object prints with score now ninety. The other properties are unchanged.
gui.tutorials.jsObjects.bracketForm: There is another way to reach a property — square brackets with the name in quotes. player open-bracket name close-bracket. Brackets help when the property name is in a variable.
gui.tutorials.jsObjects.whenToUseWhich: Dot when you know the name in advance. Brackets when the name comes from a variable. Most of the time, dot.
gui.tutorials.jsObjects.bundleRelated: Objects bundle related values together. Every game character, every quiz question, every JSON response from a server — all objects in real JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsReadInput.goodbyePrompt: ask user is a popup — it interrupts the page. Real websites have input fields sitting on the page. JavaScript reads what the user typed using dot value. Today you build your first real form.
gui.tutorials.jsReadInput.pageReady: The page sprite already has an input with id nameInput, a button with id greetBtn and text Greet, and an empty p with id out. You edit the script sprite to wire the form.
gui.tutorials.jsReadInput.clickScript: Click the script sprite. JavaScript blocks in the flyout. Drag the script hat.
gui.tutorials.jsReadInput.grabThree: Three const blocks named nameInput, greetBtn, and out. Each value is find element by id with the matching id. Three references to page elements.
gui.tutorials.jsReadInput.wireClick: Drag a when click on block. Element is greetBtn. Inside, a set text of block. Element out. Text joins Hello comma space, the get value of nameInput, and an exclamation. The get value block is JavaScript's dot value — it reads whatever the user typed.
gui.tutorials.jsReadInput.saveAndOpen: Click Save. Default name is script. Click Save. Then View Files, open index.html. Type a name in the box, click Greet. The paragraph fills with your greeting. No popup — the page itself responds.
gui.tutorials.jsReadInput.firstRealForm: An input, a button, a handler that reads dot value and writes to the page. The shape of every signup form, every search box, every comment field on the web. dot value works on text fields, number fields, dropdowns, and checkboxes.
gui.tutorials.jsStringsControl.makeText: Make a variable called text. Set it to two spaces, then Hello JavaScript, then two more spaces. Those spaces are part of the string. We will clean them up.
gui.tutorials.jsStringsControl.methodsLi: Scratch's strings extension has blocks like trim and replace as separate blocks. JavaScript builds them right onto every string. You call them with a dot — text dot trim. They are called methods.
gui.tutorials.jsStringsControl.trimAndCa: Log text dot trim — removes spaces from both ends. Then text dot toUpperCase — all caps. Then text dot toLowerCase — all small. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsStringsControl.openConso: Click the Console button next to Run. You see the trimmed, the uppercase, and the lowercase versions.
gui.tutorials.jsStringsControl.lengthInc: Log text dot length — same as Scratch's length-of block, counts every character, spaces too. Then text dot includes with Java in quotes — returns true or false. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsStringsControl.replace: Log text dot replace with two arguments — Java and Type. Click Run. replace swaps the first piece for the second and returns a new string.
gui.tutorials.jsStringsControl.split: split cuts a string into an array. Log text dot split with a space as the separator. JavaScript chops wherever it sees a space — turning a sentence into a list of words.
gui.tutorials.jsStringsControl.neverChan: Big rule — every string method returns a NEW string. The original variable does not change. That is why you print or save the result — the method by itself leaves text untouched.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.setupDat: Drag a let block. Name it name, set to Riya. Another let. Name it score, set to eighty-five — no quotes, it is a number. We will print a sentence two ways.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.plusGlue: Drag a log to console block. Inside the message slot, build the sentence by joining several pieces with plus: the literal Hi-comma-space, plus the variable name, plus the literal you-scored-space, plus the variable score, plus the literal space-points. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.openCons: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears at the bottom-right.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.thirdQuo: JavaScript has three kinds of quote — single, double, and backtick. Backticks are special. Inside a backtick string, drop a variable straight in using dollar curly-brace. No joining, no plus signs.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.tryTempl: Add a second log to console block. Inside the message slot, drop a template-string block from the Strings section. Write the sentence as ordinary text but use dollar-open-curly name close-curly where the name belongs, and dollar-open-curly score close-curly where the score belongs. The whole thing reads as one sentence. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.halfTheN: Open the console. Both lines print the same sentence. But the backtick version reads like the actual sentence with small holes for variables. Real JavaScript almost always uses this shape.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.anyExpre: Inside dollar curly-brace you can put any expression — a variable, maths, a function call. The result drops into the string.
gui.tutorials.jsTimers.codeOnClock: Scratch's wait block pauses the script. JavaScript timers DO NOT pause — they schedule code to run later while everything else keeps running. Run once after fires one time after a delay. Repeat every fires over and over. These power every clock, slideshow, and animation on the web.
gui.tutorials.jsTimers.pageReady: The page sprite already has a p with id clock and text Seconds zero. You edit the script sprite to make the clock tick.
gui.tutorials.jsTimers.clickScript: Click the script sprite. JavaScript blocks in the flyout. Drag the script hat.
gui.tutorials.jsTimers.setupState: A const named clock with find element by id and the text clock. A let named seconds with value zero. The counter we bump every tick.
gui.tutorials.jsTimers.startInterval: Drag a repeat every block. Set the number to one thousand. JavaScript timers count in MILLISECONDS — one thousand of them is one second. The body runs once per second. Unlike Scratch's wait, this does not pause anything else.
gui.tutorials.jsTimers.bumpAndShow: Inside repeat every — a change by block. Variable seconds. Amount one. Then a set text of block. Element is clock. Text is a join of Seconds, a space, and seconds. The number ticks on the page.
gui.tutorials.jsTimers.saveId: Right after the repeat every block, add a const named timerId. Value is the last interval id reporter. Every JS timer has an id. Save it now because you need it to cancel later.
gui.tutorials.jsTimers.stopAfterTen: Inside the repeat every body, after the update, add an if block. Condition — seconds triple-equals ten. Inside — a cancel interval block with timerId. Then a set text of on clock with the value Seconds ten done.
gui.tutorials.jsTimers.saveAndWatch: Click Save. Default name is script. Click Save. Then View Files, open index.html. The clock counts up one, two, three. At ten it freezes and prints done.
gui.tutorials.jsTimers.timersEverywhere: Repeat every for ticking clocks and game loops. Run once after for one-shot delays. Save the id to cancel later. Timers run alongside the rest of your code, not instead of it — that is the JS way.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.aName: In Scratch you make variables with the set variable to block. JavaScript does the same thing with the keyword let. Same idea — a name that holds a value — just a different word. In this tutorial you will make a variable called message, put text into it, and then print it to the console.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.makeABox: Drag the let block. Name it message. Value is Hello JavaScript in quotes. The right-hand panel shows the real JS — let message equals Hello JavaScript.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.whatsInside: Same rule as Scratch — drop in the variable name to read it. Quotes mean literal characters. No quotes means use the variable's value.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.printUsingN: Drag a log to console block. Drop the message variable inside, no quotes.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.run: Click Run. The preview is blank — output went to the console.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run to open the Console pane. The pane appears at the bottom-right of the View area with the title Console.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.namingRules: A few rules for JavaScript variable names. No spaces. Cannot start with a digit. Case matters — Message with a capital M and message with a small m are two different variables. The common style is camelCase — start with lowercase and capitalise each new word, like myFirstName or playerScore. Use names that describe what the value is for, not single letters like x or a.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.oneChange: let for changeable values, camelCase names, no spaces. The rest works just like Scratch.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.repeatNoCopy: Scratch has repeat-until — it loops UNTIL a condition becomes true. JavaScript has while — it loops WHILE a condition stays true. Same idea, opposite wording. while count less than ten in JS is repeat until count equals ten in Scratch.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.setupCounter: Drag a set variable block. Set count to one. The counter is what the loop watches.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.whileShape: Drag a while block from Control. The condition is count less-than-or-equal five. Leave the body empty for a moment — we will fill it in next. The Code panel shows while open-paren count less-than-or-equal five close-paren open-brace close-brace.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.bodyPrintStep: Inside the body, log Count colon space joined with count. Then change count by one. Without that change, count stays at one forever.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.runawayLoop: A loop that never ends is called an infinite loop — in Scratch the green flag just keeps running, but in JavaScript the browser tab freezes. Always change something inside the body so the condition can turn false.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.openConsole: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears. Count one through Count five prints, one per line, then the loop stops.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.walkThrough: Step by step. count is one — check true — run body — count becomes two. Up to five. Count becomes six. Check — six is not less-than-or-equal five — false. The loop exits.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.whilePlusCount: while plus a counter that you change inside the body — repeats exactly the right number of times. For loops with known counts, the for loop is even shorter — coming next.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.intro: You know and, or, and not from Scratch's boolean blocks. JavaScript spells them as symbols. Two ampersands for AND. Two pipes for OR. An exclamation mark in front for NOT. Same rules — different shape. In this tutorial you will combine two checks with each operator and see exactly when each one is true.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.setup: Drag a let block. Name it age and set it to fifteen. Drag another let. Name it hasPass and set it to true — no quotes. The words true and false without quotes are JavaScript booleans. With quotes they would be text.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.and: Drag an if block. Inside the condition slot, build age greater-than ten AND hasPass — using the AND operator from the Operators section. Inside the body, log Allowed. For AND, both halves must be true. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.or: Add a second if block. Condition — age greater-than eighteen OR hasPass. Body — log Welcome. Age fifteen is NOT greater than eighteen, but hasPass is true. For OR, only one half needs to be true. Click Run — Welcome appears.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.not: Add a third if. Condition — exclamation-mark hasPass. That is NOT hasPass. The NOT operator flips true to false and false to true. Body — log Buy a pass first. hasPass is currently true, so NOT hasPass is false. Click Run. Buy a pass first does NOT appear.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.flip: Change hasPass from true to false. Click Run again. Now Buy a pass first appears. NOT flipped false into true, so the third if ran. AND and OR also re-evaluated — the Allowed line no longer prints because hasPass is now false.
gui.tutorials.jsAndOrNot.combine: Real checks combine all three — like age greater-than eighteen AND hasPass OR isVip. When you mix AND with OR, use parentheses to group them, just like maths. The next tutorial moves on to loops — letting JavaScript repeat work for you.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.intro: Scratch lists do not have anything like these. JavaScript arrays come with built-in tools that take a small function and run it on every item. The three big ones are forEach, map, and filter. In this tutorial you use each in turn on a list of numbers.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.setup: Drag a let block. Name it numbers. For the value, drop in an array with five items — one, two, three, four, five. These are real numbers, no quotes anywhere.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.threeTools: forEach visits each item. map transforms each into a new one. filter keeps only items that pass a test. Each takes a callback. Each leaves the original array untouched, except for forEach which is purely about doing something with each item.
gui.tutorials.jsArrayMethods.modernJs: Real JavaScript code reads like English with these — take the users, filter the active ones, map their names, log them. Chain them together and a five-line loop turns into a single readable line. The next tutorial covers string methods that work the same way.
gui.tutorials.jsArraysManyThings.intro: You already use lists in Scratch — add things to list, item one of list, length of list. JavaScript calls them ARRAYS. Same idea, different name, slightly different rules. In this tutorial you will make an array of fruits, read items out of it, count them, and add a new one.
gui.tutorials.jsArraysManyThings.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsArraysManyThings.makeArr: Drag a let block. Name it fruits. For the value, drop in an array block from the Arrays section and add three items — apple, banana, mango. Each fruit is text, so each one goes in its own slot. The Code panel shows let fruits equals square-bracket apple comma banana comma mango close-bracket.
gui.tutorials.jsArraysManyThings.printAl: Drop a log to console block and pass it the variable fruits. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsArraysManyThings.openCon: Click the >_ button next to Run. The Console pane appears. The whole array prints — square brackets, items separated by commas, the lot.
gui.tutorials.jsArraysManyThings.length: Add another log line and pass it fruits dot length. Click Run. The console prints three. dot length is JavaScript's version of Scratch's length-of-list — it tells you how many items the array currently holds.
gui.tutorials.jsArraysManyThings.indexZe: A big surprise after Scratch. Scratch lists start at position ONE — item one is the first thing. JavaScript arrays start at ZERO. So the first fruit is fruits at index zero. The second is at index one. The third is at index two. This is just how JavaScript counts — get used to off-by-one.
gui.tutorials.jsArraysManyThings.readByI: Add two more logs: fruits at index zero, then fruits at index two. Click Run. You see apple and mango — the first and third items. The block uses square brackets around the index, exactly how it looks in real JavaScript code.
gui.tutorials.jsArraysManyThings.push: Call fruits dot push with kiwi. That sticks kiwi on the end of the array — same idea as Scratch's add-to-list. Log fruits again and click Run. The array now has four items: apple, banana, mango, kiwi.
gui.tutorials.jsArraysManyThings.recap: The same three list jobs you used in Scratch: read by position, count, add. JavaScript spells them fruits-at-index, dot length, and dot push. Remember the zero-based indexing — that single difference trips up everyone moving from Scratch to JavaScript. In the next tutorial you walk through an array with a for-of loop.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.askName: Drag a let block under the script hat. Name it name. For the value slot, find the ask user block in the Dialogs section and drop it INTO the value slot. Set the ask user prompt to What is your name. This is different from Scratch — the answer comes straight back as the value of the variable, in one step. No separate answer reporter.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.greet: Drag a log to console block under the let. Inside the message slot, build a join: the literal text Hello comma space — that one IS in quotes because it is fixed text — plus the variable name with no quotes because we want its value. The plus sign in JavaScript glues two strings together. The Code panel now shows console.log open-quote Hello-comma-space close-quote plus name close-paren semicolon.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.run: Click Run. A popup appears over the preview asking What is your name. Type your name and click OK.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.greetingShown: There it is — Hello, then your name. JavaScript took the fixed text Hello-comma-space, stuck the value of the name variable onto the end, and printed the whole thing in one go.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.concatenation: Sticking strings together with the plus sign is called concatenation — a long word for end-to-end join. JavaScript looks at the plus sign between two pieces of text, glues them, and gives you back one combined string. You will use concatenation in almost every program from now on.
gui.tutorials.jsAskTheUser.returnsText: One important warning. Whatever the user types comes back as TEXT — even when they type digits. If the kid types five, you get the text five, not the number five. That matters when you try to do maths on it. The Numbers tutorial coming up shows you exactly when this bites and how to fix it.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.askScore: Drag a let block. Name it score. For the value, wrap a Number block around an ask user block that asks Score. We will run three different checks against this one value.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.perfect: Drag an if block. Inside the condition slot, build score triple-equals one hundred — the triple-equals operator is in the Operators section. Inside the body, log Perfect. Click Run and type one hundred.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.pass: Add a second if block. Condition — score greater-than-or-equal fifty. Body — log Pass. Click Run and type seventy. The console shows Pass. Run again with forty-five — nothing prints, the condition is false.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.below: Add a third if. Condition — score less-than fifty. Body — log Fail. Click Run, type forty-five. Now Fail prints. The Operators section has every comparison you need for the rest of these tutorials.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.threeEquals: JavaScript also has double-equals — two equal signs. Double-equals is loose; it says the text five and the number five are equal. Triple-equals is strict; it says they are different because one is text and the other is a number. Use triple-equals everywhere — it catches bugs that double-equals would hide.
gui.tutorials.jsComparisons.oneTruth: Six comparison operators, every one returns true or false. Same shape as the boolean blocks you used inside Scratch's if blocks — just spelled with symbols. The next tutorial combines comparisons with and, or, and not so you can write conditions like age greater-than ten and hasPass.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category in the flyout and drag the script hat block into the empty workspace. Every JavaScript program starts with this hat.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.pi: Find the const block in the Variables section of the flyout. Drag it under the script hat. Name the slot pi and set the value to three point one four. The Code panel on the right shows const pi equals three point one four semicolon. JavaScript will now refuse any line that tries to change pi.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.radius: Radius can change for different circle sizes, so it goes in a let. Drag a let block under the const, name it radius, and set the value to five. The Code panel now shows two lines: const pi equals three point one four; let radius equals five. One locked, one changeable.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.logArea: Drag a log to console block and snap it below the let. Inside the message slot, build the formula pi times radius times radius — that is the area of a circle. Use the Operators category to drop in the multiply operator, and grab the variables pi and radius from the Variables section. No quotes anywhere — we want the computed number, not the literal text.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.run: Click the Run button at the top-right. The View pane stays blank — the answer goes to the console.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.areaShown: The console prints seventy-eight point five. That is the area of a circle of radius five — pi times five times five. JavaScript did the arithmetic, the variables held their values, and the answer is there.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.tryBreak: Imagine you had used let for pi instead of const. Anywhere later in the program, an assignment like pi equals four would silently change the value. The next line that uses pi would compute the wrong area, with no warning. const stops that — it makes pi a promise to JavaScript that nothing will reassign it.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.redError: If you do try to reassign pi later — by typing pi equals four in real code — JavaScript shows a red error in the console: assignment to constant variable. The program stops there. That refusal is the safety const gives you. radius can still change because it is a let, but pi is locked forever.
gui.tutorials.jsConstVsLet.defaultConst: A habit real JavaScript programmers follow: reach for const by default. Only switch to let when you genuinely plan to reassign the variable later. Locking values down by default catches a whole class of bugs early. In the next tutorial we will use prompt to ask the user for input.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.intro: You know repeat ten times from Scratch. JavaScript has the same idea in a shape called the for loop. The for loop bundles three jobs on one line — start the counter, check whether to continue, change the counter. When you know exactly how many rounds you want, this is the shape to use.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.stepExplained: Step is how much i changes after each round. Step one counts up one at a time. Step two skips every other number — one, three, five. Step minus one counts DOWN. Scratch's repeat block could only count up by one; the for loop is more flexible.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.body: Inside the for block, drop a log to console. The message is a template string saying Round space dollar-curly i close-curly. Each time the loop runs, i takes the next value — one, two, three, four, five — and the template pastes it into the sentence.
gui.tutorials.jsForLoop.workhorse: The for loop is the workhorse loop in JavaScript. Counting up, counting down, walking through a list. In the next tutorial you will meet arrays — JavaScript's word for lists — and use a for loop to read through each item.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.intro: In Scratch you walk a list by writing a repeat plus item-i-of-list. JavaScript has a shortcut called the for-of loop. It hands you each item one by one, no index counter needed. When you only need the items themselves, this is the cleanest loop.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.setup: Drag a let block. Name it fruits. For the value, drop in an array with four items — apple, banana, mango, kiwi.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.likeEach: Inside the for-of body, drop a log to console block. The message is a template string saying I like space dollar-curly f close-curly. Click Run.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.naming: We used the letter f for fruit. Pick whatever variable name reads well in your loop body — fruit, item, thing. The shorter and more descriptive, the easier the code reads.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.forOfVsFor: Use for-of when you just need each item in turn. Use the classic for loop when you ALSO need the position number — like printing Item one apple, Item two banana. Same job, slightly different toolkit.
gui.tutorials.jsForOfLoop.cleaner: for-of is the cleanest way to walk an array in JavaScript. The next tutorial shows even shorter shapes — array methods like forEach, map, and filter — that often replace a loop with one line of code.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.decide: You already know if and if-else from Scratch. JavaScript has the same two — just spelled if and else. In this tutorial you write the JavaScript shape: parentheses around the condition, curly braces around the body. Same idea you used in Scratch.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.askAge: Drag a let block. Name it age. For the value, wrap a Number block around an ask user block that asks How old are you. Remember from the last tutorial — the Number block converts the typed text into a real number so we can compare it with another number.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.indented: Look at the Code panel on the right. Lines inside the if body are indented by four spaces. Curly braces — open brace, close brace — mark where the if body starts and ends. Indentation does not change what runs; it just makes the code easier for humans to read.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.runTwelve: Click Run. The prompt appears. Type twelve. Twelve is greater than or equal to ten, so the condition is true.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.runSeven: Click Run again and type seven this time. Seven is less than ten, so the condition is false. The else branch runs. The console now shows You are too young.
gui.tutorials.jsMakeAChoice.twoPaths: Same shape you used in Scratch: exactly one branch runs each time. The JavaScript syntax is parentheses around the condition and curly braces around each branch. In the next tutorial you will learn all the comparison operators so you can write any condition you want.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.addOne: Drag a log to console block. Inside the message slot, build: the literal text Next year you will be, plus the variable age, plus the number one. Use the plus operator from the Operators category. We are TRYING to compute the age plus one. Click Run and type the number ten.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.wrong101: The console says Next year you will be one zero one. That is wrong — you typed ten, the answer should be eleven. What happened? The next step explains.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.whyWrong: Big JavaScript surprise. The ask user block always hands back TEXT, never a number — even when the kid types digits. In JavaScript, when plus has text on one side, it glues. So the text ten and the number one became the text one zero one. JavaScript was not doing maths; it was concatenating.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.convertNumber: Find the Number block in the Conversions section of the flyout. Drag it AROUND the ask user block — the Number block wraps the ask user so the text comes out as a real number. The Code panel now shows let age equals Number open-paren prompt close-paren. Click Run and type ten again.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.correct11: Open the console. The answer is now eleven. The Number block converted the text ten into the real number ten, and JavaScript could do real addition. The fix was tiny — one extra block — but it changed the whole behaviour.
gui.tutorials.jsNumbers.fiveOneRule: Five operators — plus, minus, star, slash, percent — and one rule to remember. Always wrap every ask user inside a Number block before doing arithmetic. Without that wrap, plus will glue text. With the wrap, plus adds. In the next tutorial you will learn template strings — a cleaner way to build text with values inside.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.intro: Gluing strings with the plus sign works, but it gets noisy fast. JavaScript has a cleaner way called template strings. In this tutorial you will build the same sentence two ways — once with plus, once with a template — and see why the template version reads more like the actual sentence.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.setup: Drag a let block. Name it name and set the value to Riya. Drag another let. Name it score and set it to eighty-five — no quotes, because it is a number. We will print a sentence that uses both.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.worksNoi: The sentence appears in the console — Hi-comma Riya, you scored eighty-five points. It works. But look at the Code panel: the line is full of quotes, plus signs, and tiny snippets. It is hard to read at a glance.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.backtick: JavaScript has three kinds of quote — single, double, and backtick. Backticks look like little slanted lines. They are special. Inside a backtick string, you can drop a variable directly using dollar-sign open-curly variable close-curly. JavaScript pastes the variable's value right into the sentence.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.halfNois: Open the console. Both lines print the same sentence. But the backtick version reads like the actual sentence with small slots for the values. Real JavaScript code uses templates all the time once you have more than one variable to embed.
gui.tutorials.jsTemplateStrings.anyExpr: Inside dollar-open-curly you can put any expression — a variable, an arithmetic calculation, a function call. The result drops into the string at that spot. From now on, prefer template strings whenever you have variables to embed — they keep your code readable as your sentences get longer.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category in the flyout on the left and drag the script hat block into the empty workspace. Just like in the last tutorial, this hat marks where your JavaScript program starts. The Code panel on the right shows the beginning of your program.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.dragLet: Find the let block in the Variables section of the flyout. Drag it under the script hat. The block has two slots — a name slot on the left and a value slot on the right. This is JavaScript's way of saying make a new variable with this name and this value.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.fillSlots: Click the name slot and type message. Click the value slot and type Hello JavaScript. You only type the words — no quotes. Now look at the Code panel: it shows let message equals open-quote Hello JavaScript close-quote semicolon. The block wrapped the value in quotes for you because the value is text. If it were a number, no quotes would be added.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.seeCode: The Code panel now shows: let message equals quote Hello JavaScript quote semicolon. That is real JavaScript that any browser can run. The word let creates the variable, the equals sign sets its value, and the semicolon ends the line.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.quotesVsNo: Here is the key rule. If you write a word with quotes around it, JavaScript treats it as literal text. If you write the same word without quotes, JavaScript looks for a variable with that name and uses its value. Same rule as Scratch — drop in the variable name to read it. We will use this now.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.logVariable: Drag a log to console block and snap it below the let block. Now, instead of typing text into the message slot of the log block, drop the message variable in there. You will find the message variable in the Variables section — it appeared after you made it. The log block now shows the variable, not the words.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.clickRun: Click the Run button at the top-right. Like before, the View pane stays blank — the message goes to the console, not to the page.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.consoleResu: There it is — Hello JavaScript — printed by reading the variable. JavaScript took the value out of message and showed it. Change the variable's value once, and every place that reads message picks up the new value automatically. That is the whole point of a variable.
gui.tutorials.jsUseAVariable.recap: You made a variable with let, gave it a value, used the variable to read the value, and watched the console print it. From now on, every JavaScript program you write will use variables this way: let to declare, a name, an equals sign, a value. In the next tutorial you will learn about const — for values that never change.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.intro: Scratch has repeat-until — it loops UNTIL a condition becomes true. JavaScript has while — it loops WHILE a condition stays true. Same idea, opposite phrasing. In this tutorial you will print numbers one through five by letting a while loop count them for you.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.dragHat: Open the JavaScript category and drag the script hat into the empty workspace.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.counter: Drag a let block. Name it count and set it to one. This counter is what the loop will watch — it starts at one and we will count up to five.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.printStep: Inside the while body, drop a log to console block. The message is a template string saying Count colon space dollar-curly count close-curly. Then drop a change-by block from Variables and set it to change count by one. Without that change, count would stay at one forever — the loop would never end.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.runaway: A loop whose condition never becomes false is called an infinite loop. In Scratch the green flag just keeps running, but in JavaScript the browser tab freezes and you have to close it. Whenever you write a while loop, double-check that something inside the body changes the value the condition is checking.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.run: Click the Run button at the top-right.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.walkthrough: Step by step: count starts at one, the check is true, the body runs, count becomes two. The cycle repeats. When count becomes six, the check fails — six is not less-than-or-equal five — so the body does not run and the loop ends. Exactly five rounds, just what we wanted.
gui.tutorials.jsWhileLoop.recap: A while loop plus a counter that changes inside the body — that pattern repeats exactly the right number of times. When you know the count in advance, the next tutorial shows an even shorter shape — the for loop.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.oneSpriteOneFi: In Scratch, each sprite held the code for one CHARACTER. Here, each sprite holds the code for one FILE. The sprite called index dot html holds the HTML — that is your web page. The sprite called script dot js holds the JavaScript — that is your script. Click a sprite, the workspace switches to that file. Two sprites, two files, one project that runs together.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.openSwitcher: At the top-right there is a small icon with two arrows — that is the sprite switcher button. Click it. The sprite switcher dialog opens, showing every sprite in the project. This is how you move between files.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.clickViewTab: Now on the index dot html page sprite, click the View tab on the right. The browser loads your index dot html. Inside it is the script src block pointing at script dot js — that line tells the browser to fetch your script and run it.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.seePage: Look at the View pane. The h1 that said My Page now reads Hello from JavaScript — the browser ran your script and changed the page. Two files, one project, working together.
gui.tutorials.jsHtmlAndJs.backToPage: Your script is saved. But the View on this script sprite would only show a tiny test page — not your real page. To see your real page run, go back to the index dot html page sprite. Open the sprite switcher dialog again and click the index dot html row.
